items.title: Paint by Numbers - A Family-Friendly Art Activity
items.author: Samm Oshen
items.content:
In today’s fast-paced world, finding activities that the whole family can enjoy together is a challenge. Many parents struggle to balance work, household responsibilities, and quality family time. Fortunately, Paint by Numbers has emerged as an engaging, stress-free, and fun artistic activity that everyone in the family can participate in. Whether you're a seasoned artist or a complete beginner, Paint by Numbers offers an enjoyable way to bring creativity into your home.

The best part? There’s an option for , allowing families to personalize their painting experience with meaningful images. Whether it's a cherished family photo, a beloved pet, or a stunning vacation memory, custom kits take the Paint by Numbers experience to a whole new level. Let’s explore why this artistic hobby is a fantastic family-friendly activity and how it benefits people of all ages.

1. Paint by Numbers – A Simple and Fun Activity for Everyone

One of the main reasons Paint by Numbers is an excellent family-friendly activity is its simplicity. Unlike traditional painting, where artistic skills and knowledge of color mixing are required, Paint by Numbers provides a structured approach to creating beautiful artwork. Each canvas is pre-marked with numbers that correspond to specific colors, making it easy for children and adults alike to follow along.

Even young children can participate with minimal guidance, making it a wonderful way for families to bond over a shared activity. Whether you choose a beginner-friendly design or a more intricate painting, the structured format ensures a successful outcome for everyone.

2. Encourages Creativity and Self-Expression

Although Paint by Numbers provides a numbered guide, it still encourages creativity and self-expression. As family members work on their individual sections, they can experiment with blending techniques, shading, and even making slight modifications to personalize their artwork.

With Custom Paint by Numbers, the creativity extends even further. Families can choose to paint portraits of themselves, family vacations, or even recreate a child’s drawing. This customization adds a personal touch to the experience and turns the painting into a cherished keepsake.

3. Strengthens Family Bonds Through Shared Activities

Spending quality time together is crucial for building strong family relationships. Paint by Numbers offers an opportunity for families to gather around the table, chat, and collaborate on a single project. The relaxed and structured nature of the activity makes it easy for family members to engage in meaningful conversations while painting.

For parents, this is a great way to engage with their children in an environment free from digital distractions. Instead of spending time in front of screens, families can work together to complete a painting that they can proudly display in their home.

4. Enhances Focus and Patience in Children

In a world filled with fast-paced entertainment, many children struggle with focus and patience. Paint by Numbers helps children develop these essential skills by encouraging them to concentrate on each numbered section, take their time, and complete the artwork step by step.

This sense of accomplishment when completing a painting boosts a child’s confidence and teaches them the importance of patience and perseverance. Custom Paint by Numbers featuring their favorite cartoon characters, animals, or family photos can make the process even more engaging.

5. Therapeutic and Stress-Relieving for All Ages

Painting has long been known for its therapeutic benefits, and Paint by Numbers is no exception. Engaging in this activity promotes relaxation, reduces stress, and provides a sense of mindfulness. Both adults and children can benefit from the calming effects of painting, making it an ideal way to unwind after a long day.

Parents who lead busy lives often find that Paint by Numbers is a fantastic way to de-stress and spend time with their loved ones. Similarly, children who experience anxiety or restlessness can benefit from the soothing nature of painting.

6. Educational Benefits for Kids

Beyond being an enjoyable activity, Paint by Numbers provides several educational benefits for children. It enhances their motor skills, hand-eye coordination, and attention to detail. Additionally, children learn about color recognition, number matching, and the importance of following instructions.

For younger children, engaging in Paint by Numbers fosters early cognitive development, while older kids can use it as a gateway to exploring more advanced art techniques. Parents can use this activity to introduce basic art concepts and spark their child’s interest in creative pursuits.

7. Affordable and Accessible for Every Family

Unlike some family activities that require expensive materials or memberships, Paint by Numbers is an affordable and accessible option for families. Kits come in various sizes, styles, and difficulty levels, making it easy to find something suitable for every age group.

With the growing popularity of Custom Paint by Numbers, families can even create their personalized kits without breaking the bank. Many online retailers offer affordable options for custom kits, allowing families to turn their favorite photos into beautiful paintings.

8. A Memorable and Decorative Family Keepsake

One of the most rewarding aspects of Paint by Numbers is the ability to create a finished piece of art that holds sentimental value. Families can frame and display their completed paintings as a reminder of the time they spent together.

With Custom Paint by Numbers, the emotional connection becomes even stronger. Imagine painting a portrait of a loved one, a cherished pet, or a scenic view from a memorable vacation. The finished artwork is not just a decoration; it’s a meaningful keepsake that tells a story.

9. Encourages Digital Detox and Screen-Free Time

With the increasing reliance on digital devices, many families struggle to find screen-free activities. Paint by Numbers is an excellent way to encourage a digital detox and engage in hands-on creativity.

Instead of spending hours in front of screens, family members can come together for a relaxing painting session. This activity fosters real-life connections and provides a refreshing break from social media, video games, and television.

10. Perfect for Special Occasions and Family Gatherings

Paint by Numbers can be incorporated into special occasions, such as birthdays, holidays, and family reunions. Hosting a family painting night can turn into a memorable tradition where everyone contributes to a collaborative artwork.

For families looking for a unique holiday gift, Custom Paint by Numbers makes for a heartfelt and personal present. Gifting a loved one a painting of a cherished memory adds a thoughtful touch that can’t be found in store-bought items.

Paint by Numbers is more than just an art activity; it’s an opportunity for families to bond, relax, and create something beautiful together. Whether using a pre-designed kit or opting for Custom Paint by Numbers, this activity provides lasting benefits for people of all ages.

From strengthening relationships and enhancing creativity to promoting relaxation and reducing screen time, is a family-friendly hobby that everyone can enjoy. So, gather your family, pick up a brush, and start painting memories that will last a lifetime!

items.title: Grpc server for learning purposes
items.author: Jhonatan Morais
items.content:
I wrote a GRPC server with basic features to support my aboratories, written articles, and videos it provides a sample of unary call, client streaming, server streaming and bidirectional streaming.

All the samples are "Hello world like" but once you have the project structure is possible to extend it to any purpose.

And I want to share it here. the image src code is available in my github

docker run  grpc  50051:50051 getjv/go-grpc-server

Exploring server calls with grpcURL

gRPCurl is a super handy tool, and the main commands are:

  • grpcurl -plaintext [::]:50051 list – lists the available services.
  • grpcurl -plaintext [::]:50051 describe <ServiceName> – provides more information about the available service.
  • grpcurl -plaintext -d '<JSON-Payload>' [::]:50051 <ServiceName.MethodName> – makes a direct call to a method with a payload.

it's a unary call with single request single response.

grpcurl ::]:50051 helloworld.Greeter.SayHello

it's a server streaming sample with 1 request and 10 messages from server

grpcurl  localhost:50051 helloworld.Greeter/StreamGreetings

it's a client streaming sample with 10 client requests and 1 message from server Here the steps are a bit different:

grpcurl  @ localhost:50051 helloworld.Greeter/EchoGreetings
  • the terminal will wait the input give the objects bellow and press : 
: : :
  • Now press to notify all was sent. Server will send you the final confirmation.

Bi-directional streaming: this is the most advanced sample an open channel to both sides keeping interacting to test execute:

      grpcurl  @ localhost:50051 helloworld.Greeter/OpenGreetings
  • the terminal will wait the input give the objects bellow and press :
  • After each Server will send you a confirmation.
  • Now press to notify you are done.

items.title: DevSecOps: Integrating Security into Your CI/CD Pipeline
items.author: Yash Sonawane
items.content:
Introduction: Why Security Matters in DevOps

Imagine launching a new application with a fast-paced CI/CD pipeline, only to realize later that it has critical security vulnerabilities. This nightmare scenario has happened to major companies, leading to data breaches, compliance violations, and reputational damage.

Traditional DevOps focuses on speed and agility, but security is often an afterthought. This is where comes in—seamlessly integrating security into the DevOps workflow. By embedding security at every stage of the CI/CD pipeline, organizations can ensure that vulnerabilities are detected and mitigated early, without slowing down development.

🚀 Why is DevSecOps a Game-Changer?

  • Identifies vulnerabilities before production.
  • Ensures regulatory adherence (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).
  • Fixing bugs early saves time and money.
  • Automated security testing at every stage.

Step-by-Step Guide: Integrating Security into CI/CD

1️⃣ Shift Left Security: Start Early

Shifting security left means integrating it as early as possible in the development lifecycle. This includes:

  • Static Application Security Testing (SAST): Analyzing code for vulnerabilities.
  • Software Composition Analysis (SCA): Checking dependencies for known vulnerabilities.

🔹 Example: Running SAST with SonarQube 


curl  sonar-scanner.zip https://binaries.sonarsource.com/Distribution/sonar-scanner-cli/sonar-scanner-cli-4.6.2.2472-linux.zip
unzip sonar-scanner.zip  /opt/

sonar-scanner .projectKeymy-project .sources./src .host.urlhttp://localhost:9000

💡 Troubleshooting: If SonarQube fails to start, ensure Java 11+ is installed.

2️⃣ Automated Security Testing in CI/CD

Security tools should be part of your CI/CD pipeline to ensure continuous scanning.

🔹 Example: Adding Security Tests in GitHub Actions

💡 Alternative: Use tools like OWASP ZAP for dynamic security testing.

Never hardcode secrets in your repositories. Use tools like or .

🔹 Example: Storing and Retrieving Secrets from HashiCorp Vault 


vault kv put secret/db_password mysecurepassword


vault kv get secret/db_password

💡 Tip: Use environment variables to inject secrets at runtime.

4️⃣ Container Security: Scan Docker Images

Before deploying a container, ensure it is free from vulnerabilities.

🔹 Example: Scanning Docker Images with Clair 

clairctl analyze my-docker-image:latest

💡 * for container scanning.*

Real-World Use Cases & Comparisons

✅ Case Study: Netflix’s Approach to DevSecOps

Netflix integrates security automation into its CI/CD pipeline using tools like and . By automating security testing, Netflix reduces security risks without slowing down innovation.

🔄 DevSecOps vs Traditional DevOps

  • ❓ Which security tool is best for scanning vulnerabilities? (Trivy, SonarQube, OWASP ZAP)

SEO Optimization Checklist

✅ DevSecOps ✅ Learn how to integrate security into your CI/CD pipeline using DevSecOps best practices. ✅ [Included for easy navigation] ✅ Beginner-friendly yet informative for advanced users.

Engaging Storytelling & Call-to-Action

When I first implemented DevSecOps in my CI/CD pipeline, I underestimated how many security issues were lurking in my codebase. After integrating and , I caught vulnerabilities that could have led to major security risks. This experience taught me that security should never be an afterthought.

Have you faced security challenges in your CI/CD pipeline? Comment below and share your experiences!

🚀 Want more DevOps insights? for weekly updates!

📚 Resources & Further Reading

By following these steps, you can build a secure, efficient, and resilient CI/CD pipeline. DevSecOps isn't just about security—it’s about enabling faster, safer, and more reliable software delivery. Start integrating security today! 🔒🚀

items.title: My Journey with Deepseek R1 on NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super using Docker and Ollama
items.author: Ajeet Singh Raina
items.content:
DeepSeek R1 is an innovative large language model developed by DeepSeek AI, designed specifically for efficient deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Unlike many larger models that require substantial computational resources, DeepSeek R1 delivers impressive language capabilities in a compact form factor, making it ideal for hardware like the Jetson Orin series.

The 1.5B parameter version represents an excellent balance between performance and efficiency. It features an optimized architecture that maintains strong reasoning capabilities, code generation, and contextual understanding while significantly reducing memory and computational requirements. This smaller footprint allows it to run directly on edge devices without compromising too much on quality. DeepSeek R1 has been quantized using various techniques (like the q4f16_ft version we'll be using) to further enhance its efficiency on GPU-accelerated platforms. These optimizations make it possible to deploy advanced AI capabilities in scenarios where cloud connectivity might be limited or where privacy and latency concerns necessitate local processing.

In this post, I'll share my journey setting up various AI tools on the Jetson Orin Nano Super, from basic system verification to running sophisticated language models. I've captured everything in my notion page and am now sharing it with you.

  • Jumper wire(for initial setup)
  • A Linux Laptop with SDK Manager

You will need put the board into a recovery mode by using a jumper pin. On the Jetson Orin Nano, look for the FC REC pin and GND pin on the GPIO header.The FC REC pin is typically marked on the board (check the Jetson Orin Nano Developer Kit pinout diagram). Place the jumper across the FC REC and GND pins.This forces the board into Force Recovery Mode (RCM mode) when powered on. Power On the Board. Connect the power supply while keeping the jumper in place. Remove the Jumper (Optional, if required). Connect the Jetson Orin Nano to a Linux PC via USB Type-C cable.

Start SDK Manager software on the Linux system and flash this board with JetPack 6.2. Choose NVMe SSD instead of SD card. Once the board has powered on, you can remove the jumper if necessary. Verify Recovery Mode on Host PC.

First, I checked if the OS was properly installed on the NVMe storage:

df -h /
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/nvme0n1p1  456G   11G  422G   3% /

This confirmed that the system was running from the fast NVMe storage with plenty of space available (456GB total with 422GB free).

The first AI tool I installed was Ollama, which provides a convenient way to run large language models locally:

curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh

The installation went smoothly, with the script automatically configuring Ollama for the NVIDIA Jetpack environment:

Installing ollama to /usr/local
Downloading Linux arm64 bundle
######################################################################## 100.0%
>>> Downloading JetPack 6 components
######################################################################## 100.0%
>>> Creating ollama user...
>>> Adding ollama user to render group...
>>> Adding ollama user to video group...
>>> Adding current user to ollama group...
>>> Creating ollama systemd service...
>>> Enabling and starting ollama service...
Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/default.target.wants/ollama.service → /etc/systemd/system/ollama.service.
>>> NVIDIA JetPack ready.
>>> The Ollama API is now available at 127.0.0.1:11434.
>>> Install complete. Run "ollama" from the command line.

Setting Up OpenWebUI Using Docker

To provide a user-friendly interface for interacting with Ollama, I deployed OpenWebUI using Docker:

sudo docker run -d --network=host \
  -v ${HOME}/open-webui:/app/backend/data \
  -e OLLAMA_BASE_URL=http://127.0.0.1:11434 \
  --name open-webui \
  --restart always \
  ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main

I confirmed the container was running successfully:

sudo docker ps
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE                                COMMAND           CREATED          STATUS                             PORTS     NAMES
c4de9820647e   ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main   "bash start.sh"   11 seconds ago   Up 10 seconds (health: starting)             open-webui

Running DeepSeek R1 with Ollama

Next, I tested Ollama with a compact but capable language model, DeepSeek R1:

ollama run deepseek-r1:1.5b --verbose
The model download proceeded smoothly:
Copypulling manifest
pulling aabd4debf0c8... 100% ▕████████████████▏ 1.1 GB
pulling 369ca498f347... 100% ▕████████████████▏  387 B
pulling 6e4c38e1172f...   0% ▕                ▏    0 B
pulling f4d24e9138dd...   0% ▕                ▏    0 B
pulling a85fe2a2e58e... 100% ▕████████████████▏  487 B
verifying sha256 digest
writing manifest
success

Testing the Model with a Prompt

I tested the model with a practical prompt relevant to the Jetson platform:

Write a short Python script to capture and process images using OpenCV on Jetson

The model generated a detailed Python script with explanations of key components for image capture and processing on Jetson hardware. The response included proper imports, device number retrieval, camera configuration, image capture, and processing steps.

Analyzing Model Performance

After the completion of the response, Ollama provided performance metrics:

total duration:       30.039637931s
load duration:        60.895507ms
prompt eval count:    19 token(s)
prompt eval duration: 282ms
prompt eval rate:     67.38 tokens/s
eval count:           920 token(s)
eval duration:        29.693s
eval rate:            30.98 tokens/s
  • Total processing time: 30.04 seconds
  • Model loading time: 60.9 milliseconds (very fast)
  • Prompt evaluation: 19 tokens processed at 67.38 tokens/second
  • Response generation: 920 tokens generated at 30.98 tokens/second

The performance is quite impressive for an edge device, with the model loading quickly and generating nearly 31 tokens per second.

Using Docker Compose for Advanced Deployment

For a more sophisticated setup, I used the following Docker Compose configuration to manage multiple services. I downloaded it from this link

I faced the following error message:

sudo docker compose up -d
[sudo] password for ajeetraina:
[+] Running 1/1
 ✔ llm-server Pulled                                                                                    2.8s
[+] Running 0/1
 ⠸ Container llm_server  Starting                                                                       0.3s
Error response from daemon: failed to create task for container: failed to create shim task: OCI runtime create failed: runc create failed: unable to start container process: error during container init: error running prestart hook #0: exit status 1, stdout: , stderr: Auto-detected mode as 'csv'
invoking the NVIDIA Container Runtime Hook directly (e.g. specifying the docker --gpus flag) is not supported. Please use the NVIDIA Container Runtime (e.g. specify the --runtime=nvidia flag) instead.: unknown

This error indicated that the Jetson's Docker configuration was having issues with the newer deploy resources syntax for GPU access. The error specifically suggests using the older runtime: nvidia approach instead of the newer GPU device specification

Modified Docker Compose Configuration

I adjusted my approach to use the older runtime: nvidia syntax instead:

 services:
  llm-server:
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    container_name: llm_server
    network_mode: host
    runtime: nvidia
    ports:
      - 9000:9000
    environment:
      - NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all
      - DOCKER_PULL=always
      - HF_HUB_CACHE=/root/.cache/huggingface

Important changes and optimizations include:

  • Using runtime: nvidia instead of the newer deploy resources syntax
  • Using host networking mode for optimal performance
  • Volume mounts for caching model weights
  • Health checks to ensure service availability
  • Added parameter to reduce memory usage

Deploying with Docker Compose

I launched the compose setup with:

sudo docker compose up -d

The output confirmed successful deployment:

[+] Running 1/1
 ✔ llm-server Pulled                                                                                                                                         2.8s
[+] Running 1/1
 ✔ Container llm_server  Started                                                                                                                             0.6s

Checking the container status: 

sudo docker compose ps
NAME         IMAGE                 COMMAND                  SERVICE      CREATED         STATUS                            PORTS
llm_server   dustynv/mlc:r36.4.0   "sudonim serve --mod…"   llm-server   6 seconds ago   Up 5 seconds (health: starting)

Memory Challenges on the Jetson Orin

When running the LLM server, I initially encountered memory limitations with the Jetson Orin Nano. The following error appeared in the logs:

[bt] (2) /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/mlc_llm/libmlc_llm_module.so(+0x303cfc) [0xffff64853cfc]
[bt] (1) /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/tvm/libtvm.so(tvm::runtime::detail::LogFatal::Entry::Finalize()+0x68) [0xffff898dc7f8]
[bt] (0) /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/tvm/libtvm.so(tvm::runtime::Backtrace[abi:cxx11]()+0x30) [0xffff8b7ebfc0]
File "/opt/mlc-llm/cpp/serve/threaded_engine.cc", line 287
TVMError: Check failed: (output_res.IsOk()) is false: Insufficient GPU memory error: The available single GPU memory is 6476.857 MB, which is less than the sum of model weight size (876.640 MB) and temporary buffer size (10771.183 MB).
1. You can set a larger "gpu_memory_utilization" value.
2. If the model weight size is too large, please enable tensor parallelism by passing `--tensor-parallel-shards $NGPU` to `mlc_llm gen_config` or use quantization.
3. If the temporary buffer size is too large, please use a smaller `--prefill-chunk-size` in `mlc_llm gen_config`.

This error message revealed the core challenge:

  • Available GPU memory: ~6.5GB
  • Model weight size: ~0.9GB
  • Temporary buffer needed: ~10.8GB
  • Total required: ~11.7GB (exceeding available memory)

The error suggested three potential solutions:

  • Increase GPU memory utilization (not applicable as we were already pushing the limits)
  • Enable tensor parallelism (not applicable for single-GPU setups like the Jetson)
  • Reduce the prefill chunk size

I opted for the third solution by adding the parameter to the command, which reduced the temporary buffer size enough to fit within the available memory. This parameter essentially limits how much context the model processes at once, trading off some efficiency for reduced memory usage.

Let's fix this by following option #3 in the error message. You need to reduce the temporary buffer size by using a smaller prefill chunk size. Here's how to modify your Docker Compose file:

command: sudonim serve --model
  dusty-nv/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B-q4f16_ft-MLC --quantization
  q4f16_ft --max-batch-size 1 --chat-template deepseek_r1_qwen --host
  0.0.0.0 --port 9000 --prefill-chunk 1024

Re-run the Compose Services:

sudo docker compose logs -f
llm_server  |
llm_server  | Running auto update command in /opt/sudonim
llm_server  |   git pull && pip3 install --upgrade-strategy only-if-needed -e .
llm_server  | remote: Enumerating objects: 42, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (40/40), done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (7/7), done.
llm_server  | remote: Total 24 (delta 19), reused 22 (delta 17), pack-reused 0 (from 0)
Unpacking objects: 100% (24/24), 2.46 KiB | 209.00 KiB/s, done.
llm_server  | From https://github.com/dusty-nv/sudonim
llm_server  |    7f568e6..c8d2f98  main       -> origin/main
llm_server  | Updating 7f568e6..c8d2f98
llm_server  | Fast-forward
llm_server  |  pyproject.toml            |  2 +-
llm_server  |  sudonim/runners/export.py | 28 +++++++++++++++++++++++++---
llm_server  |  sudonim/runtimes/mlc.py   |  2 +-
llm_server  |  sudonim/utils/docker.py   | 20 +++++++++++++++++---
llm_server  |  4 files changed, 44 insertions(+), 8 deletions(-)
llm_server  | Looking in indexes: https://pypi.jetson-ai-lab.dev/jp6/cu126
llm_server  | Obtaining file:///opt/sudonim

So far, everything looks normal - it's pulling the latest code from the repository and updating the package.

face_hub->sudonim==0.1.7) (6.0.2)
llm_server  | Requirement already satisfied: tqdm>=4.42.1 in /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages (from huggingface_hub->sudonim==0.1.7) (4.67.1)
llm_server  | Requirement already satisfied: typing-extensions>=3.7.4.3 in /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages (from huggingface_hub->sudonim==0.1.7) (4.12.2)
llm_server  | Requirement already satisfied: charset-normalizer<4,>=2 in /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages (from requests>=2.26.0->docker->sudonim==0.1.7) (3.4.1)
llm_server  | Requirement already satisfied: idna<4,>=2.5 in /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages (from requests>=2.26.0->docker->sudonim==0.1.7) (3.10)
llm_server  | Requirement already satisfied: certifi>=2017.4.17 in /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages (from requests>=2.26.0->docker->sudonim==0.1.7) (2024.12.14)
llm_server  | Building wheels for collected packages: sudonim
llm_server  |   Building editable for sudonim (pyproject.toml) ... done
llm_server  |   Created wheel for sudonim: filename=sudonim-0.1.7-0.editable-py3-none-any.whl size=4464 sha256=dddf389242d52ea17d83e1fb4fa2a438b10fb22a7c913c4df4695b069c8764cb
llm_server  |   Stored in directory: /tmp/pip-ephem-wheel-cache-_jbwjsiw/wheels/4a/b4/2c/516abc57fcca2f71adffc99520e49ddd6c2ee2be8fd0dd86b6

llm_server  | [12:16:50] sudonim | sudonim version 0.1.7

llm_server  | │ CUDA_VERSION   12.6      │ GPU 0                       │ CACHE_ROOT      /root/.cache │
llm_server  | │ NVIDIA_DRIVER  540.4.0   │  ├ name      Orin Nano 8GB  │ HAS_MLC         True         │

Fetching 38 files: 100% 38/38 [00:00<00:00, 828.06it/s]

Here's the final working Compose file:

services:
  llm-server:
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    container_name: llm_server
    network_mode: host
    runtime: nvidia
    environment:
      - NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all
      - DOCKER_PULL=always
      - HF_HUB_CACHE=/root/.cache/huggingface
    pull_policy: always
    volumes:
      - /mnt/nvme/cache:/root/.cache
    image: dustynv/mlc:r36.4.0
    command: sudonim serve --model
      dusty-nv/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B-q4f16_ft-MLC --quantization
      q4f16_ft --max-batch-size 1 --chat-template deepseek_r1_qwen --host
      0.0.0.0 --port 9000 --prefill-chunk 1024
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://0.0.0.0:9000/v1/models"]
      interval: 20s
      timeout: 60s
      retries: 45
      start_period: 15s

  perf-bench:
    profiles:
      - perf-bench
    depends_on:
      llm-server:
        condition: service_healthy
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    network_mode: host
    volumes:
      - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
      - /mnt/nvme/cache:/root/.cache
    runtime: nvidia
    environment:
      - NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=all
      - DOCKER_PULL=always
      - HF_HUB_CACHE=/root/.cache/huggingface
    pull_policy: always
    image: dustynv/mlc:r36.4.0
    command: sudonim bench stop --model
      dusty-nv/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B-q4f16_ft-MLC --quantization
      q4f16_ft --max-batch-size 1 --chat-template deepseek_r1_qwen --host
      0.0.0.0 --port 9000 --prefill-chunk 1024

  open-webui:
    profiles:
      - open-webui
    depends_on:
      llm-server:
        condition: service_healthy
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    container_name: open-webui
    network_mode: host
    environment:
      - ENABLE_OPENAI_API=True
      - ENABLE_OLLAMA_API=False
      - OPENAI_API_BASE_URL=http://0.0.0.0:9000/v1
      - OPENAI_API_KEY=foo
      - DOCKER_PULL=always
      - HF_HUB_CACHE=/root/.cache/huggingface
    volumes:
      - /mnt/nvme/cache/open-webui:/app/backend/data
      - /mnt/nvme/cache:/root/.cache
    pull_policy: always
    image: ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main

You're still encountering the same memory error. The --prefill-chunk 1024 parameter isn't reducing the memory requirements enough. Let's try an even smaller chunk size and look at other options:

First, modify your compose file to use an even smaller prefill chunk size:

command: sudonim serve --model
  dusty-nv/DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B-q4f16_ft-MLC --quantization
  q4f16_ft --max-batch-size 1 --chat-template deepseek_r1_qwen --host
  0.0.0.0 --port 9000 --prefill-chunk 512

After making these changes:

sudo docker compose down
sudo docker compose up -d

The NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super proves to be an impressive platform for edge AI deployment. Despite some memory constraints that required careful optimization, I was able to successfully run sophisticated language models directly on the device. The combination of Docker for containerization, Ollama for model management, and OpenWebUI for user interaction creates a powerful, self-contained AI development environment. This setup demonstrates the potential for deploying AI capabilities in scenarios where cloud connectivity might be limited or privacy concerns necessitate local processing.

For developers interested in edge AI, the Jetson Orin Nano Super represents an excellent balance of performance, power efficiency, and affordability. Its ability to run models like DeepSeek R1 with reasonable performance opens up exciting possibilities for creative applications in robotics, computer vision, natural language processing, and more.

items.title: CoT Reasoning Models – Which One Reigns Supreme in 2025?
items.author: Developer Harsh
items.content:
A comprehensive analysis for o3-Mini-High vs Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking vs Grok 3 Think vs Deep Seek R1 on multiple reasoning, math, coding, and writing questions. Which one is bang for your buck in 2025?

It’s been a fascinating few months in the AI landscape with the emergence of a new breed of models – the “COT Reasoning” LLMs. These models don’t just generate text; they reason through problems step by step, often producing dramatically better results on complex tasks. But which one deserves your attention (and potentially your subscription dollars)?

I’ve spent the last few weeks testing these models across reasoning, mathematics, coding, and creative writing tasks. Let’s examine how these thinking titans compare.

But first, what is CoT? Let’s uncover that!

What is COT Based on Thinking

Chain of Thoughts (CoT) is a way of solving problems by breaking down problems into manageable steps, considering multiple approaches, and even correcting their errors – much like humans. This helps in getting more accurate and clear results.

Let’s understand through an example:

Say you’re calculating the total cost of a meal at a restaurant. (Assume 18% GST) included

    Now, instead of guessing the total cost, the CoT Reasoning model follows a logical chain of thoughts:

    1. First, add the burger and pizza: ₹100 + ₹200 = ₹300
    2. Then, add the drink: ₹300 + ₹50 = ₹350
    3. If there’s an 18% tax, calculate: 18% of ₹350 = ₹63
    4. Add the tax to the total: ₹350 + ₹63 = ₹413
    5. So, the final bill is ₹413.

    This step-by-step thinking process is called Chain of Thoughts—it helps avoid mistakes and makes it easier to understand and solve complex problems!

    For deep understanding, check out the paper: Demystifying Long Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs

    But as with all things, each model implements COT Reasoning Ability differently. Let’s examine that.

    This step-by-step thinking process is called Chain of Thoughts—it helps avoid mistakes and makes it easier to understand and solve complex problems!

    But as with all things, each model implements COT Reasoning Ability differently. Let’s examine that.

    COT Reasoning LLM’s special is their ability to allocate additional computational resources to tackle complex problems – what the AI community calls “test-time compute.”

    This means they can spend anywhere from a few seconds to several minutes reasoning through a problem, often considering multiple approaches before arriving at an answer.

    Here is a simplistic breakdown of Each of today’s contender models and how they approach “thinking” differently:

    • : The smaller but mighty cousin of o1, offering adjustable “effort” levels.
    • : An open-source reasoning powerhouse that rivals proprietary models.
    • : xAI’s reasoning model with dedicated “Think Mode”.
    • Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet (Extended Thinking): Anthropic’s model with configurable “thinking budget”.

    Ok don’t take my words for it, see benchmark score yourself!

    A. Popular Benchmark Scores

    If you are still unsure, here is a simple breakdown of the results.

    • Grok-3 leads in AIME 2024 with 93.0%, the highest among all models.
    • Claude 3.7 Sonnet is best in GPQA with 84.8%, slightly ahead of Grok-3 (84.6%).
    • DeepSeek-R1 tops MATH-500 with 97.3%, slightly better than o3-mini (96.2%).
    • Grok-3 performs best in Live Code Bench with 79.0%, while -DeepSeek-R1 follows at 65.9%.
    • o3-mini is strong in AIME 2024 (87.3%) and GPQA (79.7%) but lacks scores in some benchmarks.
    • DeepSeek-R1 has mixed performance—strong in MATH-500 but lower in GPQA (71.5%).
    • Claude 3.7 Sonnet performs well overall, ranking high in GPQA and AIME 2024.

    Now let’s look at the ARC – AGI Score

    Claude 3.7 Sonnet (Extended Thinking)

    Note: As of this date, the specific ARC AGI scores for Grok 3’s “Think Mode” have not been publicly disclosed.

    Raw Capability Showdown - Which One Prevails?

    Though benchmarks are a good way to evaluate a model’s performance, they’re not the best when it comes to rigorous testing. Raw capability matters as well.

    So, I have prepared a bunch of questions related to reasoning, math, coding, and writing. Let’s see how all our contenders perform on them!

    Mathematics tasks are where thinking models truly shine compared to their traditional counterparts due to their reasoning capabilities.

    Let’s examine how these models tackle a classic geometry problem

    Q1. Find the possible 4th vertex of a parallelogram

    A straightforward and intuitive math question from Gilbert Strang’s Linear Algebra class.

    If three corners of a parallelogram are (1,1), (4,2), and (1,3), what are all the possible fourth corners?

    It would be interesting to see how different model think about it.

    OpenAI O3-Mini-High: Pass

    O3-Mini-High got all the answers correct without any nudging, showing its amazing COT Reasoning skills.

    What’s brilliant is it first deduced the reasoning and then went for the math part; partly human I would say!

    Impressive not only Claude 3.7 Sonnet Thinking solved the problem, but it also verified it, which was not done by Open AI O3-Mini-High.

    Grok3 Passed the test, but again, there was no verification step before finalizing the answer.

    As usual, Deep Seek didn’t have any challenges to solve the problem. However, it didn’t show any verification part like Claude.

    Q2. Compute the GCD of an infinite series

    One of my favourite questions is to check the mathematical ability of a model.

    Compute the GCD of this series set {n^99(n^60-1): n>1}.

    Claude 3.7 Sonnet Thinking

    Similar to Open AI O3, Claude 3.7 Sonnet solve it, but at a reasoning time of 361 Seconds. However, it missed the verification part, same as O3.

    Grok3 gave the answer in the format question was asked. However, after a slight nudge, it delivered the final answer with a verification step included, like if it knows I am writing the blog and stating it’s not producing verification steps.

    Despite being 767 second, model still struggles to find the answer which all other models found very fast. This may be due to over-reasoning. I will update this part if it is able to find the answer.

    • If you are into research and academia, go with Grok3 and Calude Sonnet 3.7. Both of them performed answer validation, which is crucial in the domain.
    • If you are more worried about token count and inference speed and don’t care about answer validation – mostly creative task, go with OpenAI-Mini-High and Claude Sonnet 3.7
    • I would never recommend Deep Seek R1 for math-related tasks, as over-reasoning causes it to hallucinate frequently.

    Next, off to the reasoning test!

    Advanced Reasoning is fundamental to COT Reasoning Models, so let’s see how all contenders perform on some of the Easy Problems That LLMs Get Wrong.

    Q1. Will You Switch Door or Not?

    This problem is a simple version of the classic Monty Hall problem, which is used to explain concepts of Bayes’ Theorem to high school students, let’s see which model get the actual output

    Suppose you’re on a game show, and you’re given the choice of three doors: Behind one door is a gold bar; behind the others, rotten vegetables. You pick a door, say No. 1, and the host asks you, “Do you want to pick door No. 2 instead?”

    Is it to your advantage to switch your choice?

    Switching is not an advantage. It makes no difference if I switch because no additional information has been provided since the initial choice.

    Let’s see which model find it first!

    Grok 3 seemed to reason and fact-check a lot, finally deducing the right answer and even the actual reasoning task.

    Impressive only Claude Sonnet 3.7 & Grok 3 were the only model to figure out the right answer. However, Claude sonnet 3.7 gave answer the least verbosely and fastest. In Contrast, O3 and Deep Seek R1 fails the test,

    Q2. The River Crossing Puzzle

    Let’s see how different model performs on this classic flash game puzzle variation I used to play a lot in my old days. It’s a good reasoning puzzle variation too.

    A farmer wants to cross a river and take with him a wolf, a goat and a cabbage. He has a boat with three secure separate compartments. If the wolf and the goat are alone on one shore, the wolf will eat the goat. If the goat and the cabbage are alone on the shore, the goat will eat the cabbage. How can the farmer efficiently bring the wolf, the goat and the cabbage across the river without anything being eaten?

    Place the wolf, goat, and cabbage in separate secure compartments in the boat and row across the river. This will prevent any of them from being eaten by the others.

    Grok 3 captures the fact that there are three compartments and uses the fact to solve the problem correctly.

    As can be seen clearly from the thinking snippet, Deep Seek figured out the problem was a trick problem which shows its reasoning and thought capacity

    This is a straightforward and easy question, but most LLMs get these wrong, so let’s check if contender models can crack it or not

    I get out on the top floor (third floor) at street level. How many stories is the building above the ground?

    One story above the ground. – Basically, 2 are basements

    Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking

    Claude concluded that: One story above the ground. – Basically 2 floors are basements (refer para2 in COT Traces) but due to excessive thinking model nudged towards saying 0, which alternatively can be inferred as 1.

    As can be seen in the right, R1 figured out the building is one Story but was not confident enough to answer it as one.

    This shows these models have learnt some behavior that was not part of the training process.

    Grok 3 Thinking fails the test, as demonstrated by its thinking process.

    For advanced reasoning-related tasks, Deep Seek r1 and Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking can be used without a doubt. For lower-end reasoning tasks, Grok 3 & Open AI O3 Mini High can be considered to some extent.

    As a developer, it’s common for me to use LLMs to write clean, efficient, and easy-to-understand code while following all the coding principles. Let’s test how all these LLMs stack up.

    Personally, I am pretty excited about Sonnet 3.7 Thinking.

    I have taken this hard problem from Leet code. Let’s see how all models perform.

    You are given a 0-indexed integer array nums representing the strength of some heroes. The power of a group of heroes is defined as follows:

    Let i0, i1, … ,ik be the indices of the heroes in a group. Then, the power of this group is max(nums[i0], nums[i1], ... ,nums[ik])2 * min(nums[i0], nums[i1], ... ,nums[ik]). Return the sum of the power of all non-empty groups of heroes possible. Since the sum could be very large, return it modulo 109 + 7.

    O3-Mini-High – Pass (5.2 seconds)
    Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking: Pass (179 Seconds)

    Deep Seek R1 – Fails (3/3)

    • OpenAI O3 Mini High is the fastest model that generated the most optimized solution. However, it used typing module – an external library, which might be bad for some scenarios.
    • Next to it is Claude 3.7 Sonnet & Grok3, they generated a good solution, but not the best one compared. I would prefer Grok3 because of its answer validation method.
    • Sadly, Deep Seek R1 was stuck in loop, like many times from earlier and didn’t even generate a response. Not recommend using at all.

    Q2. Check If Digits Are Equal in String After Operations II

    This one I found out on Leet Code has an acceptance rate of 8.2%, so I thought of giving it a try with all our llms; let’s see how they perform on it.

    Prompt: You are given a string s consisting of digits. Perform the following operation repeatedly until the string has precisely two digits:

For each pair of consecutive digits in s, starting from the first digit, calculate a new digit as the sum of the two digits modulo 10. Replace s with the sequence of newly calculated digits, maintaining the order in which they are computed. Return true if the final two digits in s are the same; otherwise, return false.

Example 1:

Input: s = "3902"

Output: true

Explanation:

Initially, s = "3902" First operation: (s[0] + s[1]) % 10 = (3 + 9) % 10 = 2 (s[1] + s[2]) % 10 = (9 + 0) % 10 = 9 (s[2] + s[3]) % 10 = (0 + 2) % 10 = 2 s becomes "292" Second operation: (s[0] + s[1]) % 10 = (2 + 9) % 10 = 1 (s[1] + s[2]) % 10 = (9 + 2) % 10 = 1 s becomes "11" Since the digits in "11" are the same, the output is true. Example 2:

Input: s = "34789"

Output: false

Explanation:

Initially, s = "34789". After the first operation, s = "7157". After the second operation, s = "862". After the third operation, s = "48". The output is false since '4' != '8'.

Code:

class Solution: def hasSameDigits(self, s: str) -> bool:

Provide the output in the right formatted way
    O1-Mini-High: Fails (15 Seconds)

    Sadly, O3 generated the right code, but not the optimal one, resulting in Time Limit Exceed Error, when I tried to submit.

    Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking (45 Seconds)

    Sadly Claude Sonnet 3.7 Also fails to the question.

    Grok 3 Think (221 Seconds)

    Despite thinking for 221 Seconds, Grok 3 Aced it and made me the top 8.2% coder who solved this problem within the given time frame for evaluation.

    If you look at the code and the Reasoning, you will find the code was reasoned and solved using the first principal reasoning, which is remarkable.

    As expected Deep Seek R1 failed again. Now at this point I am pretty confident, I am not going to use Deep Seek R1 for my coding related task at least.

    We have a clear winner here, Grok 3 Think.

    Grok 3 not only aced all the problems but was the only model who was able to solve the last model by providing most optimized code. I will definitely go for it.

    If I am not concerned about hard coding problems, my second pick will be Open AI O3, super-fast and answered 2/3 question with fastest response time. However, I will include the prompt to format the output right way.

    Last, I would pick up Calude Sonnet 3.7 – Cause it less fast and more token consuming. I thought I would beat Grok 3, but it failed my expectations at least.

    From this point onwards, the last question becomes my personal benchmark to judge LLM’s Coding Capabilities.

    Finally, let’s move on to the writing tests!

    For this section, instead of 3, I will be testing on just 1 question as, at this point, all LLM’s performances are almost similar, and it just becomes a matter of preference.

    However, to present a challenge I am going to test the writing capability on a proven question, which many LLM’s gets wrong.

    Q1. Generate Meaningful Modified Pangrams

    Panagram is a sentence that uses every letter of the alphabet at least once. Pangrams are often used for typing practice, testing fonts, or for fun linguistic exercises.

    For this question there is a slight modification that it is not necessary to use all the letters of the alphabet. Let’s see which model captures this.

    Write a grammatically correct sentence without reusing any letter more than once.

    (any meaningful pangram will do) Mr. Jock, TV quiz PhD, bags few lynx (not so meaningful)

    The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. (meaingful but misleading)

    How razorback-jumping frogs can level six piqued gymnasts! (playful)

    Mr. Jock, TV quiz PhD, bags few lynxes.

    Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking: Passes ()
    • Mr. Jock, TV quiz PhD, bags few lynx (contains all words)
    Grok3 Think: Passes (121 Seconds)

    Glib jocks quiz nymph to vex dwarf

    • If you are into serious accuracy, Grok 3 might be a good fit, the only model that understood the changes (we don’t have to use all letters) to question and modified answers based on that. Talk about taking a shortcut.
    • If you are into writing and creating writing, go for Claude Sonnet 3.7. Not only it provides good response but also allows you to customize the response according to your liking. Also, the COT Reasoning was superb, and it generated 5 -7 sentences, close to being pangrams.
    • Open AI o3-Mini-High again displayed that it Favours training data and generated a famous pangram, which others arrived by careful reasoning, however its almost meaningless similar to Deep Seek R1.

    Analysis Based Top AI Models Across Different Categories

    • Grok 3 Think and Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking stand out, especially for their ability to validate answers and ensure correctness.
    • If you need strong mathematical reasoning, these are excellent choices.
    • When it comes to handling complex logical problems and deep reasoning tasks, Deep Seek R1 and Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking take the lead.
    • These models excel in understanding intricate scenarios and delivering well-structured insights.
    • Grok 3 Think is the best overall performer for programming tasks, delivering high-quality code solutions efficiently.
    • This is followed closely by OpenAI o3-Mini-High and Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking, both of which provide solid coding support but may fall slightly behind in handling intricate logic.
    • If your primary focus is creative and linguist-based writing tasks, you can pick any model apart from Deepseek R1, as the differences are not highly significant.
    • However, Claude Sonnet 3.7 Thinking stands out due to its support for writing style customization and tone adjustments, making it a great choice for those who need flexibility in their writing.

    I would only say this, Each model has its strengths, and the best choice depends on your specific needs.

    Whether you are working on complex math problems, advanced reasoning tasks, coding challenges, or writing projects, there is an AI model suited for you.

    With this let's conclude for today, in meantime if you have any feedback or suggestions drop it in the comments and if you want to discuss / learn something regarding the blog, hit me up on X / LinkedIn.

items.title: Monitoring & Logging in DevOps: Tools and Best Practices
items.author: Yash Sonawane
items.content:
Imagine you are running a large-scale e-commerce website, and suddenly, your customers start facing slow page loads and failed transactions. Without proper monitoring and logging, diagnosing the issue could take hours—or even days—leading to significant revenue loss and a damaged reputation. This is where Monitoring & Logging in DevOps comes in as a game-changer.

In DevOps, real-time observability is crucial to ensure smooth operations, quick incident response, and proactive problem resolution. By implementing the right monitoring and logging strategies, teams can track system performance, detect anomalies, and troubleshoot efficiently, minimizing downtime and enhancing reliability.

Why Monitoring & Logging Matter in DevOps

  1. Proactive Issue Detection – Identify issues before they impact end users.
  2. – Improve system efficiency with real-time data.
  3. – Debug and resolve issues quickly with structured logs.
  4. – Ensure adherence to regulatory requirements.
  5. – Streamline communication between Dev, Ops, and Security teams.

Step-by-Step Guide: Implementing Monitoring & Logging

1. Setting Up Monitoring with Prometheus & Grafana

Step 1: Install Prometheus

wget https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/releases/download/v2.30.0/prometheus-2.30.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz
 prometheus-2.30.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz
prometheus-2.30.0.linux-amd64/
./prometheus .fileprometheus.yml

Ensure Prometheus is running by accessing.

apt-get  adduser libfontconfig1
wget https://dl.grafana.com/oss/release/grafana_8.3.3_amd64.deb
dpkg  grafana_8.3.3_amd64.deb
systemctl start grafana-server
systemctl grafana-server

, default login: admin/admin.

Step 3: Connect Prometheus to Grafana

  • Logstash failing to read logs → Check syntax

Real-World Use Cases & Comparisons

Time-series data collection
Custom dashboards, alerts
Full-text search, scalability
Data transformation, pipeline
Real-time dashboards, analytics

Case Study: How Netflix Uses Monitoring & Logging

Netflix uses an advanced monitoring setup with Prometheus and Grafana for real-time performance tracking, ensuring a seamless streaming experience. They also leverage the ELK Stack to analyze logs, helping them detect and resolve issues proactively.

Learn the best Monitoring & Logging practices in DevOps with tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack. Step-by-step guide, use cases, and troubleshooting tips included.

DevOps Monitoring, Logging in DevOps, Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack, Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana, Site Reliability Engineering.

Monitoring and logging are the backbone of a reliable DevOps environment. By integrating tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and ELK Stack, you can ensure seamless observability, quicker debugging, and improved system performance.

items.title: What is Dynamically Linked Library
items.author: Selfish Dev
items.content:
A Dynamically Linked Library (.dll) (on Windows) or Shared Library (.so) (on Linux) is a file that contains reusable code that programs can load while running, instead of being included inside the program itself.

(Think of it like the function in Godot.)

Imagine you're developing a game using SDL and you compile your code like this:

g++ sdl.cpp -o sdl -lSDL2

Every time you update your code, you the entire program. This is time-consuming and frustrating, especially for large projects.

Instead, we can divide our game into multiple DLLs.

By moving different parts of the game (like physics, audio, and rendering) into separate DLLs, you can update only those parts without recompiling the entire game. Once the DLL is updated, your main sdl.cpp file automatically picks up the changes.

its literally like a magic

items.title: Git tips and tricks I use every day
items.author: Mark Hesketh
items.content:
I am by no means an expert, but I've been using Git for years at this point. Over that time I've picked up some tips, tricks and habits I use daily that I think could help you too.

1. Switching back to the previous branch

I swap between main/master, my feature branch and back to main/master constantly. Rather than typing out the name of branch you want to switch to, Git provides a nice alias to switch back to your previous branch:

This might look familiar as it mirrors which navigates to your previous directory in the terminal in the same way.

I use this all the time to ping pong between main/master and whatever feature branch I'm working on. For example:

$ git branch
* main

$ git checkout -b feature-branch
Switched to branch 'feature-branch'

$ git switch -
Switched to branch 'main'

$ git switch -
Switched to branch 'feature-branch'

$ git switch -
Switched to branch 'main'

2. Pushing to a remote branch of the same name

When working on a feature branch you'll often need to push your changes to a new remote branch before creating a PR. Typically, the remote branch has the same name as your local.

Its tedious having to copy/paste (or god forbid type out) your branch name. What if there was a better way?

It pushes and creates a branch on the remote using the same name as your local branch. It doesn't matter how long or tedious your branch name is. Using the alias will pick it up from your local branch, like so:

$ git checkout -b feature-branch
Switched to a new branch 'feature-branch'

$ git push origin HEAD
...
To github.com:company-name/project-name.git
 * [new branch]      HEAD -> feature-branch

I find this useful as at work we have conventions for branch names. They're often long and include ticket numbers, for example feature/123456/great-new-feature-that-will-triple-revenue.

By using I never have to care about the branch name. It works every time. In fact, I use an alias of on all my feature branches when pushing.

Git allows you to specify aliases to effectively create your own Git commands.

You probably have some shell aliases set up already, you might even have some aliases specific to Git. I prefer to use git aliases rather than shell aliases as they're scoped to Git and feel like real Git commands.

To create one open up and add your commands under the section like so:

...
[alias]
    deploy = push production master
...

This will create a new command which is as if you wrote git push production master.

# Cleans your working copy, resetting everything to the previous commit creating a blank slate. Stolen from Jeffrey Way 
nah = "!f() { git clean -d -f; git reset --hard; }; f"

# git status, but shorter
st = status -s

# Removes the last commit, but keeps the changes so you can make any changes or deletions
undo = reset HEAD~1 --mixed

# Unstages any changes
unstage = restore --staged

4. Open the current project in GitHub

A couple of shell aliases (not git aliases) I like are:

These open up the current directory's project in GitHub in the browser.

opens the remote and opens the remote, depending on whether I'm working on a fork or not.

I find these useful when I'm working on a project and want to take a look at the GitHub issues or PRs.

If you'd like to use them too, open up your shell's config or alias file (/ and add the following:


function gh() {
    local repo_url=`git remote get-url origin | sed -e 's/git@//' -e 's/.git//' -e 's/:/\//'`
    open "https://$repo_url"
}

function ghu() {
    local repo_url=`git remote get-url upstream | sed -e 's/git@//' -e 's/.git//' -e 's/:/\//'`
    open "https://$repo_url"
}
