items.title: How i Approached a last project
items.author: Sujit M
items.content:
I have been asked to solve the idea of building a workflow management, asset management and Inter Organisation Communication.

I needed to understand the problem statement of workflow management since there are numerous of application out there, so what could be different in this one.

Understanding the workflow management in 3D Garments design industry

  1. Get the clear idea how the management has working so far
  2. Evaluate if the idea is common (standard) across industry.
  1. After research and countless conversation with the existing manufacturer and Agencies, We came to a conclusion of creating a simple workflow solution

You might ask what is collection, products, and todo's in this context?

Collections - Set of products design for specific event eg. Summer Collection or Kids Collection Products- Are the individual asset related to respective collections - Summer hat, Kids Shirt

Todos - Are actual work done for the respective product

I wont be able to share more details on how does these todos work.

If you have ever build or even work related to files then you know, you have to handle the permissions and accessibility of files. If you are not aware of the file permissions then there are countless blogs available just google it.

How i approached a Asset Management

Assets have base permission

  1. Public All private Assets are accessible at a team level
  2. Private All Public Assets are accessible by any user who is part of the platform.

All the Asset permissions are handled at the DB level where each user group has a set of permissions to access r-w (Read-Write)

You might think then can i bypass the DB and access the file directly? Answer is No.

Access to files directly is blocked, Only Server will create a temporary access to that user and device. It has TTL (Expiry time for the new token) which is very low.

There are other internal permissions i have added but i can not share.

Hope this give you idea of how we achieved a simple yet powerful workflow and asset Management.

We will talk about communication in next part.

items.title: Webflow SEO Guide 2025
items.author: Takhir Adilzhanov
items.content:
If you're a developer building sites on Webflow, you've probably heard conflicting information about its SEO capabilities. Some claim it's excellent, others say it's limiting. Let's cut through the noise and get straight to what actually works.

The Foundation: Webflow's Built-in SEO Features

Before diving into tactics, you need to configure these essential settings:

  1. Enable advanced SEO settings in your site configuration
  2. Disable the Webflow subdomain after going live (crucial to prevent duplicate content)
  3. Set up global canonical tags to avoid search engines penalizing you for duplicate content
  4. Customize your robots.txt file directly in Webflow settings
  5. Verify your site with Google Search Console for performance monitoring

If you skip these steps, you're building on shaky ground. Get them right first.

On-Page SEO: The Technical Requirements

On-page optimization is where the real work happens. Here's what actually moves the needle:

Page Structure & Metadata 

Pro tip: Metadata implementation is one of the most overlooked aspects of Webflow SEO.
  • Keep under 60 characters, include main keyword near the beginning
  • Limit to 155 characters, include call-to-action, make them click-worthy
  • Keep it clean and descriptive (e.g., /services/web-development rather than )
  • Use proper H1-H6 tags to create a logical content structure
    • Use H2s for main sections

The algorithm rewards content that serves user intent:

  • Place primary keywords in H1, first paragraph, and naturally throughout
  • Create comprehensive resources (1500+ words for pillar content)
  • Connect related pages with descriptive anchor text
  • Add ALT text to all images with descriptive, keyword-rich (but natural) language

Technical SEO: What Actually Matters

Webflow handles some technical SEO automatically, but these areas need your attention:

Google's Core Web Vitals affect your rankings, so optimize for:

  • Compress all images before upload (WebP format is ideal)
  • Test all breakpoints thoroughly
  • Minimize custom code bloat
  • Webflow generates this automatically, but ensure all important pages are included
  • Add structured data for rich snippets (requires custom code)
  • Create a custom 404 with helpful navigation options

Common Webflow SEO Pitfalls

Avoid these Webflow-specific issues:

  1. often lack proper metadata - set these up individually
  2. can tank page speed - resize before uploading
  3. can create render-blocking resources - load them asynchronously
  4. - clean structure with semantic HTML elements performs better

SEO isn't a set-it-and-forget-it task:

  1. to track user behavior
  2. weekly for issues and opportunities
  3. to identify underperforming pages
  4. from relevant, authoritative sites

Automating Your Webflow SEO

If you're finding the ongoing SEO management too time-consuming, services like Postwhale offer automated SEO research, writing, and publishing specifically for Webflow sites. This can dramatically reduce the manual labor while ensuring your site maintains consistent SEO growth.

Webflow provides an excellent foundation for SEO when configured correctly. The platform itself won't limit your SEO potential - your implementation will. Focus on these technical elements first, then shift your attention to creating valuable, user-focused content that naturally attracts links and engagement.

Remember: the best SEO strategy is creating genuinely useful content for real people, delivered on a technically sound platform. Webflow gives you that platform - the rest is up to you.

What aspects of Webflow SEO are you struggling with? Drop a comment below and I'll help troubleshoot your specific issues.

items.title: Container Services on AWS: ECS, EKS, and Fargate
items.author: Sushant Gaurav
items.content:
As cloud computing evolves, containerization has become the preferred method for deploying and managing applications. AWS provides multiple container services to simplify this process, including Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service), Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service), and AWS Fargate. Each of these services caters to different needs, ranging from managed Kubernetes environments to serverless container orchestration.

In this article, we will explore , comparing their features, use cases, and best practices. By the end, you’ll have a clear understanding of which AWS container service best suits your workload.

Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service)

Amazon ECS is a fully managed container orchestration service that enables you to deploy, manage, and scale containerized applications using . ECS integrates with AWS services like CloudWatch, IAM, ALB, and IAM roles to provide a seamless container experience.

  • – Works well with AWS IAM, CloudWatch, and Load Balancers.
  • – Choose between running containers on EC2 instances or using AWS Fargate for a serverless approach.
  • – Automatically scales containers based on demand.
  • – Leverages AWS IAM roles and VPC for secure container execution.

Example ECS Task Definition:

  • Running microservices architectures.
  • Batch processing workloads.
  • Hosting API-driven applications.

Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)

Amazon EKS is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment, management, and scaling of Kubernetes applications on AWS.

  • – Automates cluster management and maintenance.
  • – Runs Kubernetes control planes across multiple AWS AZs.
  • Integration with AWS Services – Works with AWS Load Balancer Controller, IAM roles for service accounts, and CloudWatch monitoring.
  • Supports Native Kubernetes Tooling – Helm, Kubectl, and Terraform.

Example: Deploying a Kubernetes Pod on EKS

  • Large-scale microservices architectures.
  • Applications requiring Kubernetes-specific features.
  • Multi-cloud or hybrid cloud deployments.

AWS Fargate is a serverless compute engine for containers that eliminates the need to manage the underlying infrastructure. It supports both , allowing you to run containers without provisioning or managing EC2 instances.

  • – AWS manages the infrastructure automatically.
  • – You pay only for the resources used.
  • – Each task runs in its own isolated compute environment.
  • – Automatically scales containerized applications based on demand.

Example: Running an ECS Task on Fargate

  • Running short-lived, event-driven applications.
  • Serverless applications needing quick scaling.
  • Developers wanting to focus only on application code.

ECS vs. EKS vs. Fargate: A Comparison

No orchestration (compute only)
User-managed EC2 or FargateUser-managed EC2 or Fargate
Fully serverless containers

AWS offers multiple container services, each catering to different needs. is great for AWS-native users, is ideal for Kubernetes users, and is perfect for those who want a fully serverless experience.

Next, we will explore AWS Route 53: DNS Management in the Cloud, covering its capabilities, configurations, and best practices for domain management in AWS.

items.title: A Cultura DevOps e o dia-a-dia de um DevOps Engineer
items.author: Olivério Neto
items.content:
Muitas pessoas ainda se perguntam: DevOps é uma cultura ou uma profissão?

E a resposta é... cultura! E, a partir dela, surgiu a profissão: DevOps Engineer! 👊

Pra gente entender, precisamos contextualizar como um dia já foi na ausência do DevOps.

Anos e anos atrás, Desenvolvimento e Operações trabalhavam de forma , quase como dentro da mesma empresa.

Times de Desenvolvimento (Dev)

  • Responsável por criar e lançar novas funcionalidades
  • Focado em velocidade e inovação
  • Entregava código para produção e passava para a próxima tarefa
  • Via o Ops como um obstáculo que dificultava os lançamentos
  • Responsável por manter os sistemas estáveis e seguros
  • Focado em confibiabilidade e disponibilidade
  • Evitava mudanças bruscas para minimizar riscos e falhas
  • Via o Dev como alguém que quebrava tudo e sumia

Essa separação gerava problemas..

❌ O Dev lançava código sem pensar na estabilidade, e o Ops sofria com bugs e falhas. ❌ O Ops bloqueava mudanças para manter o sistema estável, e o Dev se frustava com a demora.

❌ Quando algo quebrava, um time culpava o outro, em vez de resolver juntos.

Isso resultava em ciclos longos de entregas, e uma cultura de culpa e frustação.

Algo tava errado.. vários times e empresas tentavam buscar soluções pra solucionar esse problema.

Em 2008, durante a — um evento de organizado pela O'Reilly — uma nova abordagem começou a ganhar forma. e , engenheiros do , apresentaram a palestra "10+ Deploys Per Day: Dev and Ops Cooperation at Flickr". Nela, revelaram como conseguiam realizar mais de 10 deploys por dia com rapidez, eficiência e segurança.

O segredo? Colaboração entre Dev e Ops, automação e integração contínua — conceitos que se tornariam a base do .

Na época, isso era revolucionário, especialmente para grandes empresas acostumadas a ciclos de deploy longos e burocráticos.

Surge então o Patrick Debois.

Em 2009, Patrick Debois organizou o primeiro evento focado no tema: o DevOpsDays, realizado em Ghent (Bélgica) e reunindo desenvolvedores, engenheiros de operações e especialistas em infraestrutura com um objetivo comum: entregar software de forma rápida e confiável, sem atrito entre os times.

Desde então, o termo começou a ganhar força e se espalhou pelo mundo! O continua acontecendo até hoje em diversas cidades, inclusive no Brasil!

O DevOps pode ser resumido na sigla CAMS:

✅ - Colaboração entre Dev e Ops, eliminando barreiras e promovendo responsabilidade compartilhada.

✅ A - Automação (Automation) - Automatização de processos como CI/CD, testes e infraestrutura como código.

✅ M - Medição (Measurement) - Monitoramento de métricas para otimizar desempenho e identificar melhorias.

✅ S - Compartilhamento (Sharing) - Troca de conhecimento, boas práticas, feedback e aprendizado contínuo.

Com o tempo, cada vez mais surgiu a necessidade de profissionais que pudessem aplicar a cultura DevOps nas empresas e foi assim que o mercado começou a usar o termo .

Na época, lá atrás.. muitos, inclusive eu, vieram do / . Já tinham a bagagem de resolver problemas relacionados a infraestrutura local, redes, comunicação, protocolos, etc. Isso é um diferencial e tanto. Outros começaram a estudar do zero pra ingressar na área.

E quais são as habilidades que o mercado cobra desse profissional?

Entre parênteses vai exemplos.. Não significa que você precisa dominar todas ferramentas, ninguém é um robô.

  • (Scrum, Kanban, XP)
  • Conhecimento em Sistemas Operacionais (Linux, Windows)
  • (Protocolos de Comunicação, Endereçamento IP, Serviços de Redes)
  • Cloud Computing e Infraestrutura (AWS, GCP, Azure)
  • Containers e Orquestração (Docker, Kubernetes, Helm)
  • Infraestrutura como Código (IaC) e Automação (Terraform, Ansible)
  • Integração e Entrega Contínua (CI/CD) (Jenkins, Github Actions, GitLab CI/CD, Azure DevOps, ArgoCD, CircleCI, TravisCI)
  • Monitoramento e Observabilidade (Zabbix, Prometheus, Grafana, ELK Stack, Graylog, Datadog, Dynatrace, New Relic, Jaeger, OpenTelemtry)

Sou do time que prefere entender antes de avançar. Livros, documentação oficial, artigos e vídeos. Depois que estou com uma ideia meio que já formada e sei do que se trata, pra que serve, como funciona, avanço e busco um conteúdo mais amplo. Geralmente, um curso.

DevOps é uma das comunidades mais ativas que existe na área de TI.

Me sigam aqui e em minhas redes sociais!

items.title: Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator: Predicting Winter Weather with Precision
items.author: waqar Sial
items.content:
Winter brings with it a sense of wonder, cozy nights by the fire, and the occasional snow day. For students, teachers, and even remote workers, the anticipation of a snow day can be both exciting and nerve-wracking. Will school be canceled? Should you prepare for a day off? Enter the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator, a revolutionary tool designed to predict snow days with remarkable accuracy. In this article, we’ll explore how this innovative calculator works, its benefits, and why it’s becoming a must-have tool for winter weather planning.

What is the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator? The Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator is an advanced online tool that uses weather data, historical trends, and predictive algorithms to estimate the likelihood of a snow day in your area. Whether you’re a student hoping for a day off or a parent planning for childcare, this calculator takes the guesswork out of winter weather predictions.

The tool is designed to analyze multiple factors, including:

Current weather conditions: Temperature, snowfall rates, and wind speed.

Forecasted weather: Upcoming storms, precipitation types, and accumulation predictions.

Local school district policies: Each district has its own criteria for canceling school, such as road conditions or extreme cold.

Historical data: Past snow day trends in your region.

By combining these variables, the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator provides a percentage-based prediction of whether schools will close due to winter weather.

How Does the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator Work? The Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator leverages cutting-edge technology to deliver accurate predictions. Here’s a breakdown of how it works:

  1. Data Collection The tool gathers real-time weather data from reliable sources, such as the National Weather Service (NWS) and local meteorological stations. It also considers long-term forecasts to account for upcoming storms or temperature drops.

  2. Algorithmic Analysis Using machine learning and predictive analytics, the calculator processes the collected data. It compares current conditions with historical patterns to identify trends and correlations. For example, if a similar weather pattern in the past led to school closures, the algorithm factors that into its prediction.

  3. Localized Predictions The calculator tailors its predictions to your specific location. By inputting your zip code or school district, you receive customized results based on local weather conditions and district policies.

**4. User-Friendly Interface **The Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator is designed for ease of use. Simply enter your location, and within seconds, you’ll receive a detailed prediction, complete with a percentage likelihood of a snow day.

Why Use the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator?

  1. Accurate Predictions Unlike traditional weather forecasts, which focus on general conditions, the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator specializes in predicting school closures. Its accuracy makes it a trusted resource for students, parents, and educators.

  2. Time-Saving Instead of constantly refreshing weather apps or waiting for official announcements, the calculator provides instant insights. This saves time and reduces stress during uncertain weather conditions.

  3. Better Planning For parents, knowing the likelihood of a snow day allows for better childcare arrangements. For students, it helps manage homework and study schedules. Even remote workers can benefit by preparing for potential disruptions.

  4. Fun and Engaging Let’s face it—snow days are exciting! The Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator adds an element of fun to winter weather predictions, making it a popular tool among students and families.

The Science Behind Snow Day Predictions Predicting snow days isn’t just about guessing when it will snow. It involves a deep understanding of meteorology, local infrastructure, and decision-making processes. Here’s a closer look at the science behind the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator:

  1. Meteorological Factors Snow days are influenced by several weather-related factors, including:

Snow Accumulation: The amount of snow expected to fall.

Temperature: Extremely low temperatures can lead to school closures, even without significant snowfall.

Wind Chill: High winds can make conditions feel much colder and more dangerous.

Road Conditions: Icy or snow-covered roads can make transportation unsafe.

  1. School District Policies Each school district has its own guidelines for canceling school. Some districts may close for even a small amount of snow, while others require more severe conditions. The Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator takes these policies into account to provide accurate predictions.

  2. Historical Data By analyzing past snow days, the calculator identifies patterns and trends. For example, if a particular region frequently experiences school closures during specific weather conditions, the algorithm factors that into its predictions.

How to Use the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator Using the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator is simple and straightforward. Follow these steps to get your prediction:

Visit the Website: Go to the official Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator website.

Enter Your Location: Input your zip code or select your school district.

Review the Results: The calculator will display the likelihood of a snow day, along with detailed weather information.

Plan Accordingly: Use the prediction to make informed decisions about your day.

Tips for Maximizing Your Snow Day Experience Once you’ve confirmed a snow day with the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator, it’s time to make the most of your day off. Here are some fun and productive ideas:

  1. Build a Snowman Embrace the winter wonderland by building a snowman or having a snowball fight with friends and family.

  2. Bake Winter Treats Warm up with some homemade hot chocolate, cookies, or other winter-themed treats.

  3. Catch Up on Homework If you’re a student, use the extra time to complete assignments or study for upcoming exams.

  4. Enjoy a Movie Marathon Cozy up with blankets and watch your favorite movies or TV shows.

  5. Stay Safe While snow days are fun, it’s important to stay safe. Avoid unnecessary travel and dress warmly if you go outside.

The Future of Snow Day Predictions As technology continues to advance, tools like the Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator will only become more accurate and sophisticated. Future developments may include:

Integration with Smart Devices: Imagine receiving snow day predictions directly on your smartphone or smart home device.

Enhanced Machine Learning: Improved algorithms could provide even more precise predictions by analyzing additional data points.

Global Expansion: While currently focused on regions with frequent snowfall, the calculator could expand to predict other weather-related disruptions, such as hurricanes or floods.

Conclusion The Crystal Ball Snow Day Calculator is more than just a fun tool—it’s a game-changer for winter weather planning. By combining real-time data, advanced algorithms, and localized insights, it takes the uncertainty out of snow day predictions. Whether you’re a student dreaming of a day off or a parent preparing for unexpected closures, this calculator is your go-to resource for staying ahead of winter weather.

items.title: Chapter 3: images part one
items.author: Ross Angus
items.content:
Last time, we configured a local server and previewed our work:

  • We installed so we can see the contents of in a web browser
  • Learned about escape characters
  • Ran more than one command at the same time
  • Learned about the special command name
  • Installed, then removed a package because we changed our mind
  • Learned that if something goes wrong with we can always nuke it and start again

This lesson is all about optimising images. There's a lot to get through here, but I'm going to take it slow.

Perhaps right now, you're feeling a bit of imposter syndrome. We are standing on the shoulders of the giants who made all this technology, and yet we are not fit to shine their shoes.

Don't worry: I have a cure for that.

Let's visit a site made by some of the best engineers the world has ever seen. How about Apple? I need you to open the home page in (sorry, - I'm cheating on you this one time). Don't dismiss the cookies or click on anything else and open the developer tools. You can do this by either hitting the F12 key or right-clicking on an empty patch of page and selecting from the pop-up menu.

There's a lot going on in the developer tools but I want you to pay attention to the tabs across the top. They list off the different parts and start Elements Console Sources Network ... I'd like you to select . It might be hidden behind one of these wee chaps: ».

Once the Lighthouse panel has displayed, click the button and wait for the results. Is everything green?

You can scroll down the report and under the heading, you should see a red warning triangle next to a sub-heading which reads Serve images in next-gen formats. We're going to address this problem in the next lesson, but let me ramble on about for a bit first.

Running a Lighthouse audit is mostly a little disheartening. Getting green scores across the board is basically impossible, if you have a marketing department which is determined to drop a 500MB auto-playing video into a popup window. And even if you don't have this problem, improving the score requires many different departments working together.

In the next lesson, we're going to do what Apple failed to do: serve all our images in "next gen" formats.

(and if you're still feeling like a bad engineer, try running Microsoft through Lighthouse)

When it comes to packages which can convert images to cutting-edge modern formats, the new hotness is Sharp. It's so bleeding-edge that we need to write some of our own tools to run it.

is designed to be called from within a application and can do all kinds of stuff like adding blurs, adding text, rotating or converting images to different formats. It's just the last bit we want to use.

However, it doesn't just get installed and then called, like the other plugins we've been using. We're going to have to write a few functions to get it to do what we want.

An ideal developer user experience would be the following:

  1. You start to run your local server
  2. You dump a bunch of images into the directory (this doesn't exist yet)
  3. Those images get automagically converted into next generation files in the directory (this doesn't exist either, yet)

You might also want to do any of the following:

  • Run a task which converts images in the directory into their optimised versions in - for example when you've added some images but the server wasn't running at the time
  • Overwrite an existing image in with a new one with the same name, and have the corresponding images in update automatically
  • Use as many different sub-directories in and have those be replicated on - for example which lives inside as dist/img/gallery/open-day

That's a lot of features! It's easy to feel overwhelmed when seeing requirements like this. You might mentally try and solve all of them at once. Resist this temptation, if you can! Remember that computers are very, very stupid and need to be told very simple instructions. Most of our job is to take complicated tasks and break them down into very simple steps.

Let's create some new directories for these images to sit inside. Add some directories, so your Node project folder looks like this:

🗀 dist
  🗀 css
  🗀 img
🗀 src
  🗀 img
  🗀 scss
.gitattributes
.gitignore
package.json
package-lock.json

Great! Now we just need to write all the code which converts the image from one format to another. So how do we get started?

Installing another package

If you guessed "installing another package from NPM", you have earned a new achievement: "Web Developer Cynicism Level 1". Spend those experience points wisely! The package we need to install is called .

In order to use , we need to edit . Currently, the scripts node looks like this:

We need to change it to look like this:

  • - this is the name we're calling our new command. It could be anything, as long as it doesn't include spaces
  • - when we run our task, the first thing it does is invoke the method which we've just added to
  • - this is the (escaped) path to the directory which will be watching.
  • - I can't find what this empty flag means in the documentation. It seems to act as a sort of separator where anything after it is a new command which will run, when detects a change
  • - this allows us to add text to the terminal. It's the same as in JavaScript
  • - this is a string which is built up from two tokens and . This string will be ed to the terminal (this just means that the text will appear in the terminal). These tokens are passed by . What do they mean? Let's find out!

Now from a terminal, run our new command by typing:

runs constantly - it won't stop until we put the terminal in focus and hold down ctrl and c to cancel out of it. This means that as we update the directories, you can watch the terminal and see it update in real time.

Now save the second one, but overwrite the first. You should see the following:

Now try renaming the file to . You should see:

OK, so the two s we're interested in are and - when these occur, we need to ... do something. And that something has to do with the value of the second argument .

First thing we need to do is tell that we want to use JS modules. Modules are the most modern way to use JavaScript, where every function lives in its own wee file and has a single purpose (at least that's the idea).

To enable this, we need to add a new node to :

This needs to sit in the "root" of , as a sibling of other nodes you might recognise, such as and .

Passing arguments to a script

We're going to write a script (don't worry - it's a ) which will run and gather together all this information. But it's not going to sit inside .

Create a new directory called and put it in the root of the application. This means it's a sibling to and . The word "root" can get pretty confusing. Here's three different roots we might encounter:

  • The root of the application (where lives)
  • The root of the local web server (that's the directory)
  • What regards as the root of the file system (this is probably the C: drive on your computer and we'll try and avoid this root as much as possible)

The directory structure of your project should look like this:

🗀 dist
  🗀 css
  🗀 img
🗀 src
  🗀 img
  🗀 scss
🗀 tools
.gitattributes
.gitignore
package.json
package-lock.json

The new file should be called . It looks like this:

Hmm. There's a lot going on there. Let's break it down.

First thing we do is import a function called from . This is built into and allows us to examine arguments, but not in the philosophical sense. We're going to pass an argument in a moment.

Next, we do what is called destructuring, which is a very cool, modern way to assign a bunch of variable at the same time. Because we know that is going to send us data inside an Array in a particular order, we can get ahead of the game and give them nicknames.

Finally, we two of our favourite arguments to the console. ( is like , but in JavaScript)

Couple's therapy (passing arguments on)

Are you still running ? Stop that! Type ctrl and c in the terminal to kill that command.

Now in we're going to update that command so it does something more useful. Change:

This works exactly the same as before, but instead of ing to the terminal, we're passing the and arguments to our script. Let's see if it works.

Now return to my example image page and download the second image again. Save it to and just leave the name how it is.

You should see this in the terminal:

srcmgBZtOtY.png add
srcmgBZtOtY.png change

This might not seem like much progress, but we're using the power of to see a live update of what's happening on your hard drive. But this time we can manipulate it with JavaScript.

Hang on, what's doing the image optimisation exactly?

The package we're going to install to do the optimisation is called . It's quite big and complicated, so it might take a little while to finish. Let's install it! Cancel out the command which is currently running and in the terminal, type:

This should update the node in with a reference to .

Import into and see if it works. At the top of the file, add this line:

Now let's call and point it as the variable. Your whole file should look like this:

It's a little harder to read so usually we indent the chained functions by one tab stop, like the original example.

Let's run the task again. In the terminal, type

Now try taking any example image and saving it to . Give it a new name which doesn't currently exist inside the directory.

If everything's worked, you should see a new file inside your folder called . Your first "next generation" image! You should be pleased.

Sensible naming conventions

But we can't call of our images , that would be silly. Luckily, comes with a whole suite of tools called to help us manipulate filepaths and filenames. Let's pull it into the top of like we did with :

Now let's see what we're working with. Change so it looks like this:

Quick reminder of where we are:

  • is passing us the path to a file which has changed, plus an event, which we're not using yet
  • We're loading the path into our script file
  • We're using to split this path into different parts
  • Those parts are being ed to the terminal, along with the original path

Cancel the task, then immediately restart it (just in case is cacheing an old version).

Your terminal has a sort of history. If you want to re-use a command you've typed in the past, you can press the up arrow key (↑) on your keyboard to cycle through all the old commands you've entered. Press up and down until you find again, then hit return.

Rename an existing image inside to a new name (doesn't matter what). You should see an error:

Error: Input file is missing: srcmgBZtOtY.png

Right now, this code is running every time detects a change inside . This error is triggered because can't find an image we're telling it to optimise.

Comment-out , so we can see what's going on. Commenting-out can look like this:

Try renaming the file again and look in the terminal. This is what I saw:

Arguments: srcmghanged.png add
Paths:  srcmg changed.png .png
Arguments: srcmgemp.png Paths:  srcmg temp.png .png

Remember, we've got two different s happening, but they seem to be happening twice. Why is this?

The first set of logs happen during an event. The second happen during an event. It looks like is the same as deleting a file. So when I renamed a file, what really happened was that the old file was copied and added into the directory under a new name, then the old file was deleted.

We need to only run our code when the right kind of event happens inside . Save another image from the example image page but this time, save it over an existing image which isn't the same image. You should see logs which look something like this:

Arguments: srcmghanged.png change
Paths:  srcmg changed.png .png

When we did that, we got a event. So the two events we need to watch out for are and . Let's store them in an Array, for safe keeping:

Put this variable the statements. Now we need to corden off our call to behind an statement. This should only run if the is present in our new Array. So the statement should look like this:

(it's safe to comment in inside the statement)

I have terrible news for you and I, members of The™ Windows™ Community™: those kids who use Linux, MacOS and so forth giggle at us behind our backs. You see, Windows uses a backslash () to represent nested directories on the filesystem. The cool kids use a forward slash () instead. If our code is going to run elsewhere, we need to get on board.

Change your variable, so it reads:

This looks like it replaces with , but it doesn't. Remember escape characters? This is another example. The first backslash tells us to ignore the fact that the parser would usually ignore a backslash and to ignore it. It's a double negative. If you're confused right now, take strength from the thought that this is a normal reaction.

What have we achieved? Let's trigger the script again and find out! Rename an existing file to something new. Your console logs should now look something like this:

Arguments: srcmgxample-02.png add
Paths:  src/img example-02.png .png
Arguments: srcmg7zTxla.png Paths:  src/img h7zTxla.png .png

Note how the variable now reads . Before, it was . What's nice about this change is that if the same script is run on a or environment, it won't break because there won't be any back slashes to replace.

Let's get rid of the . We've learned all we can from it.

Building up a path to our distribution image

We need to change the path of a changed image - for example into the path of a converted image - for example . To do this, we need to find the image directory path () and the file name ().

To get the image directory path, let's write a new function!

This takes a path (for example ) and then uses the variable we just created - - and finds the first instance of that in the path, before replacing it with ... nothing. So it removes it.

In arrow functions, if we can squeeze the code onto one line the function returns something, we can omit both the and the statement and the function can automatically return the product. And if our arrow function has exactly one argument, we can dispense with the parenthesis around it. So our function can also be expressed as:

You might prefer the longer version for clarity and I respect that decision.

Add your favourite version of this function (only one, mind!) to . Let's use this function to create a new variable. Put it after the file extension variable, like this:

To get the image filename without the file extension, do a similar trick:

This takes the filename () and replaces the file extension () with nothing, leaving us just the name ().

Finally, we need to stitch together a couple of strings which we'll use more than once:

This will give us .

OK, we've got enough scraps of string to knot together into a proper path now. Change your call to so it looks like this:

Using the backtick character to build up string like this more flexible than the legacy method, which looks like this:

Another advantage of template literals is that they can include whitespace, so you can use whole chunks of JSON data spread out down your code, if you need to. You can switch between strings and simple JavaScript expressions by encapsulating JavaScript inside .

Get rid of your in . In terms of what order variables, imports and functions should appear in your file, the imports should always appear first. You shouldn't call on a variable before you've declared it. Some developers say you should declare all variables at the top of your file, but this isn't always practical (what about variables which occur inside of functions?).

I personally think that if your code includes an statement, then moving what variables you can to inside the statement helps lower the memory footprint (as they're never put into memory, if the code isn't parsed).

This would leave us with the following code:

We've still plenty more to do, but let's recap what we've learned:

  • We can use the package to pass events from the file system to our JavaScript
  • We can run arbitrary JavaScript files directly in - no web browser required
  • gives us access to different methods of accessing path and filename information
  • is this season's must-have image optimisation package (although perhaps that's changed now, if you are reading this in the future)

items.title: # 🚀 Understanding Virtualization, Scalability, and Cloud Computing Concepts
items.author: PETER Samuel
items.content:
Cloud computing is transforming how businesses operate, making IT infrastructure more efficient, scalable, and resilient. In this article, we’ll break down key cloud concepts, including virtualization, scalability, elasticity, high availability, and fault tolerance, to help you understand how modern cloud environments work.

1. What is Virtualization?

Virtualization allows multiple virtual machines (VMs) to run on a single physical machine. This technology enhances , , and in cloud environments.

🔹 A single server can run multiple isolated VMs, each with different applications, preventing hardware waste.

2. Scalability: Growing with Demand

Scalability refers to a system’s ability to handle increased workload by adding resources. It ensures smooth performance as user demand grows.

  • Vertical Scaling (Scaling Up): Adding more power (CPU, RAM) to an existing machine.
  • Horizontal Scaling (Scaling Out): Adding more machines to distribute the load.

🔹 A website increasing its servers during Black Friday sales to prevent crashes.

3. Agility: The Power of Quick Adaptation

Agility in cloud computing means fast adaptation to business needs without delays in deploying new applications or expanding resources.

🔹 A startup testing a new AI model can instantly deploy and scale its cloud resources instead of waiting for hardware setup.

4. High Availability: Minimizing Downtime

High Availability (HA) ensures a system remains operational . Cloud providers use redundancy and failover mechanisms to maintain service uptime.

🔹 A cloud-based banking app remains accessible 24/7, even if one data center fails.

5. Fault Tolerance: Resilience Against Failures

Fault tolerance allows a system to continue running despite failures in hardware or software components. It prevents single points of failure.

🔹 Google Cloud automatically switches to a backup server if the main server crashes.

6. Global Reach: Serving Users Worldwide

Cloud services are accessible globally, ensuring low latency and high performance for users anywhere.

🔹 Netflix stores content across multiple regions so users in different countries experience .

7. Elasticity vs. Scalability: Key Differences

Automatically adjusts resources in real-time based on demand.Increases capacity over time by adding resources.
Short-term, unpredictable workload spikes.Long-term growth and planned expansion.
Fully automated scaling up/down.Can be manual or automated.
A social media app auto-scales servers during viral trends.A growing company adds more database servers over time.
  • is like a rubber band—it expands and contracts based on immediate demand.
  • is long-term planning, ensuring a system can handle more users over time.

Understanding these cloud concepts is crucial for anyone in tech, whether you're a developer, engineer, or business leader. Virtualization, scalability, and elasticity form the foundation of cloud computing, enabling businesses to build efficient, cost-effective, and resilient applications.

🚀 What are your thoughts on cloud scalability and elasticity? Have you worked on projects that required auto-scaling? Let’s discuss in the comments! 👇

items.title: I build a Copy Past manager
items.author: Maxime Sickenberg
items.content:
Why I Created a Copy-Paste Manager for My Work ?

In today's fast-paced work environment, efficiency and accuracy are paramount. One of the most time-consuming tasks is repeatedly copying and pasting information from various sources. To streamline this process and enhance productivity, I decided to create a copy-paste manager. Here are the key reasons why this tool is essential:

  • : Manually copying and pasting data can be tedious and time-consuming. A copy-paste manager automates this process, allowing me to focus on more critical tasks.
  • : Manual copying and pasting increase the risk of errors, such as missing or incorrect information. A copy-paste manager ensures that the data is accurately transferred every time.
  • : Maintaining consistency in formatting and content is crucial, especially when dealing with large volumes of data. A copy-paste manager helps ensure that the information is consistently formatted and presented.
  • : A copy-paste manager allows me to organize and categorize frequently used snippets of text, making it easier to retrieve and use them when needed.
  • : By reducing the time spent on repetitive tasks, a copy-paste manager boosts overall productivity and allows me to allocate more time to strategic and creative work. -: The copy-past manager work without database, so you can use your passwords and hide them !

In conclusion, a copy-paste manager is an invaluable tool that enhances efficiency, accuracy, and productivity. It simplifies the process of managing and transferring information, ultimately contributing to a more streamlined and effective workflow.

items.title: Still Using Redux Everywhere? How an Event Bus Could Transform Your React Workflow
items.author: Ali Morshedzadeh
items.content:
Modern React developers often reach for state management libraries (Redux, Zustand, Recoil, etc.) by default to handle shared state. However, using these state managers everywhere can introduce and boilerplate into your apps. In many cases, an event-driven architecture can be a simpler, more flexible solution. In this article, we’ll explore the pitfalls of overusing state managers, discuss the event-driven alternative, introduce as a powerful event bus for React, and walk through real-world examples with code. We’ll also cover best practices and when to choose events vs. state managers. Let’s dive in!

The Pitfalls of Overusing State Managers

State management libraries like , , and are powerful tools for complex apps. They provide a centralized store and fancy features (time-travel debugging, middleware, etc.), but they come at a cost. Introducing Redux or similar libraries in every situation can be overkill for small to medium apps, adding unnecessary overhead. Redux in particular often requires a lot of boilerplate (actions, reducers, providers) which can make code harder to understand and maintain. Even “lighter” solutions like Zustand or Recoil, while simpler, still add an extra layer of abstraction that you might not need for straightforward cases.

Another common issue is and tangled callback chains in deep component hierarchies. In a typical React app, data flows through props and events bubble through callback props. In a simple component tree this is fine, but as your app grows, passing props through multiple layers (just to get data to a distant component) and wiring callbacks back up becomes cumbersome. You end up with intermediate components that exist only to shuttle data around, making the code harder to follow and maintain. This tight coupling of components increases cognitive load and brittleness in your codebase.

To avoid prop drilling hell, many developers instinctively turn to a global state solution (Context API, Zustand, Redux, etc.) so components can share data without explicit prop passing ([Event-Driven Architecture for Clean React Component Communication - DEV Community]. While this can solve one problem, overusing global state introduces new complexity – you might be maintaining a giant store or context for things that could be handled more simply. So is there a middle ground? Event-driven architecture offers a compelling alternative for many scenarios.

The Event-Driven Alternative: Loosely Coupled Components

Event-driven architecture in React allows components to communicate through a publish/subscribe () pattern rather than via shared state or direct parent-child links. In essence, components can (publish) and (subscribe) using a central , without needing to know about each other’s existence. This decouples your components, making the app architecture more modular and easier to maintain.

In an event-driven approach, when something happens in one component, it can broadcast an event into a central channel. Any other component interested in that event can react to it, regardless of where it sits in the component tree. This means no more drilling props down or lifting state up just to get two distant components talking – the event bus handles that communication in a way.

Because components remain unaware of who is listening or emitting, this pattern . Your components become more self-contained: they just announce what happened (e.g. ) or respond to announcements (“someone deleted an item, I should update my list”) without tight integrations. The result is often – especially for cross-cutting concerns like global notifications, logging, or syncing data – and potentially than an equivalent Redux setup. In fact, an event bus can bring clarity to a large codebase by centralizing how events are handled.

Meet : An Event Bus for React

So how can we implement an event-driven architecture in React? is a lightweight library that provides exactly this: a system. According to its documentation, is a “lightweight, zero-dependency React event bus with full TypeScript support” that enables decoupled component communication using a pub/sub model. In simpler terms, it lets you set up a global event hub in your React app, with minimal code.

  • 🔄 Provides easy methods or hooks to emit events and subscribe to them.
  • 🔒 Built with TypeScript generics, so you can define strict types for each event’s payload (catching mistakes at compile time).
  • ⚛️ Seamless React Integration: Uses React Context under the hood and offers custom Hooks (, , etc.) to interface with the event bus anywhere in your component tree.
  • 🪶 Zero external dependencies and a minimal footprint. It won’t bloat your bundle or slow your app.
  • 🧩 Allows components to talk to each other without direct relationships, eliminating the need for prop drilling or overly complex state lifting.

In short, gives you the benefits of an event bus pattern in a convenient React package.

Getting Started with

Using react-eventizer in your project is straightforward. You can install it via npm or yarn:

npm react-eventizer

Then follow these general steps to set it up:

  1. For TypeScript users, define an interface listing all event names and their payload types. For example, you might have events like , , , etc., each mapped to the shape of data it carries (or to if no payload). This step is optional but highly recommended for type safety.
  2. Create and provide the event bus: Initialize a new event bus instance (e.g. const eventBus = new Eventizer<EventMap>()) and make it available to your app by wrapping your app in an with the bus passed as a prop. Typically, you do this once at the root (e.g. in ).
  3. In any component that needs to react to a global event, use the useSubscribe(eventName, callback) hook provided by . This hook will register your callback to run whenever that event is emitted. (It also handles unsubscribing automatically on component unmount to prevent memory leaks).
  4. Whenever a component needs to broadcast something, use the hook to get an emitter function, and call that function with the appropriate payload. All subscribers to that event will then be notified.

Let’s look at a quick example of in action. Imagine we want to broadcast a user login event so various parts of the app can respond (maybe to display a welcome message, update a profile, fetch user-specific data, etc.). We’ll emit a event from a login form and subscribe to it in a user profile component:

Login

In this example, the doesn’t need to directly inform through context or shared state. It simply emits a event when the form is submitted. The component, anywhere in the app, subscribes to and updates its local state when that event occurs. The two components remain completely decoupled – they only share an event contract. If no component cared about , the event would just go into the void with no issues. If multiple components subscribe to it, all of them will receive the event. This pub-sub mechanism can dramatically simplify scenarios where many components need to respond to the same event.

Real-World Use Cases Made Easier with Events

What kinds of scenarios benefit most from an event-driven approach? Let’s explore a few common use cases where can make React development easier, along with how you might implement them.

Cross-Component Communication (No More Prop Drilling)

You have components that need to talk to each other but are not directly related in the JSX hierarchy. For instance, a deeply nested component needs to send info to a top-level layout or sibling component far away. Traditionally, you might lift state up to a common ancestor or use context – or you suffer prop drilling through many layers.

Use a global event instead! Any component can emit an event that any other component can listen for, without threading props. This achieves instant cross-component communication with .

For example, suppose you have a list of items and a separate notifications area. When an item is deleted in the list, you want to show a notification banner. Without an event bus, you might have to pass a callback from the notifications system down into the item component, or use a global state flag. With , it’s trivial:

Delete Item

Here the Delete button simply emits an event carrying an item ID. A Notification component (or any component interested in deletions) subscribes to and, when it hears one, triggers a user notification. Neither component needs to know about the other – they only share the event name and data schema. This means you can add or remove listeners freely without breaking functionality, and you don’t have to route that event through parent components. It makes your code more modular and your component tree cleaner.

Global Notifications and Alerts

You want to trigger notification messages or alerts from anywhere in the app (deep in the bowels of your code), but have them all display in a common UI component (like a toast container or alert stack). Without events, you might lift all notification state to a top-level store or use context to expose functions for adding notifications.

Treat notifications as global events. Any part of the app can emit a event with the message and type of notification. A single component can subscribe to that event and handle displaying all incoming notifications.

For example, using you could do something like:

Every time an event is emitted (perhaps from anywhere: after API calls, user actions, errors caught, etc.), the will pick it up and update its list of notifications. This way, components don’t have to directly call the notification system or import it; they just fire an event and forget. The notification handling is centralized, which is easier to manage and avoids duplicating notification logic across components.

Global UI State Changes (Themes, Modals, etc.)

Some UI state changes need to affect multiple independent components. For instance, changing the app’s theme should update many components, or closing a modal might need to inform various parts of the UI.

For one-off triggers like theme changes or modal toggles, an event can be more convenient than storing this state globally. You can emit a event or event that any component can respond to.

  • Instead of putting the current theme in a context and making every component consume that context, you could simply have a central theme store (or even just local storage) and emit events. Components that care (like those that style themselves differently or a ) subscribe to apply the new theme. This can reduce continuous re-renders that a context might cause, since an event will only trigger subscribers once when it’s emitted.
  • If a deeply nested component triggers a modal to open, and some other component needs to know when that modal closes, you can emit upon closure. Any component can listen for to, say, refresh data or update the URL, without tightly coupling to the modal logic.

The event-driven approach essentially (like “theme updated” or “modal closed”) in a way that any interested piece of UI can act on, without maintaining that intent as persistent state if it’s not needed beyond the moment of the change.

Handling Asynchronous or External Events

Your app receives asynchronous data or messages (from WebSockets, server-sent events, etc.) that multiple components may need to respond to. Managing this via state can get tricky, especially if not all components mount at the same time or if the data isn’t something you want to store globally.

You can funnel external messages into events. For instance, imagine you have a WebSocket connection that receives real-time updates. With , your WebSocket service can emit events whenever a message arrives:

By doing this, you decouple the WebSocket logic from your React components entirely. Components just declare an interest in events and react when they occur. If the WebSocket implementation changes or moves, the components don’t care – as long as events with that name are emitted. Similarly, you can handle other external or async events (like a countdown finishing, a media query change, etc.) in this pub-sub way.

Best Practices for Event-Driven Architecture in React

Using (or any event bus) effectively requires a bit of forethought. Here are some best practices to keep your event-driven React architecture clean and maintain:

  • Define event types centrally: Declare all your event names and payload structures in one place (e.g. an file with the EventMap interface). This makes it easier to manage and document what events exist in the system, and ensures publishers and subscribers use the correct event names.
  • Use namespaces for event names: Adopting a naming convention like (for example, , , ) helps avoid name collisions and keeps events organized. It’s easier to identify the purpose of an event at a glance, and you won’t accidentally have two different subsystems using generic names like that conflict.
  • If you use the hook, it will automatically unsubscribe when the component unmounts. But if you ever subscribe manually via the event bus’s method, be sure to unsubscribe () when appropriate to prevent memory leaks or unwanted behavior. Keeping subscription lifecycles tied to component lifecycles is usually safest.
  • While events are great for decoupling, avoid emitting events willy-nilly or in very tight loops. Use events for higher-level communication, not as a replacement for every function call or state update. Too many global events can make it hard to trace what's happening. Also, avoid using events to store long-term state – remember that an event is a moment in time, not a state container.

By following these practices, you can ensure that your event-driven approach remains as your application grows.

When to Use Events vs. When to Use a State Manager

It’s important to strike a balance. Event-driven architecture can simplify many aspects of a React app, but it’s not a silver bullet for all state management needs. Here’s a balanced perspective on when to choose each approach:

  • Use an Event Bus (like ) for:Decoupled interactions and notifications. If your goal is to have parts of the app react to something happening elsewhere (user actions, global UI triggers, background processes) without maintaining a lot of shared state, events are ideal. They shine for ephemeral, momentary communications – things that happen, other parts respond, and that’s it. This includes the use cases we discussed: cross-component messages, broadcast notifications, one-off UI updates, and integrating external event streams. Events keep these interactions simple and avoid the overhead of global state for transient data. Use events when you want to avoid forcing a direct link or dependency between components or modules. This can make your code more modular and testable (you can test components by faking events, for example).

  • Use a State Manager (Redux, Zustand, Recoil, Context API) for:Persistent and global state. If you have that many components need to read and update over time (such as user authentication info, a large form data structure, or caching of fetched data), a state store or context might be more appropriate. These tools are designed to hold onto data and make it accessible anywhere, whereas an event bus by itself doesn’t store data – it just transmits it.Complex state logic and debugging. When your state has complex transitions, strict requirements, or benefits from time-travel debugging and traceability (e.g. using Redux DevTools), a structured state manager is beneficial. Redux, for instance, can record every action and state change, which is great for debugging complex apps. Events are more fire-and-forget, which can be harder to debug if overused for critical state changes (though you can certainly log them).Forms and synchronized state. Some scenarios (like multi-step forms or any feature where many parts of the UI must reflect the exact same state consistently) might still be easier with a centralized state or context that components read from directly, rather than emitting events to each other to stay in sync.

In practice, you don’t have to choose one or the other exclusively. You can : use an event bus for what it’s good at and a state store for what it’s good at. For example, you might use React Context or Zustand to hold the current user and theme (since those are needed as state by many components at all times), but use to handle things like “user logged in” or “theme changed” as events to trigger animations, fetches, or notifications across the app. In fact, the combination can be powerful: the event can carry the new state, and subscribers can update their own state or even a context based on it.

“Stop using state managers everywhere” doesn’t mean state libraries are bad – it means you should . Global state managers like Redux, Recoil, and Zustand are fantastic for certain use cases, but not every problem is a nail requiring that hammer. Many times, an provides a simpler and more elegant solution for component communication without the ceremony of global stores. By using or a similar event bus, you can make your React apps more modular, reduce prop drilling, and simplify how components interact.

The key is to choose the right tool for the job: if you find yourself adding a lot of complexity just to get components talking, consider using events to decouple and simplify. On the other hand, if you truly need a single source of truth for state and the ability to inspect or revert it, a state manager might be the way to go. Often, a hybrid approach yields the best of both worlds.

By understanding both paradigms, you can architect React applications that are simpler, more efficient, and easier to maintain. So next time you’re about to pull in a heavy state management library for a simple interaction, ask yourself: “Can an event solve this?” You might be surprised how often the answer is yes, and how much cleaner your code can be as a result.

items.title: JavaScript Weekly: Top Links for March 7, 2025
items.author: JSDev Space
items.content:
Welcome to your JavaScript Weekly Roundup! As we approach mid-March, the JavaScript ecosystem continues to evolve with exciting new tools and techniques. This week's collection features breakthrough libraries, time-saving development tips, and thought-provoking articles that will enhance your coding workflow and keep you informed on the latest trends.

Ramda.js: Enhancing Array and Object Operations in JavaScript

Ramda.js is a powerful functional programming library for JavaScript. Unlike Lodash, it focuses on code purity, supports currying, and ensures data immutability.

Allows calling functions step by step:

Replaces chains with more readable code:

Filtering active users and normalizing their names:

Objects: assoc, dissoc, evolve

Modify objects without mutations:

When is Ramda Not Needed?

For simple operations, standard methods like map, filter, and reduce are often more convenient. Ramda is beneficial for complex transformations, ensuring immutability, and writing declarative code.

lume: A lightweight CLI and local API server to create, run and manage macOS and Linux virtual machines (VMs) natively on Apple Silicon.

Agno: A lightweight library for building Multimodal Agents with memory, knowledge and tools.

ricochet: A peer-to-peer instant messaging system built on Tor hidden services.

Usertour: An alternative to: Appcues, Userpilot, Userflow, userguiding, Chameleon , Etc...

Algora TV: A React component to embed a live streaming video player.

Pruvious: A reliable CMS for your Nuxt app.

webjsx: A library for building web applications with JSX and Web Components.

kreuzberg: A text extraction library supporting PDFs, images, office documents and more

Memory: A self-hosted editor and note taking app.

InfoShop: Free and open source laravel + Inertia JS (React JS) Point of Sale platform

fast-png:PNG image decoder and encoder written entirely in JavaScript.

mercurius: A GraphQL HTTP Server for Fastify with TypeScript and hooks support.

AI SDK 4.1: Vercel AI SDK 4.1 is out with new features and improvements.

Astro 5.4: Astro 5.4 is out with new features and improvements.

Thanks for checking out this week's JavaScript roundup! We hope these resources help you build better applications and solve challenging problems. Join us next Friday when we'll be back with another curated selection of JavaScript excellence from around the web.

items.title: Creating an Azure Compute Gallery and Capturing the Image of a Virtual Machine (VM)
items.author: samuel
items.content:
Azure Compute Gallery is a powerful feature in Azure that enables users to efficiently manage and share VM images across multiple subscriptions and regions. It provides a centralized location to store images and simplifies the deployment of consistent environments across your organization or across your Azure infrastructure. In this article, we will walk through the steps of creating an Azure Compute Gallery and capturing the image of a VM, with an emphasis on the state of the VM being specialized. We’ll also discuss when you might want to generalize the VM instead.

Before beginning, make sure you have the following:

  • An active Azure subscription.
  • Azure CLI or Azure PowerShell installed and configured.
  • Basic understanding of Azure Virtual Machines and image creation.
  • A VM in Azure that is in the desired configuration for capturing.

Step 1: Create an Azure Compute Gallery

First, let’s set up the Azure Compute Gallery to store and manage your VM images.

  1. Navigate to Azure Portal: Go to the Azure Portal and log into your account.
  1. Create a Compute Gallery:
    • In the search bar at the top, search for "Azure Compute Gallery" and click on "Create."
  • Fill out the required details:
    • : Choose the subscription in which the gallery will be created.
    • : Select an existing resource group or create a new one.
    • : Provide a unique name for the gallery.
    • : Select the region where the gallery will be created (it’s recommended to place the gallery in a region close to where most of your VMs reside).
  • Click on and then to deploy the gallery.

Step 2: Prepare the Virtual Machine (VM)

Before capturing an image, ensure the VM is configured and ready to be captured. It’s essential that the VM is in the correct state.

    • In the Azure Portal, navigate to your VM, ensuring it is in the desired state, with all necessary applications, configurations, and updates installed.
  1. Configure the VM for Image Capture:
    • If you want the VM to be specialized, it means the machine has unique configurations like specific user settings, applications, and data. The specialized state is typically required if you intend to use the image for subsequent VM deployments where those customizations should persist.
  • Specialized vs. Generalized VM:
    • : This is a VM that has been fully configured with custom settings. It is generally used when the machine will act as a template for an environment that needs to maintain specific configuration or software settings. It is not suitable for creating images that will be used across multiple tenants or environments.
    • : This is a VM that has been generalized to remove any unique configurations like user accounts or specific machine-specific data, making it ideal for creating reusable, uniform VM images that can be used to create new VMs. A generalized VM is prepared by running the command in Windows or in Linux.

Step 3: Capture the Image of the Specialized VM

  1. Ensure the VM is in Specialized State:

    • If the VM is configured correctly, and you want to capture it as a specialized image, leave the VM in its current state. If it is generalized, refer to the next section for instructions on how to generalize it.
    • To capture the image of the VM, follow these steps:
      • In the Azure Portal, go to the and select from the top menu.
 - Name your image, and select the **Compute Gallery** you just created.
 - Choose the **Image version** (this is how you will track different versions of your image).

 - Make sure to check the box that indicates the VM is **specialized** and **not generalized**.

 - Confirm that you understand the implications of capturing a specialized image.
 - Click **Create** to capture the image.

The VM image is now stored in your Azure Compute Gallery and can be used to deploy new VMs with the same configuration.

While we focused on capturing a specialized image, there are situations where you may want to generalize the VM instead. The primary use case for generalizing a VM is when you need to create a reusable, standardized image for scaling purposes.

  1. : To generalize the VM, use the following process (for Windows VMs, the tool is used, while Linux VMs use the command):
    • Open the inside the VM.
    • Run the following command: 
       sysprep /oobe /shutdown /generalize
    • This command prepares the VM by removing unique machine-specific data and settings. The VM will shut down once the process is completed.
    • Open the terminal inside the VM.
    • Run the command to deprovision the VM: 
       sudo waagent -deprovision+user -force
    • This will remove any user-specific data and settings, preparing the VM for image capture.
  1. Capture the Image After Generalization:
    • Once the VM is generalized, you can proceed with capturing the image by following the same steps we used to capture the specialized image, but this time, the VM image will be available for widespread deployment and will not contain unique data.

Step 4: Use the Image from the Azure Compute Gallery

Once you have captured the image, whether specialized or generalized, it’s available in your Azure Compute Gallery. You can use it to deploy new virtual machines within the same region or across different regions.

  1. Deploying VMs from the Compute Gallery:
    • In the Azure Portal, go to the and select the image you captured.
  • Choose to initiate the deployment process.
  • Follow the prompts to configure the VM settings, such as size, network, and storage.

Creating an Azure Compute Gallery and capturing a VM image is a vital part of managing and scaling virtual machines in Azure. Deciding whether to generalize or specialize the VM depends on your use case. Specialized images are ideal when you need to maintain unique machine configurations, while generalized images are more suitable for creating multiple identical VM instances. Azure Compute Gallery provides an efficient, organized way to manage these images, improving the overall efficiency of your cloud infrastructure.

items.title: A REST Implementation of ‘Docling’ with FastAPI
items.author: Alain Airom
items.content:
A sample application showing Docling implementation, exposing as a REST service, using FastPI in Python.

This is yet another request for a project (much simplified) to produce a REST application based on Docling document conversion from PDF to markdown using FastAPI.

First things first, what is Docling and what does it do if you haven’t heard of it 😲😳

Docling simplifies document processing, parsing diverse formats — including advanced PDF understanding — and providing seamless integrations with the gen AI ecosystem.

  • 🗂️ Parsing of multiple document formats incl. PDF, DOCX, XLSX, HTML, images, and more
  • 📑 Advanced PDF understanding incl. page layout, reading order, table structure, code, formulas, image classification, and more
  • 🧬 Unified, expressive DoclingDocument representation format
  • ↪️ Various export formats and options, including Markdown, HTML, and lossless JSON
  • 🔒 Local execution capabilities for sensitive data and air-gapped environments
  • 🤖 Plug-and-play integrations incl. LangChain, LlamaIndex, Crew AI & Haystack for agentic AI
  • 🔍 Extensive OCR support for scanned PDFs and images
  • 💻 Simple and convenient CLI

The first sample application is meant to be used on a CPU based platform.

The recommended steps are shown below.

#macos/linux version
python3.11 -m venv myenv
source myenv/bin/activate
  • Install the required packages and dependencies. 
pip install fastapi uvicorn docling python-multipart

from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile, File, HTTPException
from docling.document_converter import DocumentConverter, ConverterConfig
from pathlib import Path

app = FastAPI()

@app.post("/convert_pdf_to_markdown/")
async def convert_pdf_to_markdown(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
    if file.content_type != "application/pdf":
        raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Invalid file type. Only PDF files are allowed.")

    try:
        temp_file_path = Path(f"./temp_{file.filename}")
        with open(temp_file_path, "wb") as f:
            f.write(await file.read())

        config = ConverterConfig(ocr_backend="default", layout_backend="default")
        converter = DocumentConverter(config=config)

        result = converter.convert(str(temp_file_path))
        markdown_output = result.document.export_to_markdown()

        temp_file_path.unlink()

        return {"markdown": markdown_output}

    except Exception as e:
        if temp_file_path.exists():
            temp_file_path.unlink()
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail=f"An error occurred during conversion: {str(e)}")
  • Run the code in a Terminal. 
# you can change the defauly port number from 8000 to another one
# if already in use
uvicorn main:app --port 8080
  • Test your code in another Terminal (duh 🤓) 
curl -X POST -F "file=@./docker-commands.pdf" http://127.0.0.1:8080/convert_pdf_to_markdown/
