items.title: Everything* as code
items.author: Kirill
items.content:
Hello, dear visitor. Seeing you here today means you're likely a developer, engineer, coder, or some combination of these -- and that you're familiar with the term "code". For several decades engineers all over the world were writing code. This code, first and foremost, was meant to solve problems. And today I would like to tell you about solving even more problems with the power of code. I will show you why and how we should use code for more and more scenarios across our professional and personal lives.

Before diving into 21st century, let’s step back and consider what "code" really means. Today, we use it for programming, automation, and infrastructure, but the idea of encoding information in a structured, repeatable way has been around for centuries.

What comes to mind when you hear the word "code"? Most of us (myself included) immediately think of a programming language.

But the word "code" wasn’t originally popularized by programmers. Long before computers existed, humans developed ways to encode information, often to preserve knowledge, communicate securely. One of the earliest examples comes from cryptography.

Cryptography is one of my many interests. Ciphers, cryptography algorithms, encryption, all that jazz. The practice of encoding information goes back to Ancient Egypt, but one of the earliest well-known encryption methods is Caesar’s Cipher (also called Caesar’s Code).

Caesar’s Cipher is a simple technique: shifting letters forward in the alphabet to obscure the original message, making it unreadable to anyone who doesn’t know the shift pattern. The result? Encoded information. Information hidden as (somewhat) code.

At its core, this is the same principle that modern software engineering follows. We structure information in standardized formats so it can be stored, processed, and used reliably. Whether it’s YAML configurations, JSON APIs, or structured documentation, we encode information in ways that make it easy to retrieve, automate, and keep track of.

Now let's get back to our time.

Programmers have been writing software at least since 1940. This software is meant to solve different problems: calculate the sum of two numbers, print "Hello World", launch people in space and send memes to your friends. What all these examples have in common? They encapsulate the logic. An algorithm that describes an order of actions to achieve the necessary result.

Indifferent to complexity, all software is alike in this regard. They have an input: be that a number, a certain trigger, a file. They have from one to infinity steps in their core process, each of which performs a certain action. And they have an output: a rocket engine that's starting, a package with part of a meme flying over fiber optics, or a number.

And the logic presented in this very part is described via code.

Thinking rocks (computers) think in a certain way. In a very structured way. In a way that is predictable and repeatable. The same way the code that is written for these computers have to be predictable and repeatable. This way, we can make sure that, unlike meat people, when a computer is asked to run "Hello World" a million times, it will do exactly that -- and not complain on the 10th repetition that "This is pointless" or "Go hello yourself".

Making logic structured, reliable, repeatable helps people offload some of mental capacity on other things. For example, write more code! Create games! Or learn how to play violin!

Would you like to know more?

items.title: Effective Backpressure Handling in Distributed Systems: Techniques, Implementations, and Workflows
items.author: DevCorner
items.content:
Backpressure handling in distributed systems is essential to ensure systems can manage load efficiently, preventing service failures under high traffic conditions. Backpressure occurs when a system or service cannot process incoming requests as quickly as they arrive, leading to overload, failures, or data loss.

In this blog, we'll explore key techniques for handling backpressure, including implementation details and practical examples.

1. Rate Limiting (Token Bucket Algorithm)

controls how frequently a system processes requests, preventing overload by rejecting or delaying excess traffic. The is a widely used rate-limiting technique.

  • The system maintains a "bucket" filled with tokens.
  • Each incoming request consumes a token.
  • Tokens are refilled at a fixed rate.
  • If the bucket is empty, new requests are either rejected or delayed.
  • Prevent abuse by limiting user requests within a time window.
  • Avoid overwhelming downstream services with excessive traffic.
  • Controls request flow.
  • Determines the burst limit.
  • Requests without tokens are delayed or dropped.

drops excess requests when the system is overloaded, ensuring critical services remain functional.

  • The system rejects requests when traffic exceeds a pre-defined threshold.
  • Low-priority requests are discarded first.
  • This prevents the system from becoming entirely unresponsive.
  • Drop non-critical requests to maintain responsiveness.
  • Discard low-priority messages to process important ones.
  • Prevents services from crashing due to excessive load.
  • Threshold-Based Shedding: Drops requests beyond a limit.
  • High-priority requests are processed first.
  • Keeps the system functional under heavy load.

prevent cascading failures by temporarily blocking requests to failing services.

  1. All requests are allowed.
  2. Requests are blocked when failure rate exceeds a threshold.
  3. Some requests are allowed to test if recovery is possible.
  • Prevents one failing service from affecting others.
  • Fails fast instead of overloading backend services.
  • Opens the circuit if failures exceed the limit.
  • Tests whether the service has recovered.
  • Monitors service availability.

temporarily stores requests in a queue, smoothing out spikes in traffic.

  • Incoming requests are stored in a buffer (queue).
  • Requests are processed at a steady rate.
  • Prevents the system from being overwhelmed during traffic spikes.
  • Message Queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ): Store messages before processing.
  • Queue requests instead of rejecting them.
  • Process data at a controlled rate.
  • Holds requests until they can be processed.
  • Prevents sudden bursts from overwhelming the system.
  • Balances data production and consumption.

Backpressure handling techniques such as rate limiting, load shedding, circuit breaking, and buffering are essential for maintaining system stability. Choosing the right strategy depends on system requirements and constraints. As distributed systems grow, mastering these techniques is key to building scalable and resilient architectures.

items.title: Boost Your EF Core Query Performance by 4X with Just One Line of Code Change!
items.author: Ganesh Gurav
items.content:
We have a .NET 6 application with a search functionality that uses LINQ queries with  that needs to join more than 30 tables to fetch data. The feature worked perfectly at the beginning, even after importing legacy data into the new system. But gradually, the feature has begun to slow down considerably because of the huge growth in data volume.

The LINQ query was experiencing . 😞

What is Cartesian explosion? Cartesian Explosion is an Entity Framework performance problem arising when using the Include method in loading more than one level of related data. This can, in turn, multiply the count of records coming back, so it takes time and consumes additional memory.

The following query was pulling data in 30 secs. (Query changed for privacy purposes).

_context.SomeTableName
        .AsNoTracking()
        .Include(x => x.Tasks.OrderBy(y => y.Id))
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Owner)
        .Include(x => x.Tasks)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.TaskStatus)
        .Include(x => x.Tasks)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.TaskComments)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.CreatedBy)
            .OrderBy(x => x.CreatedOn)
        .Include(x => x.Participants.OrderBy(y => y.Id))
            .ThenInclude(x => x.User)
        .Include(x => x.Participants)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Role)
        .Include(x => x.Regions)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Region)
        .Include(x => x.Countries)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Country)
        .Include(x => x.Product)
        .Include(x => x.Groups)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Group)
        .Include(x => x.Businesses)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Business)
        .Include(x => x.Tiers)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Tier)
        .Include(x => x.Segments)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Segment)
        .Include(x => x.Stocks)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Stock)
        .Include(x => x.Types)
            .ThenInclude(x => x.Type)
        .Include(x => x.Policy)
        .Include(x => x.FinalOutcome)
        .Include(x => x.Status)
        .Include(x => x.Attachments)

.AsSplitQuery() to the Rescue

There are a number of ways to make a LINQ query more efficient, but applying this EF Core feature enhanced performance by . By adding  to the LINQ query, the time it took to retrieve data was cut down to 7.5 seconds.

Below are the Before vs After results:

Rather than passing a single SQL query with join, EF Core when utilizing  will pass several SQL queries without join, each retrieving smaller amounts of data and then merging the results, this aids in avoiding the risk of .

There are also few trade-offs linked to this approach:

  • Increases the database round-trips, which may introduce latency, this can worst if the UI app and API are in different regions.

  • Data may get changed between the execution of split queries, which results in inconsistent data.

  • This may not be helpful for smaller dataset.

If you enjoy the content then please leave a like. Thank you! 🙂

items.title: PostgreSQL Security Best Practices: A Developer's Guide
items.author: Team Timescale
items.content:
Secure your PostgreSQL database by configuring access control (UDS + TCP/IP), implementing proper user roles and row-level security, enabling SSL encryption, and using for sensitive data.

Your database contains your most valuable asset: data. Yet a shocking number of PostgreSQL instances remain vulnerable to attacks due to poor security practices.

This guide cuts through the complexity of PostgreSQL security, providing actionable best practices to lock down your database and protect what matters most—without sacrificing performance or accessibility.

PostgreSQL Access Control

To ensure only authorized processes and remote connections can access your PostgreSQL database—providing a foundational layer of security—you need to secure access via Unix Domain Sockets (UDS) and TCP/IP.

Unix Domain Sockets (UDS) provide a file-based access control system, managing permissions between processes on the same host. They facilitate data exchange between processes, establishing a secure and efficient method for local inter-process communication.

PostgreSQL UDS configuration: allows precise control through parameters such as:

  • : Defines the directories where Unix-domain sockets for client connections will be placed.

: Sets the owning group of the Unix-domain sockets.

: Specifies the access permissions for the Unix-domain sockets.

TCP/IP protocol is essential for managing remote database connections. By default, PostgreSQL listens on port 5432 for incoming connections.

is critical - only known and trusted IP addresses should be allowed to connect.

*

This setup allows connections to port 5432 from a trusted IP address while dropping requests from unknown sources.

PostgreSQL User Roles and Groups

Creating users in PostgreSQL is fundamental for database access management. Users are distinct from operating system users, having global scope across a database cluster rather than being restricted to individual databases.

The primary SQL command for this operation is , which allows specifying the username and optionally, a password for the user.

The process is straightforward:

This command creates a new database user with login capabilities.

Roles in PostgreSQL serve as a versatile mechanism for managing database permissions. They can represent individual users, groups of users, or a set of permissions.

This example creates a role with the ability to log in and create databases. Roles streamline permission management through a centralized role-based access control system.

PostgreSQL's Row-Level Security (RLS) feature adds a finer granularity of access control, allowing administrators to define policies that restrict access to specific rows within a table.

💡 _This is particularly useful in multi-tenant environments where users should only see their data. _

To enable RLS, you use the command:

Following this, you can define policies that specify the conditions under which rows are visible or modifiable by a role or user, using the command.

SSL encryption safeguards data in transit, preventing man-in-the-middle attacks. While PostgreSQL doesn't natively implement SSL, it supports SSL connections to encrypt data between clients and servers.

Setting up SSL for PostgreSQL involves:

  • Generating SSL certificates

  • Configuring the PostgreSQL server for SSL

  • Enforcing SSL connections for clients

Database encryption with

The pgcrypto extension adds encryption capabilities directly within PostgreSQL, allowing for the encryption of individual fields, entire tables, or data transactions. This is particularly useful for sensitive data that requires encryption at rest, such as healthcare records or personal identification information.

Basic example of using : To encrypt data without specifying a key explicitly, you can use the function. Here's a simple example that encrypts a text string:

Decrypting the data can be done with the corresponding :

Advanced Security with Timescale

Timescale enhances PostgreSQL by seamlessly integrating with it, inheriting its robust security framework while introducing advanced features.

Using pgcrypto with Timescale

After ensuring Timescale and PostgreSQL are properly installed, activate pgcrypto:

This allows the use of various cryptographic operations within your Timescale database environment. For example, to encrypt sensor readings in a hypertable, you could use the following approach:

To decrypt the data for use, you would use:

Timescale Security Features Timescale offers several advanced security features:

  • : Always encrypted, both in transit and at rest

  • : Access strictly over TLS-encrypted connections

  • Maintenance access protocol: Rigorous protocol with audit logging

  • : Compliant with regulations such as GDPR

  • : Available for Enterprise Tier customers

  • : Isolated access without public internet exposure

We've explored essential best practices for securing PostgreSQL databases:

  • Configuring Unix Domain Sockets and TCP/IP protocols

  • Implementing role-based access control

  • Enabling row-level security

  • Utilizing SSL and pgcrypto for encryption

Timescale further extends PostgreSQL's security framework with advanced features that ensure comprehensive protection for time-series data.

With these security measures, you can create a robust security posture that protects your valuable data against unauthorized access and potential breaches.

What security practices do you implement for your PostgreSQL databases? Share your experiences in the comments!

items.title: আপনি একজন প্রকৃত সফটওয়্যার ইঞ্জিনিয়ার হিসেবে সবসময় সঠিকভাবে নিজেকে প্রস্তুত রাখতে পারবেন
items.author: Md Tanvir Ahamed Shanto
items.content:
🚀 সফটওয়্যার ডেভেলপমেন্টের মৌলিক বিষয়সমূহ

নতুন নতুন টেকনোলজি (React, Django, Flutter) আসবে-যাবে, কিন্তু কিছু মৌলিক বিষয় কখনো পুরোনো হবে না। এগুলো ভালোভাবে জানা থাকলে, আপনি সবসময় দক্ষ সফটওয়্যার ইঞ্জিনিয়ার হিসেবে নিজেকে প্রস্তুত রাখতে পারবেন।

🎯 যে বিষয়গুলো প্রতিটি ডেভেলপারের জানা উচিত:

1️⃣ Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA)

📌 কেন? 👉 সমস্যার সমাধানে কার্যকরী ও দক্ষ পদ্ধতি খুঁজে বের করতে DSA অপরিহার্য।

Arrays, Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues

Trees (Binary Tree, BST), Graphs

Sorting & Searching Algorithms (Quick Sort, Merge Sort, Binary Search)

Dynamic Programming, Greedy Algorithms

📌 কার্যকরী ক্ষেত্র: 🔹 Performance optimization, code efficiency & scalability

2️⃣ Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)

📌 কেন? 👉 বেশিরভাগ প্রোগ্রামিং ল্যাঙ্গুয়েজে OOP কনসেপ্ট ব্যবহার হয় (Java, Python, C++, etc.)

Abstraction, Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism

Design Patterns (Factory, Singleton, Observer)

📌 কেন? 👉 কোডের পুনর্ব্যবহারযোগ্যতা, মডুলারিটি ও maintainability বাড়াতে।

Creational Patterns (Factory, Builder)

Structural Patterns (Adapter, Composite)

Behavioral Patterns (Observer, Strategy)

4️⃣ Software Architecture

📌 কেন? 👉 স্কেলিং এবং পারফরম্যান্স সমস্যা এড়াতে সঠিক আর্কিটেকচার ডিজাইন অপরিহার্য।

Monolithic vs Microservices

Client-Server Architecture

MVC, MVVM, Clean Architecture

Distributed Systems & Scalability

📌 কেন? 👉 ডেটা ম্যানেজমেন্ট প্রতিটি সফটওয়্যার সিস্টেমের মেরুদণ্ড।

Transactions & ACID Properties

Caching Mechanisms (Redis, Memcached)

📌 কেন? 👉 ইন্টারনেটে বা ক্লাউডে কাজ করার সময় নেটওয়ার্ক কনসেপ্ট জানা দরকার।

HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, WebSockets

Authentication Protocols (OAuth, JWT)

7️⃣ Version Control (Git)

📌 কেন? 👉 টিমের সাথে কাজ করার সময় কোড ম্যানেজমেন্ট ও ট্র্যাকিং করার জন্য অপরিহার্য।

Git Basics (Clone, Commit, Push, Pull)

📌 কেন? 👉 বাগমুক্ত ও reliable সফটওয়্যার নিশ্চিত করতে।

Unit Testing, Integration Testing

Automation Testing Frameworks (Selenium, Cypress)

9️⃣ Command Line & Shell Scripting

📌 কেন? 👉 ডেভেলপমেন্ট ও ডিপ্লয়মেন্টের কাজ দ্রুত করার জন্য।

🔟 Cloud & Deployment Basics

📌 কেন? 👉 সফটওয়্যার তৈরি করে সেটি প্রোডাকশনে ডিপ্লয় করা অপরিহার্য।

Cloud Platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP)

📌 কেন? 👉 ব্যবহারকারীর ডেটা সুরক্ষিত রাখতে ও সিস্টেম হ্যাকিং থেকে রক্ষা করতে।

Authentication & Authorization

XSS, CSRF, SQL Injection প্রতিরোধ

1️⃣2️⃣ Problem-Solving Skills

📌 কেন? 👉 সফটওয়্যার ডেভেলপমেন্ট মানেই সমস্যা সমাধান করা।

Breaking down complex problems

📌 কেন? 👉 সফটওয়্যার ডেভেলপমেন্টে টিমওয়ার্ক ও কমিউনিকেশন খুবই গুরুত্বপূর্ণ।

প্রকৃত সফটওয়্যার ইঞ্জিনিয়ার হওয়া মানে শুধুমাত্র একটি টুল বা ফ্রেমওয়ার্ক জানা নয়। বরং সমস্যার সমাধানের দক্ষতা ও নতুন টেকনোলজি শেখার মানসিকতাই আপনাকে সফল করে তুলবে।

💬 আপনার কী মনে হয়? কোনো পয়েন্ট মিস করেছি? কমেন্টে জানাবেন!

🔗 শেয়ার করুন এবং অন্যদের সাহায্য করুন! 🚀

items.title: 🎉 I just hit 100 WPM 🎊
items.author: Shrijal Acharya
items.content:
As the title suggests, I just hit . 😎

This has been a long, long journey. I still remember the first time I ran a typing test, it was around (spear typing, of course!). That wasn’t even that long ago, maybe just three years back.

I always wanted to improve my typing, but touch typing just didn’t seem like my thing (having smaller hands doesn’t help 😭).

Enough is enough, for a month, I forced myself to keep my hands on the , slowly building the habit. It was just terrible, but eventually I got used to it.

Anyone who has struggled with touch typing will understand how hard it is to get used to using their pinky fingers for , , and . And let’s not even talk about numbers, I was still spear typing them just 6 months back! 🫠

For new touch typists, here were my stats a few months ago for inspiration.

Share your in the comments! ✌️

items.title: Kế hoạch Du lịch Úc Tự Túc Hoàn Hảo Nhất 2025
items.author: Tài chính Nguyễn Lê
items.content:
Chào mừng bạn đến với hành trình khám phá Úc – xứ sở chuột túi, nơi hội tụ vẻ đẹp thiên nhiên hoang dã và các thành phố hiện đại. Dưới đây là hướng dẫn toàn diện giúp bạn lên kế hoạch cho chuyến du lịch Úc tự túc một cách dễ dàng, từ việc chọn thời gian lý tưởng, phương tiện di chuyển, đến những địa điểm không thể bỏ qua và mẹo tiết kiệm chi phí.

1. Thông Tin Cơ Bản Về Úc

Úc là quốc gia lớn nhất châu Đại Dương với diện tích hơn 7,6 triệu km², chia thành 6 bang và 2 vùng lãnh thổ. Mỗi nơi có nền văn hóa, khí hậu và địa hình khác biệt. Thủ đô Canberra nằm giữa hai thành phố lớn Sydney và Melbourne. Úc có ba múi giờ, điều này sẽ ảnh hưởng đến lịch trình di chuyển của bạn, vì vậy hãy sắp xếp thời gian phù hợp.

2. Thời Điểm Lý Tưởng Để Du Lịch Úc

Úc có 4 mùa rõ rệt, với sự khác biệt khí hậu giữa các khu vực. Mùa xuân (tháng 9 - 11) và mùa thu (tháng 3 - 5) là thời điểm lý tưởng nhất, khi thời tiết dễ chịu và không quá đông đúc. Mùa hè (tháng 12 - 2) thích hợp cho những ai yêu biển, nhưng là mùa cao điểm với giá cả tăng. Mùa đông (tháng 6 - 8) lý tưởng cho hoạt động trượt tuyết ở các khu nghỉ dưỡng miền Nam. Chọn thời gian du lịch phụ thuộc vào sở thích cá nhân và các hoạt động bạn muốn tham gia.

Có nhiều chuyến bay thẳng từ Việt Nam đến các thành phố lớn của Úc như Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane. Đặt vé trước để có giá tốt và thuận tiện cho lịch trình của bạn.

Úc có hệ thống giao thông công cộng phát triển với xe buýt, tàu điện ngầm và xe lửa. Tuy nhiên, khoảng cách giữa các thành phố lớn có thể khá xa, nên bạn có thể cần di chuyển bằng máy bay. Lưu ý rằng giao thông ở Úc đi bên trái, và bạn cần tìm hiểu kỹ về giao thông công cộng tại địa phương.

4. Những Địa Điểm Du Lịch Không Thể Bỏ Qua

Nhà hát Opera là biểu tượng của Sydney và nước Úc, thu hút hàng triệu du khách mỗi năm. Bạn có thể tham quan khu vực bên ngoài hoặc mua vé tham dự các buổi biểu diễn.

Một công trình kiến trúc nổi tiếng khác, cầu cảng Sydney cho bạn tầm nhìn tuyệt đẹp ra toàn cảnh thành phố. Du khách có thể tham gia leo lên cầu hoặc đi xe buýt qua cầu.

Công Viên Quốc Gia Uluru-Kata Tjuta

Uluru là khối đá sa thạch khổng lồ, biểu tượng văn hóa của người bản địa. Đây là điểm đến linh thiêng, du khách không được phép leo lên Uluru để tôn trọng văn hóa địa phương.

Vườn Quốc Gia Blue Mountains

Nổi bật với những vách đá hùng vĩ và thác nước, Blue Mountains mang đến cảnh quan tuyệt đẹp và cơ hội khám phá thiên nhiên hoang dã.

Ngoài ra, Úc còn rất nhiều địa điểm hấp dẫn khác như Great Ocean Road, Đảo Kangaroo, và Bờ Biển Vàng (Gold Coast). Tùy vào thời gian và sở thích, bạn có thể lên kế hoạch khám phá thêm các điểm đến độc đáo khác.

5. Ẩm Thực Úc – Những Món Ngon Nên Thử

Ẩm thực Úc là sự pha trộn giữa các nền văn hóa khác nhau. Một số món ăn đặc trưng bạn nên thử là thịt Kangaroo, gà Parmigiana (biến tấu từ Ý), bánh Pavlova, và các loại hải sản tươi ngon. Bạn sẽ tìm thấy sự phong phú của ẩm thực tại các nhà hàng và quán ăn địa phương.

6. Cách Tiết Kiệm Chi Phí Khi Du Lịch Úc

Giá vé máy bay phụ thuộc vào thời điểm bạn đặt vé và hãng hàng không. Để tiết kiệm, bạn nên đặt vé trước và theo dõi các chương trình khuyến mãi.

Phí xin visa Úc là khoản chi cố định. Hãy nộp hồ sơ sớm để có thời gian xử lý và tránh sai sót trong quá trình xin visa. Trong quá trình xin visa cần đảm bảo các thủ tục đầy đủ và chi tiết, khó khăn nhất là chứng minh tài chính với sổ tiết kiệm có số dư cao. Nếu bạn có bất kỳ khó khăn nào trong thu nhập bản thân hãy liên hệ đến dịch vụ chứng minh tài chính du lịch Úc tại https://taichinhnguyenle.com.vn/dich-vu-chung-minh-tai-chinh-du-lich-uc.html để được tư vấn và tìm giải pháp cho khách hàng với chi phí siêu rẻ.

Chi Phí Khách Sạn và Ăn Uống

Khách sạn và ăn uống là một phần lớn trong chi phí. Để tiết kiệm, bạn có thể chọn ở hostel, Airbnb hoặc khách sạn bình dân. Tự nấu ăn cũng là cách tiết kiệm, đặc biệt nếu bạn ở trong các căn hộ có nhà bếp.

Sử dụng các phương tiện công cộng như xe buýt và tàu điện ngầm thay vì taxi sẽ giúp bạn tiết kiệm. Bạn cũng có thể thuê xe để di chuyển giữa các điểm du lịch, đặc biệt nếu đi theo nhóm.

Ngoài ra, bạn nên dự trù thêm chi phí cho các hoạt động tham quan, mua sắm, và phí vào cửa tại các địa điểm du lịch. Hãy dự phòng một khoản để đảm bảo không bị động khi cần chi trả.

Hy vọng với kế hoạch du lịch Úc này, bạn sẽ có một chuyến đi đáng nhớ! Chúc bạn có một hành trình tuyệt vời khám phá xứ sở chuột túi!

items.title: Understanding SAML Authentication: Key Concepts and Differences Between SP and IdP Initiated Flows
items.author: Dushmanta
items.content:
Most enterprises work with different services like Slack, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, and Google Workspace daily. So, have you ever wondered how these enterprises provide access to all these services in one dashboard for their employees? Or imagine you have an enterprise and want to give your employees access to these platforms.

Since these services are independent, they would typically require separate login credentials. However, managing different usernames and passwords for each service would be an overhead and not an efficient solution. Can you imagine handling 10 different login credentials if there are 10 different services? This not only creates unnecessary complexity but also becomes a bottleneck in productivity and security management.

This is where SAML comes into the picture. SAML authentication solves this overhead by enabling seamless access to multiple services with a single set of credentials (the credentials of their own organization / identity provider). In this article, let’s learn about SAML, how it works, and the important terms related to SAML authentication that you need to know.

SAML, or Security Assertion Markup Language, is an open standard based on XML (Extensible Markup Language) used for authentication and sharing identity data. SAML transmits authentication and identity data using an XML data structure over HTTP or SOAP methods. This data is exchanged between an organization’s and a —which we will discuss next.

In SAML authentication, the is the entity that provides identity information about the user who needs authentication. The IdP can be your organization itself, or if you're using a third-party service like Google or Okta, then it will act as the IdP.

The is the entity that offers services to the organization and its users. For example, Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace act as Service Providers for organizations integrating with them. Any platform that provides a service and support SAML authentication can be considered an SP.

For example, imagine you have an organization let’s say and you want to offer health benefits to employees through a health benefits provider let’s say Your employees need to log in to Healthy Inc.'s portal to access their benefits. If you establish SAML authentication, acts as the , providing identity data for employees, while acts as the , granting access based on that authentication.

Establishing Trust Between IdP and SP

SAML authentication occurs between an and a . Before the authentication process begins, both entities must establish a trust relationship. This is achieved by exchanging metadata, specifying endpoints, defining connection methods, and sharing signing and encryption certificates based on Public Key Infrastructure (PKI).

Let’s explore some key terms required for trust establishment.

ACS URL (Assertion Consumer Service URL):

The is provided by the and serves as the endpoint where users are redirected to complete authentication and access the service. Typically, SAML authentication requests to this endpoint are made via a .

IdP Single-Sign On (SSO) / Login URL:

The is provided by the to the and serves as the endpoint that receives the SAML authentication request from the . This URL is where users are redirected when they attempt to log in via SAML authentication to the using , allowing the to obtain authentication data from the .

Audience URI / Entity ID:

The is provided by the to the and serves as a unique identifier representing the . This indicates who the assertion is intended for, and it is validated by the . Typically, the is included in the SAML response data.

The is a parameter provided by the to the . It is a URL or identifier used to maintain the user's state. The Relay State determines where to redirect the user after successful SAML authentication. Since the SP doesn't know the user's state before the SAML authentication login, the Relay State holds a value that guides where the user is redirected after successful authentication. In most cases, the Relay State is a URL where the user will be redirected.

A (Public Key Infrastructure) is used to secure the SAML authentication process through encryption and signature.

  • Both the and generate their own and share their public keys with each other.

  • These keys are used to sign the requests and responses with private keys, and they can be verified using the shared public keys. This ensures that the requests or responses are legitimate and have not been tampered with.

These above configuration are used to establish trust between the and .

Next, let’s explore how works.

  1. The user tries to access a service (

    ) like

  2. SP Redirects User to IdP SSO URL

    The generates a SAML Authentication Request and redirects the user to the .

  3. User Authenticates with IdP ( in this example).

  4. IdP Generates SAML Response

    Upon successful authentication, the

    creates a , including a (more about and are discussed below) containing the user’s identity data. Then the response is digitally signed with the IdP’s private key to ensure authenticity.

  5. redirects the user back to the SP’s ACS (Assertion Consumer Service) URL with the , including the , via an .

  6. SP Validates the SAML Response:

* Verifies the **digital signature** using the IdP’s **public key** (which was provided during trust establishment).

* Ensures that the response is valid and hasn’t been tampered with.
  1. After successful verification, the redirects the user to the (provided in the initial request). This ensures the user lands on the correct page after authentication.

Here’s an image illustrating SAML authentication flow:

SAML Response and Assertion

A is an XML-based authentication message sent by the Identity Provider (IdP) to the Service Provider (SP). It contains the , which holds user identity data, attributes, and other important information such as the signature, issuer, destination, and timestamp.

What is a SAML Assertion?

A is the core part of the SAML Response, as it carries the user’s identity information to the . The SP uses this assertion to and to the requested service.

Before sending the , the IdP signs it using its private key. The response includes:

  • (ensuring data integrity)

  • (confirming authenticity)

  • (used for validation)

Additionally, the SAML Assertion itself can also be signed with the same key. This means:

  • Both the and the can be signed separately.

  • The digest value, signature, and X.509 certificate are included in both if needed.

For , it is recommended to sign both the SAML Assertion and the SAML Response to prevent tampering.

Structure of SAML Response

A typical SAML response contains the following data:

  1. Issuer → Identifies which IdP issued the SAML Response.

  2. Status → Indicates whether the authentication .

  3. Assertions → Contains authentication, attribute, and assertion authorization details.

  4. Signature & Encryption → Contains the signature data for the whole SAML response.

SP-Initiated vs. IdP-Initiated Flow

Now that we understand how SAML works, it's important to know that there are two types of authentication flows in which SAML can be implemented:

  1. → The user starts at the .

  2. → The user starts at the .

The is the SAML authentication flow. The SAML authentication process we discussed earlier follows this flow, where the user starts from the Service Provider's login page.

  1. The user tries to log in from the SP (e.g., Healthy login page).

  2. The to the , sending a SAML Authentication Request.

  3. The IdP authenticates the user.

  4. The IdP generates a SAML Response and (via ACS URL).

  5. The SP validates the response and logs the user in.

In an , the authentication request starts from the SP, and the SAML Authentication Request is sent from the SP to the IdP.

Here’s an image illustrating the SP-Initiated SAML authentication flow:

The is but is often used in , where employees and access multiple services from a (Single Sign-On).

  1. The user logs in directly at the IdP (e.g., a corporate dashboard).

  2. When the user wants to access a specific service, the IdP generates a SAML Response and sends it (without a request).

  3. The SP validates the response and logs the .

In an , there is no authentication request sent from the SP. The IdP directly initiates the authentication process and sends the .

Here’s an image illustrating the IdP-Initiated SAML authentication flow:

Here are some key benefits of using SAML authentication:

  • Eliminates the need for multiple passwords, reducing the risk of phishing and credential theft.

  • Users only need to log in once to access multiple service providers, reducing password fatigue.

  • Users don’t have to remember multiple credentials, leading to faster and smoother access.

  • Enterprises can use their own IdP to manage authentication securely across various platforms.

  • Service providers don’t need to store or manage user credentials in their database, reducing security risks and compliance overhead.

How SAML is Different from Oauth?

Now that we've learned about SAML, you might wonder how it differs from OAuth and whether OAuth can be used for login to multiple service providers instead.

Both and are open standards, but they serve different purposes:

  • is primarily used for , verifying a user’s identity.

  • is used for , granting access to resources without exposing login credentials.

: Let’s say you want to log in to multiple services—Microsoft 365, Slack, and Salesforce—using instead of SAML. Here’s what happens:

  1. You visit and choose to log in with Google.

  2. Google redirects you to an asking for permission.

  3. Once you grant permission, receives an and logs you in.

Now, if you want to log in to more service providers, you have to for each provider separately. Not to mention, your since you’re using Google OAuth. This becomes difficult for enterprises that have their own Identity Provider (IdP).

Now, if we use ,

  • We can integrate multiple service providers with a single .

  • Users can and seamlessly access all service providers from through SAML authentication.

As we learned above, SAML authentication is a secure way to access multiple independent services. It helps enterprises provide access to multiple services from their IdP. It enables seamless SSO, reduces security risks, and simplifies identity management. It’s also worth mentioning that SAML is not limited to enterprises—it can be used by any organization or system that requires secure and efficient authentication across multiple service providers.

I hope this article helped you understand the key concepts of SAML, its different authentication flows, and its benefits. Please feel free to comment if you have any doubts or reach out to me. Also, let me know if I’ve made any mistakes in the article—I’d love to learn and improve! Happy coding :)

items.title: Looking for collaboration
items.author: Samuel Parker
items.content:
Seeking Tech Co-Founder for AI-Powered Food & Community Platform

I’m building an innovative platform that combines AI, food, and community—designed to connect, educate, and empower people through interactive experiences. The project is in its early stages, and I’m looking for a tech co-founder to join me in bringing this vision to life.

The goal is to create a dynamic, community-driven platform that evolves with the input of its users. The core experience will revolve around an AI-powered chatbot that feels like a real chef guiding users through cooking, meal planning, and providing personalized food advice. But it doesn’t stop there—the platform will grow into a collaborative space for people to share recipes, music, self-care tips, and more.

This platform will be built on a community-first mindset, where users contribute based on the value they receive. The goal is to create an inclusive, ethical environment where members can learn, grow, and support each other. Ultimately, this project is about building something greater than just a tool—it’s a movement.

AI Chatbot Development: A chatbot using AI tools like ChatGPT that offers personalized recipe suggestions, meal guidance, and food-related tips.

Voice Integration: Voice-powered interactions where the chatbot guides users through cooking processes in a more immersive way.

Website Framework: Build a scalable, user-friendly website that can support the growth of both the platform and its community.

Community Features: Lay the groundwork for a socially interactive community where users can contribute content, interact, and help shape the platform's development.

I need someone who is passionate about both tech and community—someone who can help shape the platform's tech, but also be deeply involved in the collaborative, ethical nature of the project. This is not a “hire and done” gig; it's a co-founder role with an opportunity to grow, innovate, and build something impactful.

If you’re someone who believes in the power of collaboration, creativity, and community, and you want to be part of something special, I’d love to hear from you!

Reach out, and let's discuss how we can turn this vision into reality together.

items.title: Using Clang++ and Make natively on Windows
items.author: Martin Licht
items.content:
• You find several links to different packages of MinGW-w64 bundled together with compilers and additional software.

    This guide focuses on the LLVM package, , which provides the LLVM compilers and also comes bundled with and (Version >= 3.9).

  • We need to understand which version we want to download. A brief explanation is found on the project homepage on Github:

    We want to work on Windows directly, so we look for Zip-files called

    llvm-mingw-<version>-<crt>-<.zip
    1. - A running number that simply indicates the version of the package
    2. - The C runtime library required, which is either or . The former is the MSV C runtime, which is available on all versions of Windows but has less features. The latter is the Universal C runtime, which is pre-installed from Windows 10 onwards. Please note that the Zip-file will not provide that runtime library, you need to make sure that your Windows installation already provides it. For that reason, we will pick the MSV C runtime.
    3. - This indicates the processor architecture, which is one of the following: - , which is the 32-bit architecture of Intel - , which is the 64-bit architecture of Intel - , which is the 32-bit architecture of ARM - , which is the 64-bit architecture of ARM All the possible packages contain binaries to compile any of those architectures but these binaries only run the specified architecture. For that reason, you need look up which processor is being used on your machine and pick the appropriate package.

    Let us suppose from now on that we have picked 64-bit Intel CPU and, just for compatibility reasons, use the MSV C runtime. The version will be . You adapt the following lines according to your choice of package, and likely your version number will be more advanced.

    Whatever package you choose, download the Zip file.

items.title: Histoire des Fonctions et Instruments Trigonométriques
items.author: Ben
items.content:
Les fonctions trigonométriques jouent un rôle crucial en mathématiques, en physique, en ingénierie et dans de nombreuses autres disciplines. Leur histoire est marquée par des découvertes progressives dans les civilisations anciennes, qui ont cherché à comprendre les relations entre les angles et les longueurs des côtés dans les triangles. Cet article explore l'évolution des fonctions trigonométriques et des instruments associés à leur étude.

1. Les Origines de la Trigonométrie

1.1. La Trigonométrie dans l'Antiquité

Les premières traces de trigonométrie remontent à l'Antiquité, où elle était utilisée principalement en astronomie et en géométrie. Les Babyloniens, les Égyptiens et les Grecs ont contribué à l'élaboration des concepts trigonométriques. Par exemple, les Babyloniens utilisaient des tables de rapports pour calculer les longueurs des côtés des triangles dans leurs observations astronomiques.

Les Grecs anciens, comme Hipparque et Ptolémée, ont été des pionniers dans l'application des tables trigonométriques pour l'astronomie. Hipparque est reconnu pour avoir compilé la première table trigonométrique en utilisant des cordes, qui étaient essentielles pour la mesure des angles dans les cercles.

2. L’Évolution des Fonctions Trigonométriques

2.1. Le Développement de la Trigonométrie Indienne

Au Moyen Âge, la trigonométrie a fait des avancées majeures en Inde, grâce aux travaux de mathématiciens comme Aryabhata et Brahmagupta. Aryabhata a introduit le concept de sinus (sinus en latin) et a utilisé des valeurs trigonométriques pour calculer les positions des corps célestes. La notion de "jya" (l'ancêtre du sinus) est née dans ces travaux.

2.2. Les Contributions Arabes

Les mathématiciens arabes, tels qu'Al-Battani et Al-Khwarizmi, ont joué un rôle clé dans la transmission des connaissances trigonométriques. Al-Battani a amélioré les tables trigonométriques et a introduit la notion de cotangente, de secante et de cosecante. L'usage des tables trigonométriques s'est alors étendu, avec des calculs plus précis pour l'astronomie et la navigation.

2.3. La Trigonométrie au Moyen Âge Européen

En Europe, la trigonométrie a été redécouverte à la Renaissance, notamment par des mathématiciens comme Regiomontanus et Copernic. Les tables trigonométriques sont devenues plus accessibles, et l'usage de la trigonométrie s'est intensifié pour la cartographie, la navigation et l'étude du mouvement des planètes.

3. Les Instruments Trigonométriques

L'un des premiers instruments trigonométriques était la , utilisée par les Grecs et les Romains pour mesurer les angles dans les cercles. La corde était un instrument essentiel pour déterminer les longueurs des côtés d'un triangle en fonction de l'angle étudié.

Le est un instrument de navigation qui utilise les principes trigonométriques pour mesurer l'angle entr

items.title: Design Patterns in Laravel
items.author: John Yu
items.content:
If you want to code like a pro in Laravel, understanding design patterns is a game-changer. Laravel itself is built on various design patterns, and using them correctly can make your applications more scalable, maintainable, and efficient. Here are some essential Laravel design patterns you need to know:

  1. Singleton Pattern 📌 Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access. Use case: When you need a single shared instance, like a logging service, database connection, or configuration settings.

✅ Example (Service Binding in Laravel)

// AppServiceProvider.php public function register() $this->app->singleton('App\Services\PaymentGateway', function ($app) {

return new PaymentGateway();

}); Why? This ensures that PaymentGateway is only instantiated once and reused throughout the app.

Refactoring Guru — Singleton in PHP

Elevating Laravel Development with the Signleton Design Pattern

How to use Laravel’s bind and signleton methods

  1. Repository Pattern 📌 Decouples database logic from business logic. Use case: Makes it easier to switch between databases or mock data for testing.

✅ Example (Using a Repository in Laravel)

// Interface interface UserRepositoryInterface {

public function getAllUsers();

}

// Repository Implementation

class UserRepository implements UserRepositoryInterface {

public function getAllUsers() {

return User::all(); }

// Binding in AppServiceProvider

$this->app->bind(UserRepositoryInterface::class, UserRepository::class);

Why? Your controllers only depend on UserRepositoryInterface, so you can swap implementations without modifying business logic.

  1. Factory Pattern 📌 Creates objects without specifying the exact class to instantiate. Use case: When you need dynamic object creation, such as generating test data or creating models with varying attributes.

✅ Example (Laravel Model Factories)

// Define in database/factories/UserFactory.php use Illuminate\Database\Eloquent\Factories\Factory;

class UserFactory extends Factory {

protected $model = User::class;

public function definition() {

return [

'name' => $this->faker->name(),

'email' => $this->faker->unique()->safeEmail(),

'password' => bcrypt('password')

]; }

// Use in tests or seeders

User::factory()->count(10)->create();

Why? Automates object creation for testing and seeding without manually writing insert queries.

  1. Strategy Pattern 📌 Defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. Use case: Payment gateways, authentication strategies, or data formatting.

✅ Example (Payment Strategy in Laravel)

interface PaymentStrategy { public function pay($amount); class PayPalPayment implements PaymentStrategy {

public function pay($amount) {

return "Paid $amount via PayPal"; }

class StripePayment implements PaymentStrategy {

public function pay($amount) {

return "Paid $amount via Stripe";

} // Using the strategy

function processPayment(PaymentStrategy $paymentMethod, $amount) {

return $paymentMethod->pay($amount); echo processPayment(new PayPalPayment(), 100);

Why? Allows switching payment providers without modifying core payment logic.

  1. Observer Pattern 📌 Allows objects to be notified when another object’s state changes. Use case: Email notifications, logging, updating user stats.

✅ Example (Laravel Event Listeners)

// Event: UserRegistered class UserRegistered { public function __construct(User $user) { } // Listener: SendWelcomeEmail public function handle(UserRegistered $event) {

Mail::to($event->user->email)->send(new WelcomeEmail());

} // Fire Event

event(new UserRegistered($user));

Why? Decouples the event trigger from the response (e.g., sending an email, logging activity).

  1. Decorator Pattern 📌 Dynamically adds behavior to objects without modifying their class. Use case: Wrapping existing functionality with extra features, like adding logging to a service.

✅ Example (Using Middleware in Laravel)

// LoggingMiddleware.php class LoggingMiddleware {

public function handle($request, Closure $next) {

Log::info("Request made to: " . $request->url()); } Why? Keeps code modular and applies behavior dynamically without modifying the original request handling.

  1. Facade Pattern 📌 Provides a static interface to a class while maintaining flexibility. Use case: Simplifies access to complex subsystems, like caching, authentication, or logging.

✅ Example (Laravel Facade Usage)

use Illuminate\Support\Facades\Cache; // Store a value

Cache::put('key', 'value', 3600);

// Retrieve value

$value = Cache::get('key');

Why? Facades make complex functionality easily accessible while keeping the underlying implementation flexible.

  1. Command Pattern 📌 Encapsulates a request as an object, allowing for queuing, logging, and undo mechanisms. Use case: Queue jobs, scheduled tasks, and artisan commands.

✅ Example (Laravel Queue Jobs)

class SendEmailJob implements ShouldQueue { public function handle() {user@example.com')->send(new WelcomeEmail()); } // Dispatch Job

dispatch(new SendEmailJob());

Why? Helps structure background tasks and make them manageable.

Conclusion Using design patterns in Laravel improves code quality, maintainability, and scalability. Mastering these patterns helps you write professional-grade applications that are modular, testable, and efficient.

🚀 Which pattern do you find most useful in your projects?

items.title: Migrando do esbuild para o rspack em Funções AWS Lambda: Guia Prático com AWS SAM e Makefile
items.author: Daniel Santos
items.content:
Nos últimos anos, o ecossistema de ferramentas para desenvolvimento em JavaScript e TypeScript tem evoluído rapidamente, oferecendo opções cada vez mais eficientes para construir e otimizar aplicações. Uma dessas ferramentas que tem ganhado destaque é o , um bundler moderno baseado em Rust que combina desempenho excepcional com compatibilidade com o Webpack, permitindo aproveitar plugins e configurações já conhecidas no mercado.

Recentemente, ao trabalhar em uma função AWS Lambda utilizando o AWS SAM, decidi migrar o processo de build do para o . Essa mudança não foi motivada por limitações do , que continua sendo uma excelente escolha, mas sim pela busca por padronização e flexibilidade em meus projetos.

Neste tutorial, compartilho o passo a passo dessa migração, desde a instalação do até a configuração do AWS SAM para utilizar um Makefile personalizado. O objetivo é fornecer um guia prático para quem deseja explorar essa ferramenta em seus próprios projetos, seja para funções Lambda ou outras aplicações baseadas em Node.js.

Substituindo o esbuild pelo rspack

A primeira etapa da migração é substituir o pelo no projeto

yarn add  @rspack/cli @rspack/core terser-webpack-plugin
yarn remove esbuild

Para facilitar o processo de build, adicionaremos um script ao . Esse script será responsável por executar o comando de build do sempre que necessário:

Com isso, você poderá rodar para gerar o bundle da função Lambda diretamente a partir do terminal, simplificando o fluxo de trabalho.

O próximo passo é criar o arquivo de configuração do , o . Esse arquivo define como o código será empacotado, incluindo entradas, saídas, regras de processamento e otimizações.

No arquivo do AWS SAM, precisamos ajustar a configuração da função Lambda para usar um Makefile personalizado em vez do . Isso nos dá maior controle sobre o processo de build.

  HelloWorldFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: app.lambdaHandler
      Runtime: nodejs20.x
      Architectures:
      - x86_64
      Events:
        HelloWorld:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /increment
            Method: post
    Metadata:

Finalmente, criamos um para definir o processo de build. O Makefile é uma ferramenta que permite automatizar tarefas, como instalação de dependências, execução de scripts e cópia de arquivos.


    yarn  yarn build
    dist/app.js ARTIFACTS_DIR

items.title: Shift Left Testing: A Comprehensive Guide for QA Test Engineers
items.author: dheeraj pd
items.content:
Shift Left Testing is a modern approach to software development that emphasizes moving testing activities earlier in the software development lifecycle (SDLC). This strategy aims to identify and fix defects sooner, improving overall software quality and reducing costs. By integrating testing into the early stages of development, QA engineers can play a crucial role in ensuring that issues are detected and resolved before they become costly and complex.

Key Principles of Shift Left Testing

  1. Early Involvement of Testers: Testers should participate in requirement reviews and design discussions to identify ambiguities and potential issues.
  2. : Automating unit, integration, and regression tests ensures quick feedback for developers and reduces manual effort.
  3. : Leveraging CI/CD pipelines to integrate and test code frequently allows for immediate detection and resolution of defects.
  4. Collaboration Across Teams: Developers, testers, and business stakeholders must work together to ensure a unified understanding of quality goals.

Benefits of Shift Left Testing

  • : Identifying defects early in the lifecycle is significantly cheaper than fixing them post-production.
  • : Resolving issues early minimizes delays and accelerates delivery timelines.
  • : Testing requirements, design, and code early ensures that quality is built into the product from the start.
  • : Shift-left testing encourages cross-functional teams to work together, breaking down silos and improving communication.

Practical Steps to Implement Shift Left Testing

  1. Adopt a Test-First Mentality: Incorporate practices like Test-Driven Development (TDD) or Behavior-Driven Development (BDD).
  2. Integrate Testing into the Development Workflow: Use tools like Jenkins, GitLab CI, or GitHub Actions to automate testing as part of your CI/CD pipeline.
  3. : Run static code analysis and unit tests as soon as code is written to catch defects early.
  4. : Provide training to developers and testers on modern testing tools and practices.
  5. Invest in Collaboration Tools: Utilize platforms like Jira or Azure DevOps to facilitate communication and track quality metrics.

Types of Shift Left Testing

  1. Traditional Shift-Left Testing: Involves starting testing activities during the requirements and design phases.
  2. Incremental Shift-Left Testing: Testing is performed incrementally as each new feature or module is developed.
  3. Agile/DevOps Shift-Left Testing: Integrates testing into Agile and DevOps practices, emphasizing continuous integration and delivery.
  4. Model-Based Shift-Left Testing: Uses models to design and execute tests, ensuring consistency and coverage.

Challenges and How to Overcome Them

  • : Address cultural barriers by fostering a mindset of open communication, collaboration, and shared responsibility for quality across teams.
  • : Provide training and resources to ensure all team members understand the value of shift left and are equipped with the necessary skills.
  • : Achieve equilibrium between speed, agility, and security by integrating security practices into development and leveraging automation to streamline testing.

Best Practices for Shift-Left Testing in Agile

  1. Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery (CI/CD): Ensure that testing is integrated into the CI/CD pipeline to provide immediate feedback.
  2. : Use automated testing tools to cover various types of checks, such as unit, integration, and performance-related tests.
  3. : Develop a collaborative culture where development, QA, and operations teams work closely together.
  4. : Practice incremental rollouts of new features using techniques like canary releases to test in a real-world environment with minimal risk.
  5. Documentation and Knowledge Sharing: Maintain thorough documentation of testing practices and findings to promote knowledge-sharing across teams.

Shift Left Testing is a powerful approach that can significantly enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of software development. By integrating testing early and continuously, QA engineers can play a pivotal role in delivering high-quality software quickly and cost-effectively. Embracing this approach not only improves the quality of the final product but also fosters a collaborative and proactive development culture.
