items.title: State Management in React: Context API vs. Redux
items.author: Erasmus Kotoka
items.content:
What do you think about this?

State management is crucial for building scalable React applications.

But should you go with Context API or Redux? Let’s break it down.

✔ Built-in – No extra libraries required.

✔ Lightweight – Ideal for small to medium apps.

& .

⚠ Not optimized for frequent updates – Can cause unnecessary re-renders.

⚠ No built-in dev tools – Lacks debugging support like Redux.

🔹 Redux ✔ Centralized store – Keeps all states in one place.

✔ Predictable state updates – Uses actions & reducers.

✔ Powerful debugging – Redux DevTools makes tracking state changes easy.

⚠ Boilerplate-heavy – Requires actions, reducers, and middleware.

⚠ Overkill for small apps – Can add unnecessary complexity.

🚀 When to use what?

🔸 Use Context API for lightweight state management (theme, user authentication).

🔸 Use Redux for complex apps with deeply nested state and multiple data sources.

Both have their strengths—choosing the right one depends on your project needs!

💡 What’s your go-to state management solution? Let’s discuss! 👇

items.title: Apache Spark: Revolutionizing Big Data with Sustainable Open Source Funding
items.author: Rachel Duncan
items.content:
Apache Spark has transformed how businesses, researchers, and developers handle big data analytics. In this blog post, we take a closer look at how Apache Spark’s sustainable open source funding and transparent business model have driven its success. Drawing from an in-depth article on Apache Spark’s open source business model, funding, and community, we explore the evolution, governance, and technical merits behind this innovative data processing engine.

Apache Spark, originally born at UC Berkeley’s AMP Lab, is now a globally recognized engine that accelerates data processing and real-time analytics. Managed by the Apache Software Foundation, Spark’s longevity is fueled by its robust community contributions, corporate sponsorships, and adherence to the Apache 2.0 license. This blend of community spirit and corporate backing has positioned Apache Spark as a powerful tool in industries ranging from finance and e-commerce to healthcare and social media analytics. At the heart of Apache Spark’s success is its open source business model. Relying on a mix of corporate sponsorships, individual donations, and volunteer contributions, Spark has established a funding ecosystem that supports continuous innovation while remaining accessible to both developers and enterprises. In our discussion, we also touch upon alternative funding paradigms that have emerged, such as blockchain-based methods. For more details on these innovative approaches, you can check out discussions on open source funding for open source.

Summary of the Apache Spark Business Model

Apache Spark isn’t just a framework for distributed data processing; it’s a rich ecosystem that includes libraries for machine learning, stream processing, and graph processing. A key aspect of Spark’s ecosystem is its reliance on community contributions. Developers from around the world collaborate on its GitHub repository, ensuring that Spark remains at the cutting edge of technology. The governance process, characterized by transparency and meritocracy, builds trust among contributors and sponsors alike. An essential component of Apache Spark’s model is its use of the Apache 2.0 license. This permissive license not only shields contributors with patent protection but also allows enterprises to integrate Spark into proprietary systems without legal hurdles. The license enables a free flow of innovation—companies can both use and contribute to Spark’s codebase, leading to enhancements that benefit the entire community. The funding mechanisms sustaining Apache Spark are as diverse as they are innovative. Corporate sponsorships play a significant role, with companies dedicating resources and finances to support ongoing development. Additionally, grant programs and community donations help maintain an ecosystem where improvements and new features are continuously shared with users worldwide. These sustainable funding practices ensure that Apache Spark can meet the demands of real-time analytics and high-volume data processing.

The Role of Community and Governance

A thriving community is a key driver behind Apache Spark’s evolution. Regular code contributions, collaborative discussions, and user conferences have created a vibrant ecosystem around Spark. The Apache Software Foundation’s commitment to transparent decision-making helps the community align around a common vision—combining technical excellence with sustainable business practices. Moreover, Apache Spark’s open source funding model serves as an inspiring case study for other projects. By balancing corporate investments with community-led initiatives, Spark has created an environment in which innovation is continuously fueled by diverse funding sources. This balance is a testament to the power of open collaboration, where the success of one project elevates the entire open source movement. For similar insights, take a look at discussions on opensea funding models for open source.

In conclusion, Apache Spark’s journey from a research prototype to a cornerstone of big data analytics is a story of innovation, community collaboration, and smart funding strategies. The project’s adherence to the Apache 2.0 license and its commitment to transparent governance have built an ecosystem that continues to inspire both established enterprises and emerging startups. Apache Spark is not just a tool—it is a thriving community that demonstrates how combining corporate sponsorships with community contributions can lead to a sustainable and robust open source project. For anyone looking to understand how open source funding can drive technical advancement, the original article on Apache Spark’s business model and funding is a must-read. By adopting similar practices, new and existing projects can achieve the delicate balance between free access and financial sustainability, ensuring that the innovative spirit of open source thrives for years to come. Embrace the power of Apache Spark, and join a global movement that’s redefining the limits of big data analytics!

items.title: Cypress — Programmatically Control Test Data
items.author: David Ingraham
items.content:
Test data is a core element of any automated test. It forms the foundation for various scenarios, inputs, and conditions encountered during test execution. Effective test data management empowers testers to verify software functionality with confidence and consistency.

Fortunately, Cypress offers a straightforward and easy-to-implement pattern for fully controlling test data programmatically.

In this blog we’ll explore:

  • How to cleanup and create data using cy.request
  • How to create Custom API Cypress Commands
  • Why controlling data matters

To do this, let’s pretend we are testing a user table and the goal is to validate three basic test cases:

    Let’s begin by examining what an initial attempt at these three tests might look like, starting with creating a new user.

    We populate the user’s name in a form, submit it, and validate its existence in the table. So, what’s wrong with it? The issue occurs when the test is reran. If the request checks for duplicate users, the endpoint will fail with a response as soon as this test runs again. Additionally, even if our endpoint doesn’t check for duplication, the test will continue dumping identical users into the same table, rendering the creation validation invalid. Due to the test not controlling the state of it’s environment, until the test user is manually removed from the table, the test will fail. So, how can our test guarantee it always creates the same user repeatedly without issue?

    Let’s dive into the pattern.

    To begin, let’s create an api folder under the default folder. Within that, create a file. In the file, let's create a cleanup helper that checks if our test user exists and deletes it if it does. It should look something like this:

    Let’s breakdown what’s happening here.

    First we fetch all users from the environment using , then we check to see if a user exists within the API response that matches our test user. If the user exists, we delete the user. Cypress allows us to make these real API calls using the cy.request command, which is also much faster than deleting the user through the UI and removes any unnecessary front-end dependencies from the test. Finally, the cleanup helper is exported at the bottom of the file and can therefore be imported into the file like this:

    By doing this, a new custom command is created that can be executed within any test spec file by appending to the helper. In our case, this would be . Let's modify the initial test and add the cleanup command now.

    All we’ve done is add one line to the , but that line makes all the difference. The custom command ensures that we are always in a clean state whenever the test executes, allowing us to rerun this test repeatedly. Notice the cleanup also occurs before the test executes, not after. This is a best practice, and more information can be found here.

    We’ve looked at cleaning up data; now let’s examine how data can be programmatically created for the edit and delete users tests. Again, let’s break down what an initial edit user test might look like.

    The biggest issue with this test is that it is entirely dependent on a specific user existing in the environment when it’s executed. If the test environment doesn’t contain , this test will fail. It’s unnecessarily data-dependent. Furthermore, this test faces a similar issue in which it is unable to be rerun due to the underlying, update data manipulation.

    Fortunately, let’s create a method following a similar approach from before in our file.

    Similarly to before, allows us to create a real user that the test can edit through the application's API. This is a decent start, but what if another test uses this same hard-coded user, or better yet, how can we guarantee that our test data will always be unique? An easy way to accomplish this is by taking advantage of random, faker data and persisting our user response in a variable.

    By replacing the static name variables with random data, it guarantees our user will always be unique each run, avoiding any possibility of conflict with other tests. Also, by storing the real, user response in a variable the dynamic user data can now be accessed within the test file. Let’s wrap the method in a custom command again and refactor the edit user test.

    Since the custom command is called in the , when the it hook begins, the Cypress.env('currentUserBody') contains the real, dynamically created user. Then by destructuring the response object, our test is always editing a new, randomly created user each time. Finally, faker can also be used in the update data to keep the new user random too.

    This same pattern can also be used in the delete user test.

    The result looks similar. A user is created programmatically, the data is persisted in an environment variable which then drives what user will be deleted in the test. To reiterate, the data setup occurs in our setup hook, , guaranteeing the the environment will always be in the correct state before the test begins.

    Now that these three tests are refactored, the data in our test is no longer a variable determining if the test will succeed or not.

    To recap, the benefits of programmatically controlling test data are:

    • Guarantees consistent results every time
    • Ensure tests are able to run (and rerun) against any environment
    • Ensure tests are able to run in parallel without conflict
    • Setup state fast and remove unnecessary UI dependencies

    By leveraging Cypress’s capabilities, we can overcome the limitations of manual test data management and environmental, data-dependencies. Ensuring resilience in our test suites is critical and using this pattern enables reaching full confidence in our results. Thank you for reading.

items.title: Microservices Architecture: A One-Stop Interview Guide
items.author: DevCorner
items.content:
Microservices have become the go-to architecture for building scalable, maintainable, and distributed systems. This guide covers essential microservices concepts, including service discovery, inter-service communication, circuit breakers, distributed tracing, API gateways, and more.

1. Service Discovery in Microservices

Service discovery helps microservices locate and communicate with each other dynamically without hardcoded IPs. The most popular service discovery tools include:

  • Eureka Server acts as a service registry where microservices register themselves.
  • Eureka Clients query the registry to discover other services.
  • Supports self-healing and automatic failover.

Implementation in Spring Boot:

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-netflix-eureka-server
  • Distributed, highly available service discovery and configuration system.
  • Supports health checking and key-value storage.
  • Centralized service registry that follows a leader-follower model.
  • Used for distributed coordination and dynamic discovery.

2. Inter-Service Communication: Synchronous vs Asynchronous

Microservices communicate using or message brokers like Kafka, RabbitMQ (asynchronous).

Synchronous Communication

  • Simple but leads to tight coupling.
  • Example using :

Asynchronous Communication

  • Uses message brokers like Kafka, RabbitMQ.
  • Improves scalability and decoupling.
  • Example using Kafka Producer and Consumer:

3. Circuit Breakers in Spring Boot

Circuit breakers prevent cascading failures by detecting failures and stopping requests to failing services.

Implementation Using Resilience4j

io.github.resilience4jresilience4j-spring-boot2
  1. Use annotation:

4. SAGA Pattern for Distributed Transactions

SAGA ensures data consistency across multiple microservices using a series of local transactions.

  • (event-driven, no central coordinator).
  • (uses a central orchestrator to manage workflow).

Example using Orchestrator:

  1. Order Service creates an order and triggers an event.
  2. Payment Service processes the payment and publishes an event.
  3. If payment fails, Order Service rolls back.

5. Spring Cloud Config Server

Spring Cloud Config Server provides externalized configuration management.

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-config-server
  1. Store configuration in a Git repository.

6. OAuth2 & JWT for Authentication in Microservices

OAuth2 and JWT provide secure authentication in microservices.

  1. Client requests a token from Authorization Server.
  2. Token is validated by Resource Server.
  3. JWT (JSON Web Token) is used to pass claims securely.

Spring Security Implementation:

7. Distributed Tracing in Microservices

Distributed tracing helps track requests across microservices.

Using Spring Cloud Sleuth & Zipkin

org.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-sleuthorg.springframework.cloudspring-cloud-starter-zipkin

API Gateways handle authentication, routing, and load balancing.

  • Route requests to different services.
  • Reactive, lightweight alternative to Zuul.
  • Example Route Configuration:

Understanding microservices architecture concepts like service discovery, inter-service communication, circuit breakers, authentication, tracing, and API gateways is crucial for building scalable systems. Mastering these topics will help you ace microservices interviews and build robust distributed applications.

items.title: OAuth 2.0 and JWT Tokens: One-Stop Interview Guide
items.author: DevCorner
items.content:
Security is a critical aspect of modern web applications. OAuth 2.0 and JWT (JSON Web Tokens) are widely used authentication and authorization mechanisms. This guide provides a detailed, interview-ready explanation of these concepts.

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that allows third-party applications to securely access user resources without exposing credentials. It enables through access tokens.

  • : The user who owns the data.
  • : The application requesting access.
  • : Issues access tokens.
  • : The API or service that requires authentication.

OAuth 2.0 provides different flows (grant types) for various use cases:

1️⃣ Authorization Code Flow (Most Secure)

: Web applications with a backend server.

  1. User logs in and authorizes the client.
  2. Client receives an .
  3. Client exchanges the code for an .
  4. Client uses the token to access resources.

2️⃣ Client Credentials Flow

: Machine-to-machine (M2M) communication.

  1. Client authenticates itself with the authorization server.
  2. Client receives an .
  3. Client uses the token to access resources.

3️⃣ Implicit Flow (Deprecated)

: Legacy single-page applications (SPA).

  • Access token is directly returned in the URL.

⚠️ due to security vulnerabilities (e.g., token leakage).

4️⃣ Resource Owner Password Credentials Flow

: Trusted applications (not recommended for third parties).

  • User provides username and password directly to the client.
  • Client exchanges credentials for an access token.

⚠️ due to security risks (password exposure).

2. What is JWT (JSON Web Token)?

JWT is a compact, self-contained token used for authentication and information exchange. It is digitally signed to ensure integrity and authenticity.

JWT consists of three parts:

  1. : Contains metadata (e.g., signing algorithm)
  1. : Contains claims (user data, roles, expiration)
  1. : Ensures the token hasn’t been tampered with 
   HMACSHA256(
     base64UrlEncode(header) + "." + base64UrlEncode(payload),
     secretKey
   )
  1. : Short-lived tokens for accessing resources.
  2. : Long-lived tokens used to obtain new access tokens.
Can use JWT or opaque tokens

4. Securing OAuth 2.0 and JWT

🔒 Best Practices for OAuth 2.0

✅ Use for web applications. ✅ Keep confidential. ✅ Implement PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) to prevent attacks. ✅ Use securely and rotate them. ✅ Validate to prevent phishing attacks. ✅ Apply for least privilege access.

✅ Use for signing JWTs. ✅ Set for tokens (short for access tokens, longer for refresh tokens). ✅ Store JWTs (e.g., HttpOnly, Secure cookies). ✅ Validate issuer (iss) and audience (aud) claims. ✅ Do store sensitive information in JWT payload.

5. Implementing OAuth 2.0 with JWT in Spring Boot 

org.springframework.bootspring-boot-starter-oauth2-resource-serverio.jsonwebtokenjjwt0.11.2

Step 2: Configure OAuth2 Resource Server

Step 3: Generate and Validate JWT

OAuth 2.0 and JWT are essential security mechanisms for authentication and authorization. Mastering their concepts, flows, and implementations will help you ace technical interviews and build secure applications.

  • OAuth 2.0 is a framework for delegated access.
  • JWT is a self-contained token format for authentication.
  • Use with PKCE for web apps.
  • Store JWTs securely and validate claims.
  • Always follow security best practices.

🚀 Practice implementing OAuth 2.0 and JWT in a Spring Boot project to reinforce your understanding! Happy coding! 🎯

items.title: Security in Backend Development: One-Stop Solution for Interviews
items.author: DevCorner
items.content:
Backend security is crucial for protecting applications from attacks, data breaches, and unauthorized access. In this guide, we will cover key security concepts frequently asked in interviews.

OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework that enables secure API access without exposing user credentials. It involves four roles:

  • : The user granting access.
  • : The application requesting access.
  • : Issues access tokens.
  • : The API or service that requires authentication.
  • (Most Secure): Used for web applications.
  • (Deprecated): For single-page applications.
  • : Machine-to-machine authentication.
  • Resource Owner Password Flow: Direct credential exchange (not recommended).

JWT is a self-contained token format used for stateless authentication. It consists of three parts:

  • : Algorithm and token type (e.g., { "alg": "HS256", "typ": "JWT" }).
  • : Claims (e.g., user ID, roles, expiration time).
  • : Ensures integrity and authenticity.
  • : No need to store tokens in the backend.
  • : Signed using a secret key (HMAC) or public/private key pair (RSA/ECDSA).
  • : Reduces database lookups.

2. Preventing SQL Injection and XSS Attacks

SQL Injection occurs when attackers manipulate queries by injecting malicious SQL code.

  • Use Prepared Statements and Parameterized Queries
  • (e.g., Hibernate, JPA) that abstract direct SQL queries.
  • Validate and Sanitize User Input (e.g., restrict special characters).
  • Use Least Privilege Principle for database users.

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)

XSS occurs when attackers inject malicious scripts into web pages.

  • : Encode special characters (, , , , ).
  • Use Content Security Policy (CSP) to restrict allowed scripts.
  • using libraries like (Java) or (JavaScript).

3. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing)

CORS is a security mechanism that controls which domains can make requests to your API.

Handling CORS in Backend:

  • Enable CORS with Allowed Origins
  • Use Proxy for Internal APIs: Avoid exposing APIs directly.
  • Implement Token-Based Authentication instead of relying on cookies.

4. Implementing Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

RBAC restricts system access based on user roles.

  1. (e.g., Admin, User, Moderator).
  2. (e.g., Admin can delete users, User can only view data).
  3. Enforce Role-Based Access in Code

5. Securing APIs Exposed to the Internet

  • : Encrypt communication with SSL/TLS.
  • Implement API Rate Limiting to prevent abuse (e.g., using Spring Boot’s RateLimiter).
  • Authenticate Every Request (Use OAuth 2.0, JWT, API Keys).
  • Validate and Sanitize Input to prevent injection attacks.
  • to detect anomalies.

6. Data Encryption at Rest and in Transit

Protects stored data from unauthorized access.

  • Use Database-Level Encryption (e.g., AES-256 for MySQL, TDE for SQL Server).
  • (e.g., passwords, credit card details).
  • Store Hashes for Passwords (Use bcrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2).

Secures data during transmission.

  • Use HTTPS (TLS 1.2 or higher) to encrypt traffic.
  • Enable Secure WebSockets (wss://) for real-time communication.
  • Implement Mutual TLS (mTLS) for client-server authentication.

Security in backend development requires a multi-layered approach. By implementing OAuth 2.0, JWT, SQL injection prevention, XSS protection, CORS handling, RBAC, API security best practices, and encryption mechanisms, you can build robust and secure applications. Mastering these concepts will not only help you ace interviews but also create secure backend systems in real-world applications.

✅ Practice implementing these security techniques in your backend projects to solidify your understanding! 🚀

items.title: Cypress — Easy Multi-Environment Configuration
items.author: David Ingraham
items.content:
Whether running a subset of tests against a local environment, a regression suite against a hosted, UAT environment, or anything in between, testing against multiple environments is an important and common practice across the development life cycle.

In this article I’ll demonstrate an easy, clean and scaleable pattern for running against any environment with Cypress.

Before diving into the implementation, let’s explore some alternative solutions, break down why they are limited, and build upon their benefits until we define the most optimal solution.

Manually Update cypress.config.js.

The least optimal approach would be to manually update the each time a different environment is required.

Looking at a simple config file below, for a local environment, the user would need to change the and both env-specific variables only to have to change them back if they wish to run locally again. As this file grows and more settings and variables differ across environments, manually updating this file each time is not only taxing but impractical.

  • Quick for a one-time solution
  • Merging changes impacts everyone
  • Repetitive and time consuming

Another approach would be to take advantage of the CLI by overriding any individual default configurations or variables with parameters. This solution allows users to define multiple run commands, each one with specific overrides for that environment.

In the example below, two script commands are defined in the file. Since the file is configured for the local environment, the command doesn’t need to modify anything. However, the command overrides both the and the environmental variables using the and options.

This allows users to simply change which environment they are running against by switching up the command they run. This is not only faster than the previous approach but less manual. Doing this is optimal when there are small differences between environments so the run commands stay small. For environments with larger amounts of differences, passing in multiple parameters can be tedious and hard to maintain as the deviations grow.

Additionally, it’s not recommend to define secrets in a so this approach limits what environmental variables can be overridden.

  • Useful for small environmental differences
  • Support fast environment switching
  • Doesn’t scale well as environmental differences grow
  • Forced to override secrets in a public file

While both solutions above work for small environmental differences, what about environments that have tons of variations between them?

One way to handle this would be to remove all specific overriding from the commands and instead pass in a single environment, defining variable such as

Now conditional logic can be implemented in the test suite by performing Cypress.env('ENVIRONMENT') to access the variable. Let’s take a look at a basic test attempting to login and break it down.

Using switch/case conditional logic we are able to check the passed-in option and set the correct url and authentication data within the test itself.

While this approach cleans up the CLI and moves all the logic adjacent to the rest of the test code, there’s a ton of repetitious logic needed to accomplish this, and as more env-specific values grow, the repetition will only worsen. Even if these helpers were abstracted, each test would need to trigger this logic resulting in much more code quantity than the other alternatives. Finally, this faces the same issue as before in which secrets are publicly defined.

  • Moves environment specific info close to the test code
  • Duplicated conditional logic
  • Greater quantity of code needed to accomplish the same solution
  • Potential confusion having config data in both a config file and within the test suite.
  • Forced to define secrets in a public file

Now that several alternative solutions have been explored, let’s look at a solution that combines all of the pros from the alternative solutions in a way that supports adding a new environment incredibly easy.

  • The file is defined for both global settings across all environments as well individual settings for the most frequently used environment (such as local).
  • The library is used to import secrets from a local file. These secrets are fetched using the syntax.

  • Next, additional are created, one for each environment. These configuration files define only what is different for that environment, while the remaining settings are inherited by spreading from the base file.

  • So in the example below, the base file is imported, the QA and login variables are overridden and the overall configuration is redefined by spreading everything back together.

  • Finally, each environment has it’s own run command that passes in a single CLI option
  • Notice that the command doesn’t pass in a configuration file, meaning the default will be used.

Running against QA is as simple as executing or for a UAT env while the base command supports local testing. I recommend also creating env-specific commands for utilizing the test runner alongside the run commands for headless, CI/CD usage.

This pattern is not only clean but adding a new environment is as easy as creating a new command and env-specific config file. Additionally, debugging or modifying any settings is simple, as global-level changes are conveniently tweaked in the base config file while all other env-specific changes occur separately. Importing important secrets from files is also possible.

Hopefully, this helps and simplifies your environmental configuration moving forward. Thank you for reading.

items.title: Vercel Ai Sdk
items.author: Abdullah
items.content:
As of March 4, 2025, Vercel has introduced enhancements to its AI SDK, focusing on developer experience (DX) improvements and client-side tool execution.

The AI SDK now offers better observability, allowing developers to monitor token usage and errors when using and . The new callback provides insights into token usage upon stream completion.

Developers can now access the final, typed object as a promise from the result, ensuring type safety.

To optimize bundle size, the AI SDK UI has been split by framework. Developers are encouraged to migrate to , , , or .

Client-Side Tool Execution

With the 3.2 release, building generative UI chatbots client-side is streamlined using and in React projects. The SDK supports client and server-side tool execution with and introduces and utilities, allowing conditional UI rendering based on LLM tool calls.

Example of a chatbot determining user location:

In the call, omitting the parameter allows client-side tool execution.

:: :YesNo

The within defines functions for client-side tools. The provides access to tools selected by the LLM on the client, enabling conditional UI rendering to handle tool calls. The function allows passing user-provided information back to the LLM for future responses.

These updates enhance the flexibility and efficiency of building AI-powered applications with Vercel's AI SDK.

items.title: Run GitHub Actions Locally with Act: A Developer's Guide 🛠️
items.author: Tejas Nikhar
items.content:
As someone who has been writing GitHub Actions for a long time, I’ve come to appreciate the value of the Act tool. It’s been a game-changer for me, allowing me to:

1. Run actions locally and avoid the dreaded failed workflows showing up in the GitHub Actions tab 😅.2. Catch errors early, especially for workflows involving tasks like pushing code, Docker images, or deploying branches.3. Save time and resources by testing actions locally before pushing to GitHub.

The main dependency for Act is Docker, so make sure you have it installed on your system. Once Docker is ready, follow these steps:

Install Act using one of the following methods:

  • Using Homebrew (macOS/Linux):
  curl .2  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nektos/act/master/install.sh | bash

Once installed, run the following command to set up Act:

You’ll be prompted to choose a default image for running your workflows:

  • ~17GB download and ~75GB disk space required. Mirrors GitHub-hosted runners.
  • ~500MB. Includes essential tools for most workflows.
  • <200MB. Contains only Node.js. Not compatible with all workflows.

Choose the image that best fits your requirements. For most use cases, the medium image is a good balance between size and functionality.

Here are some common use cases for Act:

  1. To see all available workflows in your repository, use:

: The following output showcases the use of the act -l command, which lists all available workflows in your repository. This provides a clear view of the workflows, jobs, and the events that trigger them

    act 
    INFO[0000] Using docker host , and daemon socket 
    Stage  Job ID          Job name               Workflow name                   Workflow file       Events
    0      build-and-push  Build and Push Images  Build and Publish Docker Image  docker-image.yml    push
    0      lint            Linting Go Code        Lint                            lint.yml            pull_request
    0      security_audit  security_audit         Security Audit                  security-audit.yml  pull_request
    0      unit_tests      unit_tests             Unit Tests                      unit-test.yml       workflow_dispatch,pull_request
  1. Run a specific workflow by specifying the event that triggers it. For example:
  1. You can run a specific job within a workflow using:

: Below is a trimmed output for the unit_tests job. Note that a lot of logs have been omitted for brevity, as the job produces extensive output

    act  unit_tests

    Unit Tests/unit_tests] ⭐ Run Main Install Dependencies
    Unit Tests/unit_tests]   🐳  docker bash  /var/run/act/workflow/2] 
    | go: downloading github.com/fatih/color v1.18.0
    | go: downloading golang.org/x/sys v0.25.0
    | go: downloading github.com/mattn/go-colorable v0.1.13
    | go: downloading github.com/mattn/go-isatty v0.0.20
    Unit Tests/unit_tests]   ✅  Success - Main Install Dependencies

    Unit Tests/unit_tests] ⭐ Run Main Run Unit Tests
    Unit Tests/unit_tests]   🐳  docker bash  /var/run/act/workflow/3] 
    | ?     github.com/tejastn10/argus      no files]
    |  RUN   TestLoggingToFile
    |  PASS: TestLoggingToFile 0.00s
    | PASS
    | ok    github.com/tejastn10/argus/logs 0.001s
    |  RUN   TestMonitorURLTable
    |  RUN   TestMonitorURLTable/Valid_URL
    |  RUN   TestMonitorURLTable/Invalid_URL
    |  PASS: TestMonitorURLTable 0.83s
    |      PASS: TestMonitorURLTable/Valid_URL 0.83s
    |      PASS: TestMonitorURLTable/Invalid_URL 0.00s
    | PASS
    | ok    github.com/tejastn10/argus/monitor      0.827s
    Unit Tests/unit_tests]   ✅  Success - Main Run Unit Tests
  1. To use a specific workflow file, run: 
   act  .github/workflows/<workflow_file>.yml
  1. Act supports loading environment variables from a file. Create a file with your variables and run:

Same is true for secrets file too. Create a file with your secrets and run:

   act .secrets

Key Features and Limitations

  • Run workflows without pushing changes to GitHub.
  • Environment Variable Support: Load files directly for configuration.
  • Quickly debug issues in your workflows.
  • Run specific workflows, jobs, or tasks.
  • Some features of GitHub Actions are not fully supported (e.g., inputs).
  • Requires Docker, so make sure your system meets the requirements.

This tool has been a lifesaver for me when developing workflows. If you’re tired of failed actions cluttering your GitHub Actions tab or want to ensure your workflows run smoothly before pushing to GitHub, Act is definitely worth a try. Happy coding!

items.title: Why Aggregation in MongoDB
items.author: Shaktijeet Sahoo
items.content:
I have seen a lot of developers start with when working with MongoDB and stick to that. It's not inherently bad, as it's simple nature gives a simple and fluid head start, but sticking to that as you move is what I am not a fan of.

Before jumping in, let's get things out of the way. is faster than in general. If you are running a simple query, on a collection with limited documents, go with . For example (with , a JavaScript library that works as a ODM driver for MongoDB in node)):

If you run the above app, you'll have a noticeable time difference (depends on the collection size), where aggregation takes more time than find.

But that is not the reason I am writing this article, obviously. The problem with find comes in when we want to perform post-processing on the data. For example, after finding results you want to group them based on some parameter. Let's first weave an example:

📝 I am not including the results as they can be guessed.

So, I gave an example that makes aggregation looks complicated. Why go to such length when you can just it and then go about your day? Well, as I said, in the intro for simple use cases, like this one, with 10 documents in your collection, always it.

Let's complicate the example a bit, where you have a author collection and you want to bring in data from there as well. Now, if you are used to , you can recall a nifty feature called .

: All good. Never better.

: Now you have a million documents.

What changes is that you make an API call, binge watch One Piece and then come back to a meet a loading screen.

Let me explain why: The function in makes a query for every book in your document to get it's author by id. So your one .find().populate("author") actually runs 1001 queries. Without the post-processing you are going to apply, i.e. operations. And God forbid you have more relational fields that you want to populate.

What shines here is .

Now, a single query runs. And it is faster than .find().populate("author"). And we don't have to post process the data. We are served on a silver plate and then fed with a silver spoon.

And this is just me touching the surface. Quoting Sir Isaac Newton, "What we know is a drop, what we don't know is an ocean." (from Dark, ofc)

But before you jump into -ing everything, let me let you know of the caveats:

  1. You have to be extremely careful with the limits of your MongoDB server. Aggregation, while handy, requires more resources to run than find.
  2. In case you are using corporate managed MongoDB instances, like Atlas, you have to be careful about pricing, as prices spike up when you run resource expensive queries.
  3. This extends point 1: If you have a locally setup server, you must keep in mind the memory allocated to the process/server, or your deployment will crash processing while large queries.

With that out of the way, get to replacing your complex s with .

I will cover more of aggregation in details in my following writeups.

📝 Thank you for reading till the end. I am open to criticisms, constructive or otherwise, as this is my first blog. Please help me to get better.

items.title: Começando no 3D - infos iniciais.
items.author: Vincenzo Schettino
items.content:
SUMÁRIO: Crtl + F e procure por [1,2,3,4,4.14.2,4.3,5,6,6.1,6.2,7,8]

1 2 4 4.2 5 6.1 7

8- Url do STL de teste do pokemon Blastoise

Olá devs, meu nome é Gnomo e eu guiarei vocês nessa empreitada. Você que chegou até aqui, não vai achar nesse post coisas muito complexas, e sim um pontapé inicial para desenvolver 3d e entender um pouco mais. Começarei citando Luma ai. https://lumalabs.ai/dream-machine Entenda mais sobre ela de maneira individual

Crie STL's com ela a partir de imagens 2d .

2-

Pode parecer uma quest complexa de se chegar ao objetivo , mas com os recursos certos e as ferramentas exploradas aqui você pode conseguir :D .

Basicamente entenda os fundamentos de modelagem 3D.

O que é um arquivo STL? -> Um arquivo STL nada mais é que um formato de arquivo usado para impressão 3D.

STL(Standard Tessellation Language) é amplamente utilizado em impressão 3D e prototipagem rápida. Ele representa a geometria de superfícies de objetos 3D por meio de uma malha de triângulos, sem incluir informações sobre cor, textura ou outros atributos. O formato STL é simples e eficiente, sendo compatível com a maioria dos softwares de modelagem 3D e impressoras 3D.

Tipos de modelagem 3D: -> Existem vários tipo de modelagem 3D, cada um de sua maneira, com suas características, técnicas e aplicabilidade específicas. Os principais tipos são:

! Modelagem Poligonal (Polygon Modeling)

Ex: Criar personagens, cenários ou objetos para jogos.

Aplicações: Jogos, filmes, animações e impressão 3D.

!! Modelagem por NURBS (Non-Uniform Rational B-Splines) Ex: Modelar carros, aeronaves ou produtos industriais.

Aplicações: Design industrial, engenharia, automotivo e protótipos de alta precisão.

!!! Modelagem por Subdivisão de Superfícies (Subdivision Surfaces) Ex: Criar personagens realistas para filmes ou jogos.

Aplicações: Animação, personagens e objetos orgânicos.

!!!! Modelagem Sólida (Solid Modeling) Ex: Projetar peças mecânicas ou estruturas arquitetônicas.

Aplicações: Engenharia, arquitetura, design de produtos e manufatura.

!!!!! Modelagem por Escultura Digital (Digital Sculpting) Ex: Criar personagens hiper-realistas para filmes ou jogos.

Aplicações: Personagens realistas, criaturas, objetos orgânicos e arte digital.

!!!!!! Modelagem Procedural Ex: Gerar paisagens naturais, como montanhas ou florestas.

Aplicações: Efeitos visuais, geração de terrenos, cenários e simulações.

!!!!!!! Modelagem Baseada em Voxels Ex: Criar mundos voxelizados, como em jogos como Minecraft.

Aplicações: Jogos, simulações médicas (como tomografias) e arte digital.

!!!!!!!! Modelagem Paramétrica Ex: Projetar peças mecânicas com dimensões ajustáveis.

Aplicações: Engenharia, arquitetura e design de produtos.

!!!!!!!!! Modelagem por Escaneamento 3D Exemplo: Digitalizar esculturas ou peças antigas para preservação.

Aplicações: Restauração de arte, medicina, engenharia reversa e patrimônio cultural.

Listei as principais, porém a modelagem 3D é ampla e bem vasta, o que acarreta em ser uma área em constante evolução, podendo incluir variações ou técnicas mais especializadas

Agora, para continuar você precisa escolher um software de modelagem 3D: Blender (Grátis e poderoso):

Ideal para modelagem poligonal, escultura e animação.

Tinkercad (Grátis e online):

Ótimo para iniciantes, com interface simples e intuitiva.

Fusion 360 (Grátis para uso pessoal):

Focado em design técnico e engenharia, ideal para peças mecânicas.

Excelente para escultura digital e designs orgânicos.

pixologic.com 3-

Agora o objetivo é aprender os fundamentos.

Como assim? Do que se trata? Como se estrutura?

Procure no Youtube, vídeos iniciantes desses Softwares, se necessário utilize IA para te ajudar a ver os mais relevantes. LLMs como DeepSeek, Chatgpt, Copilot, Gemini, Sabiá entre outras.

LLM's = Large Language Models (Modelo de Linguagem Grande) ou Modelo de Linguagem Ampla que são -> chatbots # que utilizam linguagem natural em formato de perguntas e respostas.

Comece criando objetos geométricos de maneira geral, como prismas, cubos e esferas. Depois passe para objetos do cotidiano, como xícaras, cadeiras, mesas, potes...

4- Hora da preparação do modelo para impressão 3D.

4.1 Verifique a malha = manifold. Certifique-se de que o modelo é "manifold" (sem buracos ou superfícies invertidas).

4.2 Esse preparo é feito verificando a escala, comumente as : Escala 1:1;

Escala Reduzida (Ex.: 1:2, 1:10);

Escala Aumentada (Ex.: 2:1, 10:1);

4.3 Escolha o formato STL no software de modelagem 3D.

No software de modelagem 3D (Blender, Fusion 360, Tinkercad, etc.):

Verifique se o modelo está completo e sem erros (malha "manifold"). Vá em File > Exportar (ou opção equivalente). Defina as unidades corretas (milímetros é o padrão para impressão 3D). Use um Slicer para Preparar o Arquivo STL

Um slicer converte o arquivo STL em instruções para a impressora 3D (G-code).

G-code (ou "código G") é uma linguagem de programação usada para controlar máquinas de fabricação automatizadas, como impressoras 3D, fresadoras CNC, tornos e outras máquinas de usinagem. Ele consiste em uma série de comandos que instruem a máquina sobre como se mover, com que velocidade operar, qual temperatura definir e outras ações necessárias para criar um objeto físico a partir de um modelo digital.

Um slicer faz a conversão do arquivo STL em instruções para a impressora 3D Softwares populares:

Cura (Grátis): ultimaker.com/software/ultimaker-cura

PrusaSlicer (Grátis): prusa3d.com/prusaslicer

6- Agora é sua vez de praticar:

Participe de comunidades online como reddit nos links :

6.1 Fóruns e Comunidades Internacionais Reddit

r/3Dprinting: Um dos maiores fóruns sobre impressão 3D, com discussões sobre impressoras, materiais, problemas e projetos.

r/FixMyPrint: Focado em ajudar usuários a resolver problemas de impressão 3D.

r/AdditiveManufacturing: Discussões sobre manufatura aditiva e tecnologias avançadas.

3D Printing Forum (3DPrintBoard)

Um dos fóruns mais antigos e ativos, com seções para iniciantes, hardware, software e projetos.

Além de ser um repositório de modelos 3D, o Thingiverse tem uma comunidade ativa onde usuários compartilham projetos e discutem impressão 3D.

Focado em impressoras Prusa, mas aberto a discussões gerais sobre impressão 3D.

Comunidade dedicada a impressoras RepRap (projetos de hardware aberto).

3D Printing: Um dos maiores grupos sobre impressão 3D no Facebook.

3D Printing Enthusiasts: Outro grupo popular com discussões variadas.

6.2 Comunidades e Fóruns em Português Clube da Impressão 3D (Facebook)

Um dos maiores grupos brasileiros sobre impressão 3D, com discussões em português.

Impressão 3D Brasil (Facebook)

Outro grupo ativo com foco em impressão 3D no Brasil.

Comunidade brasileira com discussões sobre impressoras, materiais e projetos.

3D Printing Forum (Clube do Hardware)

Seção dedicada a impressão 3D no fórum Clube do Hardware.

7- Por fim participe de: Comunidades para Compartilhar Projetos

Além de baixar modelos, você pode compartilhar seus projetos e interagir com outros usuários.

Outra plataforma popular para compartilhar e baixar modelos 3D.

Plataforma para compartilhar e vender modelos 3D.

Comunidade da Prusa para compartilhar projetos e participar de concursos.

8- Chegou a hora do Boss" Blastoise aparece vorazmente " Imprima seus projetos. Se você não tiver uma impressora 3D, use serviços online como Shapeways ou 3D Hubs.

items.title: How Laravel Handles Asynchronous Queue Jobs
items.author: Kennedy Gitahi
items.content:
As I dig deeper into Laravel, one thing I have always wanted to understand better is how it handles asynchronous queue jobs. As we know, PHP is a single-threaded language. This means that when a request arrives at a server, PHP processes it lineally from start to finish before moving to the next one.

This synchronous execution model works well for many scenarios, but it does not always serve the user well. For example, it can fall short when a server needs to perform resource-intensive tasks without making the user wait.

Laravel has a neat solution: its queue system, an elegant solution that brings asynchronous processing capabilities to PHP applications.

But how does Laravel accomplish seemingly asynchrnous operations in a single-threaded environment?

Understanding PHP's Single-Threaded Nature

Before diving into Laravel's queue implementation, it's important to understand what "single-threaded" really means in a PHP context.

When your application receives a request, PHP allocates a single execution thread to process it as mentioend above. This thread can only execute one instruction at a time in a set sequence until the entire script completes.

Unlike languages with native multi-threading capabilities, PHP cannot "spawn" additional threads within the same process to handle multiple tasks simultaneously.

This limitation becomes particularly problematic and obvious when your application needs to:

  • Send emails (especially many emails at the same time)
  • Process large files (like processing a podcast episode)
  • Generate reports (e.g. invoices or excel sheets)
  • Call external APIs with unpredictable response times
  • Perform resource-intensive calculations

Forcing users to wait while these operations complete would create a poor user experience.

This is precisely where Laravel's queue system shines.

How Laravel Achieves Asynchronous Processing

Queue Drivers: The Foundation

The foundation of Laravel's queue system is its queue drivers. These are storage mechanisms that hold your jobs until they're processed.

Laravel supports several of these drivers out of the box:

  • : Uses your application's database to store jobs (simple to set up but less performant at scale due to polling overhead)
  • : High-performance, in-memory data store (excellent for high-volume queues and supports Redis Streams in Laravel 10+)
  • : Cloud-based queue service (great for distributed systems)
  • : Simple, fast work queue

Each driver has different performance characteristics and setup requirements.

However, Redis provides an excellent balance of performance and simplicity for most applications. This is the main reason why it is the recommended option by many experienced developers.

The Multi-Process Approach

What Laravel does brilliantly is circumvent PHP's single-threaded limitation by leveraging multiple processes instead of threads.

The fundamental concept works like this:

  1. Your application dispatches jobs to a queue (stored in a database, Redis, etc.)
  2. Separate PHP processes (queue workers) pick up and process these jobs
  3. Multiple workers can run simultaneously, creating true parallelism

This approach allows your main application to continue processing requests while background jobs run independently in their own PHP processes.

Queue Workers: The Workhorses

At the heart of Laravel's queue system are queue workers. These are long-running PHP processes that continuously monitor your queues for new jobs.

Starting a queue worker is as simple as running:

Once you run this command, it initiates a PHP process behind the scenes that:

  1. Connects to your configured queue driver (Redis, database, etc.).

  2. Polls for available jobs.

  3. Retrieves and processes them one by one.

  4. Continues this loop until it is manually stopped or hits a configured timeout.

Creating and Dispatching Queue Jobs

Laravel makes it remarkably easy to create and dispatch jobs.

Here's a simple example adapted from the example Laravel gives in its docs:

Dispatching this job to the queue is as simple as:

This command will dispatch the job to the queue and set it to retry 5 times with a 10-second delay between retries. Retries are handled automatically by Laravel's queue system and ensure the job is retried up to the configured number of times ($tries) in case of failure.

According to Laravel's documentation:

When a job throws an exception but the exception threshold has not yet been reached, the job will typically be retried immediately. However, you may specify the number of minutes such a job should be delayed by calling the method.

The method prevents the job from being retried too often so it does not overburden your server. The default backoff is 10 seconds, the lowest it can be set to is 1 second, with the highest being 3600 seconds (1 hour).

With the single dispatch command above, Laravel serializes the job and its dependencies, stores it in your configured queue, and allows your application to continue processing the current request immediately.

You can also send a job to a specific queue by using the method:

In this instance, the job will be sent to the queue, which you can configure in your queue configuration file. Remember that you can push the same job to multiple queues, and the worker will process them in the order you specified:

Doing this can be especially benefitial for instances where you want to prioritize or segment how jobs are processed. This is possible because the Laravel queue worker allows you to specify which queues it should process by priority.

For example, pushing jobs to a high queue can allow you to run a worker that gives them higher processing priority:

php artisan queue:work high

Scaling Beyond Single Workers

Horizontal Scaling with Multiple Workers

One of the most powerful aspects of Laravel's queue system is how easily it scales horizontally, which it does by allowing you to add more workers. This type of scaling allows running multiple queue workers simultaneously, with each processing different jobs independently:

php artisan queue:work high,low

Laravel's queue system is designed to handle high volumes of jobs by allowing you to scale horizontally by adding more workers to a queue so you can process jobs concurrently.

Laravel recommends that you start multiple processes if you would like to do this. To have multiple processes, you can either run each process in a separate terminal tab locally or configure your process manager to run multiple processes in production.

If you are worried about resources or too many workers, you can also use the option to limit the number that can run at the same time:

php artisan queue:work 10

Process Management with Supervisor

Production environments are different from development environments, and they require a different approach to managing queue workers. Since you do not always have access to a terminal or can keep an eye on your processes indefinitely in production, you need a way to keep your processes running.

Remember that a process can stop running for any variety of reasons, some of the most common ones being exceeding a worker timeout or executing the queue:restart command.

In these environments, Laravel recommends using Supervisor. This is a process control system that ensures your queue workers stay running. A typical Supervisor configuration for Laravel looks like this:

Failed Jobs and Handling Retries

Jobs fail in the real world for a variety of reasons. If one of your queued jobs is encountering an error, you likely do not want it to keep retrying indefinitely because this will either exhaust your resources or lead to massive server bills.

Laravel provides a failed job system that allows its queue system to handle this elegantly and in a structured wayusing built-in retry logic.

When a job fails, Laravel will automatically store the job and the exception that was thrown in the table. You can then use the event to handle the failed job.

Laravel allows you to specify how many times or for how long a job may be attempted in several neat ways.

The first is in the terminal. You can add the switch to the following Artisan command:

php artisan queue:work 3

This tells Laravel how many times to retry that job after the first failure. If a job exceeds its maximum number of attempts, it will be considered a "failed" job (see below for more details).

Supplying to the command will instruct your worker to retry the job indefinitely, which you might or might not want depending on your use case.

Another option is setting the number of max retries in the job class:

Laravel 10+ also allows a method inside the event class:

Like in the command we saw earlier, the method allows you to specify the number of seconds Laravel should wait before retrying a job that has encountered an exception.

For more complex retry logic, you can return an array of intervals:

In this example, the retry delay is 3 seconds after the first try, 30 seconds after the second try, and 5 minutes (300 seconds) after the third try and each subsequent failed try.

Finally, you can also set the retry until time:

With this option, the job will be retried until the specified time. It is an excellent alternative to defining how many times your application retries a job before it fails, and it allows you to define a time at which the job should no longer be attempted.

By using the method, the failing job will be attempted as many times as possible within a given time frame. In this example, the job will be retried until 12 hours have passed after the first failure.

Laravel 10+ offers a job batching feature that allows you to group multiple jobs into a single batch and process them together. This can be especially useful for processing large numbers of jobs in a single batch.

The batch's ID (a unique identifier we get through ), may be used to query for information about the batch once it has been dispatched.

You can then perform some action or run a callback when all the jobs in the batch have completed executing.

Laravel 10+ also offers a job chaining feature that allows you to run a list of jobs in sequence.

Using the example provided by Laravel:

Since this is a chain of jobs, subsequent jobs will not be run if one job in the sequence fails. For example will not run if fails.

Advanced Queue Management with Laravel Horizon

For completeness, we should mention Laravel Horizon, which is a beautiful dashboard and configuration system for your queue workers. It is an excellent option for developers running Redis queues.


composer require laravel/horizon


php artisan horizon:install


php artisan horizon
  • Real-time queue monitoring
  • Job failure tracking and retry management
  • Worker balancing across multiple queues
  • Process scaling based on queue load
  • Tags for organizing jobs.
  • Metrics about queue performance.

Performance Considerations and Best Practices

Error handling is a critical aspect of queue management because jobs are executed in the background, and unhandled exceptions can lead to silent failures. For this reason, you must implement robust error handling within jobs using try-catch blocks and logging.

Implementing blocks within the method allows you to gracefully manage potential errors.

To follow the best practices, you must:

  • Log detailed error messages, including the exception trace, to make debugging easier for you and other developers who come after you.
  • Use the job's method to perform specific actions upon failure, such as sending notifications or rolling back changes.
  • Avoid generic catch blocks and always catch specific exceptions whenever possible.

Another great practice is keeping jobs focused and relatively small. The size of your jobs is important because large, monolithic jobs can tie up queue workers and server resources for extended periods. This can delay other jobs and potentially leading to timeouts.

To ensure the best performance, you should:

  • Break down complex tasks into smaller, more manageable jobs. You can then use batching or chaining to run the jobs as you wish
  • Ensure each job performs a single, well-defined task.
  • Avoid performing resource-intensive operations directly within the method; delegate them to separate services or classes and batch or chain them.

This is a general OOP practice, but always remember to use dependency injection in job constructors. This is a best practice because dependency injection promotes code reusability, testability, and maintainability.

The best practices here include:

  • Inject services, repositories, and other dependencies into the job's constructor.
  • Avoid instantiating dependencies directly within the method.

Jobs that process large datasets or perform memory-intensive operations can exhaust worker memory, which can lead to weird side effects including crashes. To prevent this, you should configure appropriate memory limits for workers.

  • Adjusting the PHP setting in your file or within your Laravel configuration to accommodate the memory requirements of your jobs.
  • Ensuring your octane workers have enough memory when using octane.
  • Monitoring worker memory usage and adjusting the limit as needed.

You should use queue names (e.g., high, default, low) to prioritize jobs. Doing this will help ensure critical tasks are processed before less important ones.

To follow this best practice:

  • Create multiple queues with different priorities.
  • Dispatch critical jobs to high-priority queues.
  • Use the option with or Octane to specify which queues to process and in which order to do so.
  • Example dispatching to a high priority queue.

You should also ensure proper connection pooling for database queues. Database queues can create a large number of database connections that can potentially exhaust available connections.

To follow this best practice:

  • Configure database connection pooling to reuse existing connections.
  • Use a database driver that supports connection pooling.
  • Optimize database queries within jobs to minimize connection usage.
  • Ensure your database server is configured to handle the expected number of connections.

Wow! That was much more information than I intended, but I think it was worth it.

Understanding Laravel's queue system is crucial for building robust and scalable applications. This is because the system allows developers and their code to elegantly overcome PHP's single-threaded limitation by using multiple processes rather than threads.

This approach allows Laravel applications to process resource-intensive tasks asynchronously without blocking the main request cycle. The effects is significantly improved application responsiveness and user experience.

By leveraging queues efficiently, which includes choosing the right driver, using Supervisor for stability as Laravel suggests, and adopting Horizon for monitoring, you can build applications that can handle high workloads gracefully regardlessof what those workloads are.

Remember that whether you're sending emails, processing uploads, or generating reports, Laravel queues are a powerful tool that provide the flexibility and power you need to get the job done efficiently.

Feel free to leave comments, corrections, or questions blow. A share would also be highly appreciated!

items.title: Performance Optimization in High-Throughput Applications
items.author: DevCorner
items.content:
1. Reducing Latency in High-Throughput Applications

Latency is a critical factor in high-performance systems. Here’s how to minimize it:

A. Optimize Database Queries

  • Use : Proper indexing (B-Trees, Hash Indexes, Composite Indexes) speeds up lookups.
  • Use : Avoid , optimize joins, and use database EXPLAIN plans.
  • Implement : Use HikariCP for optimized database connections.
  • : Reduce load by distributing read queries to replicas.

B. Use Caching Strategically

  • Application-Level Caching: Use Redis or Memcached for frequently accessed data.
  • : Store query results in a cache.
  • : Use ETags, Cache-Control headers.
  • CDN (Content Delivery Network): Reduce latency by serving static assets closer to users.

C. Optimize Network Performance

  • Use over REST for faster communication.
  • Enable for multiplexed requests.
  • : Use Protobuf/Avro instead of JSON.
  • : Reduce handshake overhead.

D. Asynchronous & Parallel Processing

  • Use Message Queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ) for background tasks.
  • Implement to handle multiple requests in one go.
  • Use Async APIs & Event-Driven Architecture to minimize blocking.

E. Load Balancing & Efficient Resource Utilization

  • Load Balancers (Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ALB) distribute traffic evenly.
  • Circuit Breakers (Resilience4J, Hystrix) prevent cascading failures.
  • : Prevent server overload with API throttling.

2. Java Garbage Collection (GC) Tuning for Performance

Garbage Collection (GC) can be a bottleneck in high-performance applications. Here’s how to tune it:

A. Choosing the Right GC Algorithm

  • : Best for single-threaded applications (Not recommended for high throughput).
  • : Good for multi-threaded applications but may cause latency spikes.
  • : Balanced between throughput and low-pause time.
  • : Low-latency GC suitable for large heap sizes.
  • : Use and to set initial and max heap size.
  • : Enable -XX:+PrintGCDetails -XX:+PrintGCTimeStamps for analysis.
  • : Set for lower pauses.
  • : Optimize to balance Young Gen and Old Gen.

C. Avoiding Frequent Full GCs

  • : Reuse objects instead of frequent allocations.
  • Reduce Large Object Creation: Use for handling large data chunks.
  • : Enable to allocate objects on the stack instead of heap.

3. Profiling & Debugging Performance Bottlenecks

Identifying bottlenecks is crucial for optimization.

A. CPU & Memory Profiling Tools

  • : Analyze CPU and memory usage.
  • : Monitor live JVM performance.
  • : Flame graphs for CPU and allocation profiling.

B. Thread Analysis & Deadlock Detection

  • Use to analyze thread dumps.
  • : Identify locks causing slow execution.
  • Use Concurrent Data Structures: Prefer over maps.
  • : Use Jaeger, OpenTelemetry for end-to-end request tracking.
  • : Prometheus + Grafana for real-time metrics.
  • : Centralized logging (ELK Stack) for analyzing slow requests.

D. Load Testing & Benchmarking

  • : Simulate high traffic loads.
  • : Benchmark API performance.
  • Test Different GC Algorithms: Compare pause times and throughput.

Performance optimization is an iterative process. By profiling bottlenecks, optimizing database queries, tuning the GC, and using caching strategies, you can significantly improve the performance of your high-throughput applications.
